book.be:conditionvar: Choose Office Version:
book.title: Excel and SQL Educator

abstract.para: This Excel and SQL Educator course is a complete Excel and SQL training course using Excel Online. Excel lessons start at the basics, including charting, and cover advanced topics such as modeling business processes and PivotTables. Each lesson includes instructional readings, lecture videos, practice problems, and an assignment to assess student learning.
The practice problems and assignments are paired with an intelligent, robust, and detailed grading engine that automates the grading process and provides students with immediate and clear feedback.
Click here to email us for more information about the book, assignments, and course.

section.title: Organization of Information
section.para: Information in this course is organized into lessons. Each lesson covers a set of related topics and will include

listitem.para: Embedded or linked readings and videos that are clear and easy to understand
Instructional graphics or charts that reinforce learning in a visual format
Glossary terms that are called out in blue and bolded the first time they appear

section.para: Hovering over a glossary term makes its definition appear. You can also access the overall glossary from the right-hand menu in the course to search terms and view their definitions.

section.title: Customizing the Materials
section.para: This textbook allows you to display text and images based on the version of Microsoft Office you are using. Use the following steps to adjust the MyEducator settings to match your version of Microsoft Office.

listitem.para: Click Settings in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Screen print image of menu bar with red arrow pointing to the link with label: Advanced Options.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you are using.

imagedata.alt: Screen capture image of a pop-up window displaying various options. Red arrow pointing to radio buttons next to three options. Top option: Excel for Windows. Next option: Excel for Macintosh. Third option: Excel Online.

section.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

section.title: Practice and Assess Learning
section.para:
As you work through the content, you will have many opportunities to practice and reinforce what you are learning. The practice will take one of these forms:
Practice problems embedded throughout each lesson give you the chance to practice the concept that you are learning. Learning checks provide hints and targeted, immediate feedback to guide you if you struggle.
Test your skills assessments give you the opportunity to do a quick self-check to assess your understanding of the material before moving on to the next lesson.

section.title: Grading Assignments
section.para: Each of the assignments in this course is a hands-on experience. Tasks are scored automatically. You will want to carefully read the instructions for the tasks and review the automated feedback you receive when you have completed an assignment. Grades for each assignment are automatically uploaded and stored in a course gradebook.

section.title: Excel Assignments
section.para: You can use Excel 365 or Excel 2021 or Excel 2024 for Windows or Mac or Excel Online to complete the activities in this course. Please note that the cloud-based online version of Excel requires an internet connection and a Microsoft account to operate properly with the course materials; files downloaded and saved will be stored on One Drive.

The version of Microsoft Excel you need for this course is part of the Microsoft Office suite and is often available at little or no cost to current students. Your instructor may have information on how to download a free or reduced-price copy of Excel.

section.title: Check Your Version of Excel
section.para: If you plan to use a computer that already has Excel installed, you will need to check your version before starting.
Note that the version might be for business, enterprise, apps for business, professional, or home and student. Any of these Microsoft license versions is acceptable.
To check your version:

listitem.para: Click on the File menu and choose Account.
Choose File
Choose About Microsoft Excel

If you have Excel 2019 for Mac, the popup window will display 2019, as shown in

Popup window that displays the 2019 Mac version information

itemizedlist.para: Check the build number. If it is less than 11425.20204, click the Office Updates button.
A build number greater than or equal to 11425.20204 is necessary for this course. If your updated build is lower, you may need to download and install Excel 365.

listitem.para: If you have Office 365, the Account page will display 365, as shown in

imagedata.alt: File menus Account page with the Excel 365 version information displayed. Arrows call out the Account menu item, the Excel version 365 and the update apps message.
Popup window that displays the 365 Mac version information.

section.title: Accessing the Online Version of Excel
section.para: The online version of Excel uses a browser window to display the Excel Workbook. If you plan to use the online version, you will need a Microsoft account. You can create your Microsoft account for free if you don’t currently have an account. To access Excel Online, use this link: Office Live Excel.

imagedata.alt: Microsoft Account Login screen with textbox to enter username or a link to sign up for an account.

section.para: Microsoft will prompt you to sign in or create an account if you are not currently logged into your Microsoft Account.

section.title: Downloading Office 365
section.para: Many schools and universities make Office 365 available to students for free. Check with your course instructor for software that is available.
To purchase a subscription to Office 365, click this link: Microsoft Office 365

In most instances, the course images, videos, and information for Excel 2021 or Excel 2024 will match Excel 365. In instances when there are major differences between the two versions, use help resources to help you complete the task.

listitem.para: Understand and be confident using basic functions in Excel.
Know how to apply sorting and filtering techniques to real datasets.

section.title: Introduction
section.para: Excel is one of the most important business applications of all time. It allows managers to perform sophisticated and powerful analyses, including quickly connecting with corporate databases with hundreds of thousands of records. At the same time, it is intuitive and straightforward to use. It would be hard to find any business, small or large, whose managers do not rely on Excel in their daily work.

In this book, we start with the basics and then quickly demonstrate how to use some of the most advanced features of Excel. At the outset, we should get two important things out of the way. First, as a beginner, there is no substitute for experience. Each lesson in this book contains a rich set of practice problems and assessments that require you to apply what you are learning in the live Excel application. Don’t skip over the problems. The only way to learn a skill in Excel is to do it in Excel.

The second important point is that you should also find ways to experiment with Excel. When you have a decision to make that requires you to analyze something numerically, put the numbers in Excel. When you are done with a homework assignment or set of practice problems, play with the numbers in the assignment. Eventually, you will feel comfortable with Excel as a tool to help you solve many types of business and personal problems. Let us begin by discussing the Excel working environment.

section.title: Working in Excel
section.para: When you open Excel, you are typically presented with a blank workbook. A workbook An Excel file that stores all the information, previous calculations, and analyses that you may have already completed. Must contain at least one worksheet. is an Excel file that stores all the information, previous calculations, and analyses that you may have already completed. A workbook must contain at least one worksheet. A worksheet A key part of an Excel workbook that contains data and calculations and is organized into a collection of cells arranged in the form of a table. contains the actual data and calculations. A worksheet is organized into a collection of cells arranged in the form of a table. Cells Rectangular boxes in a worksheet that store individual pieces of data or calculations. store individual pieces of data or calculations. shows a blank Excel workbook. You will notice a fairly sophisticated menu system at the top of the workbook—but don’t worry! We will discuss Excel’s menus in the next few sections. Near the bottom left of , you can see the text “Sheet1” on a tab. This designates that there is one worksheet in the workbook. You can add a worksheet by clicking on the plus icon next to the word “Sheet 1.” When you have more than one worksheet in your workbook, you can move to another worksheet in the workbook by clicking on that particular worksheet tab.

You will also notice that each of the worksheets is divided into a number of cells. You can select a cell by clicking on that cell. You will also notice that each cell has an address that can be described in terms of the row and column that corresponds to that particular cell; we refer to cells by their addresses. The top-left cell in a worksheet is cell A1. The cell in column F and row 10 is called cell F10.

imagedata.alt: A blank Excel workbook with emphasis on Cell A1, Cell F10, Excel Menu Scheme, and Worksheet Tabs.
A blank Excel workbook with emphasis on Cell A1, Cell F10, Excel Menu Scheme, and Worksheet Tabs.
A blank Excel workbook with emphasis on Cell A1, Cell F10, Excel Menu Scheme, and Worksheet Tabs.

section.para: Excel has literally dozens of menu items that you can access. The menu area is divided into major groups according to each group’s function. The major menu areas can be accessed by clicking on the various menu tabs. shows the various menu tabs and the specific menu items available under the Home tab. While the menu displayed in may not exactly match the menu you see when you open Excel on your computer, it should be pretty close. Each of the major menu areas is further organized into specific groups separated by vertical lines. As you can see, the Home menu area is divided into menu groups (such as Clipboard, Font, and Alignment). There are other groups in the Home area that are not shown in .

Hidden within each of the menu groups are many other menu items that are not shown. In most cases, Excel displays the most commonly used menu features for convenient access. You can access the menu items that are not shown by clicking on the expand icon in the bottom right area of each menu grouping:

imagedata.alt: Icon used to expand the ribbon options by displaying an underlaying dialog box.

section.para: Don’t let the number of features and menu items overwhelm you; while each item can perform a helpful task, there are many items that you may never use.

Hidden within each of the menu groups are many other menu items that are not shown. In most cases, Excel displays the most commonly used menu features for convenient access. You can access the menu items that are not shown by right-clicking to display the shortcut menu and selecting Format Cells.

imagedata.alt: Right click shortcut options available for format cells.

section.para: The features in each menu tab are grouped together because they perform similar functions. (From left to right in ) The File menu is used to manipulate workbooks. Items in the Insert area include features that allow you to place specialized elements into your workbooks, such as charts, pictures, and shapes. The Page Layout menu items are used to format the appearance of worksheets. The Home area includes items used to format the appearance of data in cells. Items in the Data area are used to aid in importing and working with large amounts of data. The Review menu items contain options for sharing the Excel workbook with colleagues. The View menu items are used to change how a worksheet is displayed on the screen. The Excel menu area is also customizable; other menu tabs, such as the Developer or Add-Ins tabs, can be added to the Excel menu as you begin to use more of the features included in these groups. section.title: Working with Excel Workbook Files Using the File Menu
section.para: The File menu tab contains the menu items necessary for working with workbooks. You will notice that the File menu is different from the other tabs in Excel. Clicking on the File menu tab causes the entire current worksheet to be replaced with the File menu items (see ), while clicking on any other tab reveals a new set of icons in the menu area that can be used with the open worksheet.

The Mac Menu Bar is displayed at the top of the window. This bar contains the Excel File menu, which contains the menu items necessary for working with workbooks. Clicking on the File menu option displays a new set of options that can be used with the workbook, while clicking on the tabs in the Excel ribbon bar reveals a new set of icons in the menu area that can be used with the open worksheet.

The File menu tab contains the menu items necessary for working with workbooks. You will notice that the File menu is different from the other tabs in Excel. The File menu tab contains the menu items necessary for working with workbooks. Clicking on the File menu tab displays a fly-out menu (see ), while clicking on any other tab reveals a new set of icons in the menu area that can be used with the open worksheet.

The left column of the File menu contains the major categories of tasks that can be performed on workbooks. The Info portion of the File menu displays information about the current workbook and the users who have contributed to the file.

The left column of the File menu contains the major categories of tasks that can be performed on workbooks. The Info portion of the File menu displays options for opening the Desktop App, viewing previous versions of the file, and protecting the workbook.

The New portion of the File menu contains features related to creating a new workbook. You will notice that you can create a blank workbook or workbooks that are based on templates. Templates can be particularly useful because they often contain data, formatting, and calculations that relate to common spreadsheet tasks (such as managing a budget or expense report).

The New option on the File menu allows you to create a blank workbook. The New from Template option allows you to create a workbook based on templates. Templates can be particularly useful because they often contain data, formatting, and calculations that relate to common spreadsheet tasks (such as managing a budget or expense report).

Open is used to select a different workbook that you want to be opened in Excel (). Here, you can open recently used workbooks or select a workbook that has been saved on OneDrive (a cloud service provided by Microsoft) or locally on your computer.

Open is used to select a different workbook that you want to be opened in Excel (). With Open, you can open recently used workbooks or select a workbook that has been saved on OneDrive (a cloud service provided by Microsoft).

If the file you want to open is not displayed in the list of recent files, you will need to open your OneDrive in a browser window. Go to OneDrive, select the folder that contains the file you want to open, and click the file name. The file will open in the Excel online application.

section.para: The Save and Save As menu items allow you to (1) save the current workbook, (2) save a copy of the current workbook with a new name or location, or (3) save a copy of the current workbook as a different file type. These options can be helpful as you gain experience with some of the more advanced features in Excel.

The Save As menu item allows you to (1) save the current workbook, (2) save a copy of the current workbook with a new name or location, or (3) save a copy of the current workbook as a different file type. Save As can be helpful as you gain experience with some of the more advanced features in Excel.

The Print menu area contains items related to printing a worksheet. These items include selecting the correct printer, manipulating the various printer functions, and sending a worksheet to the printer. The Print area also displays a preview of how a particular worksheet will look once printed.

Items in the Share area () are used to make this workbook available for colleagues. The Export area allows you to convert your work to another file format (such as PDF) for those colleagues who prefer to review the work in a different application.

section.para: The Close menu closes the open workbook. The Options set of menu items allows you to customize the appearance and functionality of Excel. Finally, the Account menu items allow you to manage users.

The Close menu closes the open workbook. The Preferences set of menu items found on the Excel menu allows you to customize the appearance and functionality of Excel. Finally, the Restrict Permissions menu item allows you to manage users.

section.title: Getting Help
section.para: It is nearly impossible to keep track of all of the options and features available in Excel, and it is not uncommon to need help along the way. In addition to the large amount of Excel content that is available on the internet, there is a large help database available within Excel. To access this help, click on the lightbulb icon (Tell me what you want to do) in the top right corner. The Excel Help area () provides access to Microsoft’s extensive online help materials and information about what version of Excel you are using and how to get started with Excel. As you gain more experience with Excel, you will find that using the help features can save you a lot of time and frustration.

section.para: Take a few minutes and explore Excel; you will find that it is intuitive to use and easy to learn.

section.title: Cells and Worksheets
section.para: All Excel workbooks contain at least one worksheet. Worksheets store data and any calculations done in our workbooks. A single worksheet can contain over 17 billion cells, each of which can contain words, phrases, numbers, dates, and other types of information. They can also contain formulas and functions that perform calculations. Each workbook can contain hundreds of worksheets; the actual number of worksheets that you can store in a single workbook is limited by the memory resources of your computer. Thus, a single workbook can store enormous amounts of information. While most workbook files never come close to using the full capacity of Excel, all workbooks can become unnecessarily complex if they are not managed well. In this section, we will highlight how to work with cells and worksheets in the Excel environment.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Understanding the Excel Cell Addressing Scheme
section.para: Each cell is addressed the same way, by column letter and row number. A cell located in column A and in the third row down from the top of a worksheet is referred to as cell A3 (). This addressing scheme provides a way to specifically define and refer to cells.

The cell addressing scheme will become invaluable when we perform calculations on the data stored in our worksheets; we often use the values stored in one of the cells of our worksheets as an input to the calculation we perform in another cell. Using the values stored in another cell of a worksheet is called referencing a cellUsing the values stored in another cell of a worksheet. . You can also refer to more than one cell at a time as long as the cells are in a continuous block. This is called referencing a range Referring to more than one cell at a time as long as the cells are in a continuous block. of cells. To reference a range A group of cells in a continuous block. of cells, you specify the upper-left cell in the range followed by the ":" character and the lower-right cell in the range. For example, the range reference A2:C2 refers to cells A2, B2, and C2, while the reference B1:B4 refers to cells B1, B2, B3, and B4.

imagedata.alt: Image of Excel where each cell is addressed.

section.title: Entering Data into Cells
section.para: Entering data into cells in the Excel environment is simple: you select the cell you would like to use (by clicking on the cell) and type in the data you would like to store in the cell (see ). Once you are done entering data, press the enter key. To modify data in a cell, you can double-click on the cell you wish to change and make the changes to the text that you desire. Alternatively, you can select the cell you would like to change and modify the text in the formula bar that appears above the cells in the worksheet and below the Excel menu items.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 contains the word Hello.

section.para: Either approach will work well when you plan to add information to or alter a small number of cells in a workbook. However, when you plan to enter data into a large number of cells or when the data you plan to enter follows a repetitive pattern, other approaches are more efficient. demonstrates the use of the Fill feature in Excel.

The Fill feature allows you to automatically populate cells in a worksheet. The Fill menu item is in the Editing group on the Home tab. The Fill Up, Down, Left, and Right options allow you to automatically copy the contents of one cell to other contiguous cells. To do this, you highlight the range of cells that you would like to fill, including the cell that contains the data you would like to copy. Then you select the appropriate directional icon in the Fill menu.

You can also use fill to complete a pattern within a range of cells. demonstrates the process for inserting the numbers 1 through 5 into the range A1:A5 using the Fill command. First, you type the value "1" in cell A1 to provide a starting point for the range of values you would like to insert. Then, select Series from the Fill menu. The Series dialog window allows you to specify several options for the values you would like to insert, which in this case are the numbers 1 through 5 that are to be inserted into column A. Next, set the Series in option to Columns since you are inserting values into column A. The Type remains linear, though there are more advanced options available. The Step value: also remains as ‘1’ because we want each cell to be one number larger than the cell above it. Finally, we set the Stop value: to ‘5’ to ensure that the last number we insert is ‘5’ in cell A5. When you click OK, you will notice that Excel will automatically fill cells A1 through A5 with the values 1 through 5.

You can use AutoFill to have Excel insert values into a range of cells by completing a pattern set up in previous cells (). For example, you can fill cells A1 through A5 with the values 1 through 5 by typing "1" and "2" in cells A1 and A2, respectively. This initiates the pattern you would like to have Excel complete. You then select the range (in this case, range A1:A2). You then place your mouse pointer on the bottom right corner of the selection. The mouse pointer will display as a cross. Drag down to cell A5, and Excel will complete your pattern within the range you selected (A1:A5) by inserting numbers 3 through 5 into cells A3 through A5.

figure.title: Using the Fill Feature in Excel.

section.para: The AutoFill option in the Series dialog box is also quite useful. You can use AutoFill to have Excel insert values into a range of cells by completing a pattern in previous cells (). For example, you can fill cells A1 through A5 with the values 10 through 50 by typing 10 and 20 in cells A1 and A2, respectively. This initiates the pattern you would like to have Excel complete. You then select the range you would like to fill (in this case, range A1:A5) by selecting Fill and Series from the Home tab. In the Series dialog window, change Type to AutoFill and click OK. Excel completes your pattern within the range you selected (A1:A5) by inserting 30, 40, and 50 into cells A3 through A5.

The pattern you enter isn’t limited to increments of one. For example, you can fill cells A1 through A5 with the values 10 through 50 by typing 10 and 20 in cells A1 and A2, respectively. This initiates the pattern you would like to have Excel complete. You then select the cell with the pattern and drag the right corner of the selection down to fill the rest of the cells with the pattern of values.

section.para: Copy and Paste provide an alternative to Fill for inserting the same values into multiple cells in a worksheet. Both Copy and Paste are available in the Clipboard group on the Home tab. As the names imply, Copy and Paste allow you to reproduce a value in one cell and replicate it in other cells. highlights the process for copying the word "Hello" from cell A1 and pasting it into cells A2 through A5. First, you select cell A1 and click on Copy. You then highlight the range of cells (it could be just one cell) where you want the copied values replicated and click Paste. Excel will automatically fill the copied information into the highlighted range.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 with word Hello
Cell A1 through A5 with word Hello

section.para: With most actions you can perform in Excel, there are a number of different ways to do the same thing. For example, you can use keystroke combinations to copy (Ctrl+C) a cell and then paste (Ctrl+V) the values to another cell. You can also access a menu of common actions by right-clicking on a cell.

With most actions you can perform in Excel, there are a number of different ways to do the same thing. For example, you can use keystroke combinations to copy (command-c) a cell and then paste (command-v) the values to another cell. You can also access a menu of common actions by right-clicking on a cell.

section.title: Managing Columns and Rows

videodata.be:name: Mac - Working with Excel Rows and Columns

section.para: There are many times that you need to manage an entire row or column in a workbook. The next few examples cover common actions performed on columns and rows. In most cases, the actions described can be performed on both columns and rows even if the action is only demonstrated on a column or a row.

When working with data tables in Excel, it is not uncommon to find that you need to insert a column or a row to add information that was not originally included in the table. depicts such a scenario in which a table is missing information. In particular, the table is missing a column (between columns A and B) and a row (between rows 1 and 2) that would make the table complete.

To insert a column between columns A and B, you select a cell in column B because Excel will insert a column just to the left of the cell (or set of cells) you select. Click on Insert in the Cells group of the Home menu tab. Notice that you can insert cells, rows, columns, and worksheets. In this case, click Insert Sheet Columns.

Repeat the process to insert a row between rows 1 and 2 by selecting a cell in row 2 and clicking Insert Sheet Rows from the Insert menu items list. Notice the blank column and rows that are inserted in the table. You can now complete the table by adding the missing data.

imagedata.Alt: Cell A1 through D5 with numbers. Table of data is missing numbers.

informalfigure.title: Select a cell at the insert point.

imagedata.alt: Table of number with cell B2 selected. Cell displays the number 5.
Insert dropdown menu
Insert dropdown menu
Insert dropdown menu

informalfigure.title: Table with inserted column and row.

imagedata.alt: Table of data with new blank column and row displayed.
Table of data with inserted values displayed in red to complete pattern of values across the rows and down the columns.

section.para: Occasionally the contents of a cell do not fit within the size of the column or the row; when this happens, the contents of the cell may not display correctly. Notice that the text in cells A1 and A2 of does not fit within the width of columns A and B. To fix this, you must resize column A by first selecting a cell in column A and clicking on Format in the Cells group of the Home menu tab. Next, select Column Width from the Format menu list, and the Column Width window will appear. Here you can change the width of the column and display the text correctly. You can repeat the process to resize column B to accommodate the text in cell B1. It appears that some important information was not visible because columns A and B were not wide enough.

informalfigure.title: Cell contents too large for the cells.

imagedata.alt: Cell displaying Learning Excel is fun!

section.para: There are times that you may wish to hide a row or column in your worksheet. For example, you may want to share the result of a complicated set of analyses with a colleague; but showing all of the steps you needed to arrive at your solution would be more information than your colleague needs.

highlights how to hide a worksheet column. To hide column C, you first select a cell in column C (or multiple cells in column C). The Hide & Unhide menu options are located after navigating to the Home tab, finding the Cells group, and clicking on the Format menu list. You will notice that the Hide & Unhide menu items include options for hiding and un-hiding rows, columns, and worksheets. To hide column C, click on Hide Columns; then, column C should no longer be visible in the worksheet. Notice that the column label for column C is also omitted. To display column C, highlight a range of cells that includes cells in column C (for example, items in columns B and D) and click Unhide Columns in the Hide & Unhide menu. Follow the same steps as above to find the Hide & Unhide menu.

imagedata.alt: Sentence displayed in cells A1 to D1. Column width wide enough to display complete text.
Format dropdown menu with Hide and Unhide options visible.
Format dropdown menu with Hide and Unhide options visible.
Format dropdown menu with Hide and Unhide options visible.
Sentence with column C hidden to create a new sentence for the reader.

section.para: You will often work with large data tables in Excel. Navigating through large data tables can be problematic, particularly when column and row labels are no longer visible. depicts a large data table in which, after scrolling, the company name and the dates are no longer visible. The table is tracking the weekly ending stock price for 58 companies over the course of 22 weeks. Notice that as you navigate through a large table, it is difficult to know what the individual data points in the table mean—particularly when row and column labels are no longer visible. Cell D5 contains the value $195. Without visible data labels, it is impossible for you to know that this is the closing stock price for company 3 on August 12.

The Freeze Panes feature in Excel allows you to lock rows and columns so that they don’t disappear when you navigate through the data table. To freeze the data labels for the table in , select cell B3 and click Freeze Panes, which is found in the View menu tab and the Window group. There are three options in the Freeze Panes menu list; you can freeze the first column, the first row, or a specific worksheet area. The Freeze Panes option will freeze both the column directly to the left of and the row directly above the selected cell. In this case, selecting cell B3 will freeze column A and row 2 in the worksheet. Notice that once the Freeze Panes option is set, it is easy to determine the dates and companies for each cell in the table. To unfreeze data labels, simply select Unfreeze Panes in the Freeze Panes menu list.

section.title: Adding and Deleting Worksheets
section.para: From time to time, you will find it necessary to add or delete a worksheet from a workbook. To add a worksheet to a workbook, go to the Home menu tab, find the Cells group, click on the Insert button, and select Insert Sheet. A new worksheet (Sheet 1) will be added to the workbook. The worksheet is automatically given a name with a number that reflects the number of worksheets in the workbook. You can rename a worksheet by double-clicking on the worksheet tab (for example, ‘Sheet 1’) and editing the text of the worksheet name.

The process for deleting a worksheet is similar to the process for adding a worksheet. To delete a worksheet, you first select a cell within that worksheet; this makes the worksheet you wish to delete the active worksheet. You then navigate to the Home tab, the Cells group, and the Delete button menu. Then, you click on Delete Sheet. You will be asked to verify that you wish to delete the worksheet.

Insert menu with Insert Sheet option displayed with red border.

section.title: Practice Working with Cells and Worksheets
Formatting Cells and Sheets
section.para: Now that you have experience working with cells and worksheets, it is time to turn our attention to defining the format, or appearance, of the cells and worksheets in our workbooks. Specifically, we will cover how to (1) apply basic formatting (i.e., bolding, italicizing, centering) to the contents of cells, (2) define the data type of cells, (3) specify the background color, patterns, and outlines of cells, (4) use the format painter to copy the format of a cell and apply it to other cells in your worksheet, and finally (5) define the page margins and print areas for a worksheet.

Now that you have experience working with cells and worksheets, it is time to turn our attention to defining the format, or appearance, of the cells and worksheets in our workbooks. Specifically, we will cover how to (1) apply basic formatting (i.e., bolding, italicizing, centering) to the contents of cells, (2) define the data type of cells, (3) specify the background color and outlines of cells, and finally (4) use Format Painter to copy the format of a cell and apply it to other cells in your worksheet.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Basic Formatting of Cells

videodata.be:name: Mac - Excel Font and Alignment Menu

section.para: The menu items used to change the appearance of the contents of cells can be accessed from the Font and Alignment groups on the Home menu tab. To change the appearance of a particular cell, you first select the cell and then use the appropriate menu item. To alter the appearance of multiple cells, just select a range of cells before making your changes. Our discussion in this lesson will be limited to the commonly used menu items that appear by default when the Excel window is maximized. It is important to note, however, that you can access more formatting features by clicking the icon (see below) in the bottom right corner of either the Font or Alignment groups.

The menu items used to change the appearance of the contents of cells can be accessed from the Font and Alignment groups on the Home menu tab. To change the appearance of a particular cell, you first select the cell and then use the appropriate menu item. To alter the appearance of multiple cells, just select a range of cells before making your changes. Our discussion in this lesson will be limited to the commonly used menu items that appear by default when the Excel window is maximized.

imagedata.alt: Icon used to display additional options.

section.para: The Font group contains menu items that change the appearance of the text in the cells (see ). The Font Face drop-down menu allows you to choose the font from the list of installed system fonts on your computer; the drop-down list displays a preview of each font to help you pick the best font face for your project. The Font Size menu list allows you to specifically define the size of the text in the cells. The Increase/Decrease items will quickly make text larger and smaller. You will notice that there are also other menu items for making text bold, italicized, or underlined and for changing the color of text.

figure.title: The Font group on the Home menu tab.

imagedata.alt: Font group of the Home menu tab identified by a red border.
Font group of the Home menu tab identified by a red border.
Font group of the Home menu tab identified by a red border.

section.para: Excel also allows you to change the positioning of text within a cell or range of cells (). These features comprise the Alignment group of menu items. You can specify the top-to-bottom position of cell contents using the Vertical Alignment menu items; notice that text can be placed in the top, middle, or bottom of a cell. In addition, the Horizontal Alignment menu items define the left-to-right position of text. Cell contents can be centered or positioned to the far left or right of a cell. The Orientation feature allows you to angle cell contents. Wrap Text allows the entire contents of a cell to appear by displaying it on multiple lines within the same cell. The Indent items increase or decrease the space between the left edge of a cell and where the contents appear in the cell. Finally, the Merge Cells feature allows you to combine cells to create larger cells that span columns or rows in the spreadsheet.

Excel also allows you to change the positioning of text within a cell or range of cells (). These features comprise the Alignment group of menu items. You can specify the top-to-bottom position of cell contents using the Vertical Alignment menu items; notice that text can be placed in the top, middle, or bottom of a cell. In addition, the Horizontal Alignment menu items define the left-to-right position of text. Cell contents can be centered or positioned to the far left or right of a cell. Wrap Text allows the entire contents of a cell to appear by displaying it on multiple lines within the same cell. The Indent items increase or decrease the space between the left edge of a cell and where the contents appear in the cell. Finally, the Merge Cells feature allows you to combine cells to create larger cells that span columns or rows in the spreadsheet.

section.title: Changing the Cell Appearance

videodata.be:name: 2016 - Excel Fill Color, Borders and Patterns
Mac - Excel Fill Color, Borders and Patterns

section.para: Excel also offers options for changing the appearance of the cells. You can change the background color of a cell (or range of cells) by selecting that cell and choosing a background color from the Colors menu list in the Font group of the Home menu tab (see ). To access the Colors menu list, you click on the upside-down triangle next to the Font Colors icon. The Colors menu list is populated with frequently used Standard Colors and those colors that fit the scheme of the colors already used in the spreadsheet (called Theme Colors). To save time, you can apply the last color you used to another cell by clicking on the Font Color icon instead of the upside-down triangle.

If you need access to a wider assortment of colors, or if you would like to apply a pattern to the background of your cells, you can click on More Colors… in the Colors menu list. This will open the Format Cells dialog window to the Fill tab. Here, you will find options for specifying a pattern style and color, applying fill effects, and gaining access to more color options.

imagedata.alt: Color option dropdown list.
Color option dropdown list.
Color option dropdown list.

informalfigure.title: Fill tab in Format Cells dialog window.

imagedata.alt: Fill tab of the Format Cells dialog window.
Fill tab of the Format Cells dialog window.

section.para: In addition to changing the background colors and patterns within a cell, you can define borders for cells. To define a border for a cell (or range of cells), click on the upside-down triangle next to the Borders icon found in the Font group on the Home menu tab (). Excel populates the Borders menu list with popular choices for cell borders. You also have the option to Draw Borders as well as change the color and style of the lines used in the bordering. Additionally, you can access more options by clicking on More Borders…. This will open the Format Cells dialog window to the Border tab, where you can precisely define the border, you wish to apply. As with background colors, you can apply the last border you used to another cell by clicking on the Borders menu icon.

In addition to changing the background colors, you can define borders for cells. To define a border for a cell (or range of cells), click on the upside-down triangle next to the Borders icon found in the Font group on the Home menu tab (). Excel populates the Borders menu list with popular choices for cell borders. As with background colors, you can apply the last border you used to another cell by clicking on the Borders menu icon.

imagedata.alt: Borders dropdown list showing border options available.
Borders dropdown list showing border options available.
Borders dropdown list showing border options available.

informalfigure.title: Borders tab in Format Cells dialog window.

imagedata.alt: Border tab of the Format Cells dialog box.
Border tab of the Format Cells dialog box.

section.title: Defining Data Types
section.para: You can store a number of different types of data in Excel worksheets. Different types of data include text (letters, words, and phrases), numbers, dates and times, and other specialized or customized data types. Most worksheets contain several different types of data.

figure.title: Changing the Data Type of a Cell.

imagedata.alt: Data type dropdown list.
Data type dropdown list.
Data type dropdown list.

section.para: For example, a budgeting worksheet will likely contain text to describe the line items in the budget, dates to describe when a particular income or expenditure item will occur, and numbers to record the monetary value of each line item. While you can use many different types of data in a single worksheet, each cell in a worksheet can only be defined as a single data type. To define the data type associated with a cell, you click on the cell and select the desired data type from the Numbers group of the Home menu tab ().

As you can imagine, Excel will treat different types of data in different ways; for example, you could not do arithmetic on text. Additionally, you will likely want to display some types of data differently than others. In one case, you may wish to display monetary values using currency symbols or fractions as percentages. You may also want to change the number of decimal places that are visible to the user. All of these options are available in the Number group of the Home menu tab.

Should you find that you would like more options for defining the data types and display options for your worksheets, click on More Number Formats… on the Data Types drop-down menu list (). This will open the Format Cells dialog window to the Number tab (). Here you can select the data type and display options for a cell. You will notice that you can see a preview of the options you select in the Format Cells window.

Should you find that you would like more options for defining the data types and display options for your worksheets, click on More Number Formats… on the Data Types drop-down menu list (). This will open the Number Format dialog window. (). Here you can select the data type and display options for a cell. You will notice that you can see a preview of the options you select in the Number Format window.

imagedata.alt: Number formats available in the Number group on the Home menu tab.
Number formats available in the Number group on the Home menu tab.
Number formats available in the Number group on the Home menu tab.

informalfigure.title: Number tab of the Format Cells dialog window.

imagedata.alt: Number tab of the Format Cells dialog box.
Number tab of the Format Cells dialog box.
Number Format dialog box with Number option selected.

section.title: Reusing and Clearing Cell Formats
section.para: It is not uncommon to apply a number of different format options to a cell to make it look how you want it to look. Often you will want to reuse this same set of formatting options to display other elements of your worksheets. Fortunately, you can reuse the same set of formatting options from one cell in other cells quickly and easily using the Format Painter feature (see ). To apply the format from one cell to another, you first select the cell with the formatting you want to copy, and then in the Home menu tab followed by the Clipboard group, click on the Format Painter button. Next, select the destination cell where you want to apply the formatting.

figure.title: Copying the Format of One Cell to Another.

informalfigure.title: Select formatted cell and click Format Painter.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with colored cell selected and arrow pointing to the Format Painter option in the Clipboard group of the Home menu tab.
Worksheet with colored cell selected and arrow pointing to the Format Painter option in the Clipboard group of the Home menu tab.
Worksheet with colored cell selected and arrow pointing to the Format Painter option in the Clipboard group of the Home menu tab.
Worksheet display the copied cell format in the selected cell.
Worksheet display the copied cell format in the selected cell.
Worksheet display the copied cell format in the selected cell.

section.para: Also, when you are using several formatting options in your cells, you might not like the result. In this case, you might find that it is easier to clear away all of the formatting you have done and start over than to change the different formatting options you have already applied. To easily clear the formatting options used in a cell, select the cell you would like to clear and click on Clear Formats in the Editing group of the Home menu tab (). Notice that you can also clear the cell contents and other aspects of the cell.

imagedata.alt: Clear options available from the Clear icon dropdown menu.
Clear options available from the Clear icon dropdown menu.
Clear options available from the Clear icon dropdown menu.

section.title: Formatting Worksheets
section.para: Excel also allows you to format entire worksheets. Excel has several worksheet styles, called themesBuilt-in colors and styles that can be applied to entire worksheets. , that you can apply to quickly add colors and other style effects to your worksheets.

Worksheets are different than other documents because they are not designed around paper boundaries. When you create a word processing document, you do so with the intention of printing the document. The layout of each page is designed by thinking about how the document will appear on paper.

However, when you create a worksheet, you do so to complete a set of calculations. Often you format the various elements of the worksheet to be easy to read, and you will rarely worry about how the worksheet will appear on a printed page.

note.title: Printing Options
note.para: The desktop application for Windows and Mac contains options to modify how a worksheet will display on printed paper. Options for setting the header/footer, page orientation, page breaks, print titles, and print area can be found on the Page Layout menu in the desktop application. See Appendix for more details on how to use these options.

The online version of Excel does not include all the print options available in the desktop application. You can change the page orientation using the Print option on the File menu in the Online version of Excel. See Appendix for more detail on how to use this option.

section.para: As you can tell, we just scratched the surface of the options that are available within Excel for formatting cells and worksheets. Take some time to experiment with the other formatting options available within Excel.

section.title: Sorting and Filtering
section.para: You will often work with large data tables in Excel. In many cases, the data in these tables isn’t organized or sorted in a way that provides you much information. The sorting and filtering features of Excel allow you to reorganize and limit the items in a table to help you make sense of the table.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Sorting Items in a Table
section.para: depicts a portion of a table that lists the industry and financial information of about 20 companies. While the information on the table is useful, it is not presented so that it is easy to compare companies in the same industry. However, we can group the companies in the same industries together by sorting the companies by the "Industry" field; this is done by selecting a cell in the "Industry" column (column C) and clicking on the Quick Sort Ascending icon in the Sort & Filter menu group on the Data menu tab. Because we selected a cell in the Industry column before sorting, the items on the table will automatically sort by grouping all of the companies in the same industries together. Because we are sorting in ascending order, the companies in "Ind A" will appear before the companies in "Ind B". Notice that there is also a similar Quick Sort Descending icon for sorting table items in the opposite order.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displayed with unsorted data and the sort ascending option of the Data menu outlined in red.
Worksheet displayed with unsorted data and the sort ascending option of the Data menu outlined in red.
Worksheet displayed with unsorted data and the sort ascending option of the Data menu outlined in red.
Worksheet with Ind column displayed in sorted order.

section.para: Quick sorting is an easy way to rearrange a data table, but it is limited to sorting just one column at a time. But don’t worry; it is possible to sort a table on multiple fields at the same time. To sort on more than one field at a time, select a cell anywhere in the data table and click the Sort icon, found next to the Quick Sort Ascending button that we used earlier. The Sort dialog window is presented in . You are able to add several fields, or levels, into a single sort. demonstrates sorting the data table from by "Industry" and "Stock Price". When a table is sorted on more than one field, the first level listed will be sorted first. Once that sort is complete, the second-level sort will be performed within the levels or categories of the first sort level. In this case, the table will be sorted by industry first. This will result in a grouping of the companies in the table by industry. As with the Quick Sort depicted in , all of the companies in industry A will appear together, then the companies in industry B will be listed together, followed by the companies in industry C. After the industry sort is complete, the companies in each industry will be sorted and ranked according to stock price. The results of the multi-field sort are also presented in .

Quick sorting is an easy way to rearrange a data table, but it is limited to sorting just one column at a time. But don’t worry; it is possible to sort a table on multiple fields at the same time. To sort on more than one field at a time, select a cell anywhere in the data table and click the Sort icon, found next to the Quick Sort Ascending button that we used earlier. The Custom Sort dialog window is presented in . You are able to add several fields, or levels, into a single sort. demonstrates sorting the data table from by "Industry" and "Stock Price". When sorting on more than one field, the first level listed will be sorted first. Once that sort is complete, the second-level sort will be performed within the levels or categories of the first sort level. In this case, the table will be sorted by industry first. This will result in a grouping of the companies in the table by industry. As with the Quick Sort depicted in , all of the companies in industry A will appear together, then the companies in industry B will be listed together, followed by the companies in industry C. After the industry sort is complete, the companies in each industry will be sorted and ranked according to stock price. The results of the multi-field sort are also presented in .

section.title: Using the Filter Feature
section.para: Sorting can provide interesting insights not readily apparent in a data table. It is sometimes more helpful to reduce the complexity of a data table by removing or filtering out some information. The filtering feature in Excel allows you to display only specific information on a data table. For example, you may wish to only display the companies in "Ind B" from the data table in . demonstrates the process for filtering out all other data in the table, with detailed instructions following .

figure.title: Applying a Filter on a Data Table.

informalfigure.title: Apply filter to display only companies in "Ind B".

imagedata.alt: Filter dialog box with options for the industry column of data.
Filter dialog box with options for the industry column of data.
Filter dialog box with options for the industry column of data.
Worksheet displaying only the companies in Industry B. All other industries have been hidden by the filter

section.para: To select only companies in "Ind B", you would select a cell in the data table and click Filter in the Sort & Filter group of the Data menu tab. Filter icons will appear at the top of each column in the data table to the right of the column labels. Clicking on the upside-down triangle filter icon next to the "Industry" column will display the Filter menu list for the "Industry" column. Notice in that the checkboxes for each industry in the data table. By unchecking all the boxes except the box for "Ind B" and clicking OK, only those companies from "Ind B" will be displayed on the data table.

To select only companies in "Ind B", you would select a cell in the data table and click Filter in the Sort & Filter group of the Data menu tab. Filter icons will appear at the top of each column in the data table to the right of the column labels. Clicking on the upside-down triangle filter icon next to the "Industry" column will display a drop-down of options that can be applied to that column. Select Filter and the Filter dialog box will display a list for the "Industry" column. Notice the checkboxes for each industry in the data table in . Uncheck all the boxes except the box for "Ind B" and click OK to display only those companies from "Ind B" on the data table.

section.title: Practice Working with Sorting and Filtering
References and Calculations

listitem.para: Understand how to identify and reference cells for calculation purposes.
Know the various basic statistical functions and how to use them.
Know the various financial functions and how to use them.
Be able to identify and correct formula errors that affect functions.

section.para: Microsoft Excel may be the most powerful calculator you will ever use. Whether you need to perform simple calculations to help you with your personal budget or a complex analysis to help you make important strategic business decisions, Excel is powerful and flexible enough to fill your needs. In this lesson, we will explore how to use Excel to perform basic calculations.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Cells, References, and Calculations
section.para: As we mentioned in the first lesson, each worksheet in Excel is divided into cells with individual cell addresses. When you click on the top left cell in a worksheet, you select cell A1. Once a cell is selected, you can change what is stored as the value for that cell. A cell’s value can be a specific number, a set of letters, or even a reference to other cells in the worksheet.

To perform calculations in Excel, we often reference the values stored in other cells in our worksheets. Consider the worksheet depicted in ; the numbers 8 and 2 are stored in cells A1 and A2, respectively. The sum of these two numbers is calculated using a formula in cell A3 by referencing both cells (A1 and A2). The result of the formula "=A1+A2" is now stored as a value (in this case, the number 10) in cell A3 that itself can be referenced by other cells.

imagedata.alt: Formula =A1+A2 displayed in cell A3. A1 has a value of 8 and A2 has a value of 2.
Cell A1 displays a value of 8, A2 has a value of 2 and A3 displays the result of the formula as 10.

section.para: The formulas created in Excel can be simple or complex. Formulas can also be reused if the same calculation is performed in a different cell location; depicts such a situation. In this case, we have a table that stores information about three students as well as their scores on a homework assignment and an exam. Because of the difficulty of the exam, the professor has decided to award each of the students 10 bonus points. We need to perform two different calculations: (1) the averageA measure of the most likely or typical value for the things that we are measuring. homework and exam scores for the three students and (2) the new exam score with the bonus points added in.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with three students scores for Homework and an Exam.
Function entered in cell B6 to calculate the average. Formula entered as =(B3+B4+B5)/3.
Clipboard options on Home menu. Paste and copy are outlined in red boxes.
Clipboard options on Home menu. Paste and copy are outlined in red boxes.
Clipboard options on Home menu. Paste and copy are outlined in red boxes.

informalfigure.title: Average formula after copy and paste.

imagedata.alt: Pasted formula =(C3+C4+C5)/3 displayed in cell C6.
Cell D3 displays the formula =C3+C1.

informalfigure.title: Exam w/Bonus formula after copy and paste.

imagedata.alt: Cell D4 displays the pasted formula =C4+C2 and cell D5 displays the value 162.

section.para: To calculate the homework score for the three students, we need to take the sum of the three homework scores and divide by 3 (See ). We place the formula for the average in cell B6, which will then store the average homework score for the three students. The formula for calculating the average exam score is the same as the one we use to calculate the average homework score, so instead of reentering this formula in cell C6, we can simply copy our formula in cell B6 (select cell B6 and click Copy in the Clipboard group of the Home tab) and paste it into cell C6 (select cell C6 and click Paste in the Clipboard group of the Home tab). Notice that the cell references used to calculate the sum of the students’ scores have been automatically updated. They now point to the exam scores in column C instead of the homework scores in column B.

This demonstrates one of the powerful features of Excel. You can reuse your formulas by copying them and pasting them from one cell to another. When a formula is pasted into a new cell, the parts of the formula that reference other cells are automatically updated to point to new cells that are in the same relative position to the cell where the formula is pasted. In this case, the formula in cell B6 references the cells above it to calculate the average homework score. Once pasted into cell C6, this same formula is automatically adjusted to reference the exam scores that reside in the cells directly above cell C6 (the exam scores in column C are located in the same relative position to the formula in cell C6 as the homework scores are to the formula in cell B6). We call cell references that automatically update when a formula is pasted to a new cell relative cell referencesCell references that automatically update when a formula is pasted to a new cell. .

section.title: Absolute Cell References
section.para: There are times when we don’t want our cell references to automatically update when we paste a formula from one cell to another. This is the case when we add the bonus points to the exam scores in . Notice that we can use a formula in cell D3 to add the bonus points (stored in Cell C1) to the exam score for student A (stored in cell C3, see for this formula.) Unfortunately, we are not able to reuse our formula in cell D3 to add the bonus points to the exam scores for students B and C. Notice that if we copy and paste our formula from cell D3 to cell D4, our cell references automatically update to point to cells that are in the same relative position to cell D4 as the formula in cell D3. While we do want our formula to reference the exam score for student B (update the reference to cell C3 to cell C4), we do not want the formula to adjust the reference to the bonus points. The number of bonus points is always stored in cell C1. A cell reference that remains constant when a formula is pasted into a new cell is called an absolute cell referenceA cell reference that remains constant when a formula is pasted into a new cell; contains a $. Whenever possible, it is often best to use an absolute cell reference..

To make a cell reference absolute, we put a "$" character in front of the part of the reference that we wish to remain constant when a formula is pasted into a new cell. In the case of , we would like the reference to the cell that contains the bonus to remain constant. Since we will paste our formula from one row in the worksheet to another, we need the row part of our reference to be absolute. The correct version of our formula in cell D3 is "=C3+C$1" (see part 2.2 of ). Notice that when we copy the corrected formula in cell D3 and paste it into cell D4, our formula behaves as we want it to. The relative reference to the exam score (cell C4) is automatically updated while the absolute reference to the bonus points remains constant.

informalfigure.title: Exam w/Bonus Formula with absolute reference.

imagedata.alt: Cell D3 displays formula with and absolute reference to the row number. =C3+C$1.

informalfigure.title: Exam w/Bonus calculation after copy and paste.

imagedata.alt: Cell D3 displays correct value of 87, cell D4 displays the formula with the correct reference to cell C4 and the reference to cell C1 with the dollar sign in front of the 1. Cell D5 displays the correct value of 95.

section.para: summarizes the four types of relative and absolute cell references. Notice that depending on where you place a "$" character in a cell reference will determine what element of the cell reference will automatically update when a formula is pasted into a new cell. Also note that a cell reference can be both absolute and relative at the same time. This is helpful if you want one part of the reference (row or column) to remain constant when a formula is pasted into a new cell, but you also want the other part of the reference to automatically update.

tr.td: Both the column and row references are relative and will change when the reference is copied and pasted to other cells.

The column reference is absolute and will remain constant when copied and pasted to other cells. The row reference is relative and will change when copied and pasted to cells in other rows of the worksheet.

The column reference is relative and will change when copied and pasted to cells in other columns in the worksheet. The row reference is absolute and will remain constant when copied and pasted to other cells.
Both the column and row references are absolute and will remain constant when the reference is copied and pasted to other cells.

section.para: In summary, you can perform some very sophisticated calculations in Excel by building formulas that reference values stored in other cells of a worksheet. These formulas can be reused in the worksheet by simply copying and pasting the formulas from one cell to another. Excel can either automatically update cell references used in formulas when the formulas are copied from one cell to another make a reference constant when it is pasted from one cell to another.

section.title: Using Summary Statistics Functions

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: As you gain experience with Excel, you will begin to create more complex calculations. More advanced calculations can often be performed using one of the predefined, built-in functions in Excel. You will find that there are a number of built-in functions within Excel that perform quite a range of calculations. In this section, we will cover some basic statistical and financial functions. In subsequent lessons, we will review logical functions as well as text and date/time functions.

Consider . We would like to calculate the sum of the numbers in column A and column B using the SUM function in Excel. You insert a function into a cell by selecting a cell and clicking the function button, which is found right above the column letters.

imagedata.alt: Insert function option icon on the formula bar.
Insert function option icon on the formula bar.

section.para: Once you click on the function button, the Insert Function window appears. Notice you can search for the function you want to use or select from the various categories of functions that are available. We searched for the word "Sum". Highlight the function you wish to use and click OK. The Function Arguments window appears.

Once you click on the function button, the Insert Function window appears. You can select from the various categories of functions that are available. The Commonly Used category is displayed by default. Highlight the function you wish to use and click OK. The function will appear in the selected cell.

informalfigure.title: Select the SUM functions from the Insert Function window.

imagedata.alt: Insert function dialog box with Sum function highlighted.
Insert function dialog box with Sum function highlighted.
Insert function dialog box with Sum function highlighted.

informalfigure.title: Adding Arguments to the SUM Function using the Function Wizard.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with two columns of numbers. Cell A8 displays the Sum function entered by Excel waiting for arguments to be added.
Worksheet with two columns of numbers. Cell A8 displays the Sum function entered by Excel waiting for arguments to be added.
Worksheet with two columns of numbers. Cell A8 displays the Sum function entered by Excel waiting for arguments to be added.

informalfigure.title: Manually typing in the SUM function and related arguments.

imagedata.alt: Sum function =Sum(B1:B7) displayed in cell B8.

section.para: The Function Arguments window is more commonly known as the Function WizardThe Function Arguments window in Excel that allows for the selection of inputs (arguments), provides descriptions of the function of arguments, and lists the function result based on inputs entered. . The function wizard (1) allows you to enter or select the inputs (called argumentsThe inputs of a function. ) for the function, (2) provides a description of the function and each argument, and (3) lists the function result based on the inputs you enter.

Almost all functions require arguments. The arguments of the SUM function are the set of numbers you want to add together. You will notice that you can type in individual numbers or references to cells as arguments for the SUM function. For , we want to add the numbers stored in the range A1:A7. When you click OK, the SUM function will be inserted into cell A8. The resulting calculation is stored as the value of cell A8.

Almost all functions require arguments. The arguments of the SUM function are the set of numbers you want to add together. You will notice that you can type in individual numbers or references to cells as arguments for the SUM function. For , we want to add the numbers stored in the range A1:A7. When you click OK in the Function dialog box, the SUM function will be inserted into cell A8. Selecting the range A1 through A7 will add the argument to the function. Pressing Enter will display the resulting calculation as the value in cell A8.

You can also insert a function into a cell by typing "=" followed by the function name and related arguments in parentheses. As you gain more experience with Excel, you may opt to type functions into cells rather than insert functions using the function wizard. Manually typing functions directly into cells typically saves time, but it also requires that the syntax for the function is typed in correctly. To aid in typing the syntax correctly, Excel displays the syntax of the function on the screen while you are typing the arguments for the function.

imagedata.alt: Syntax of the SUM function displayed as a popup on the screen.

section.para: You can also insert a function into a cell by typing "=" followed by the function name and related arguments in parentheses. As you gain more experience with Excel, you may opt to type functions into cells rather than insert functions using the insert function dialog box. Manually typing functions directly into cells typically saves time, but it also requires that the syntax for the function be typed in correctly. To aid in typing the syntax correctly, Excel displays the syntax of the function on the screen while you are typing the arguments for the function.

Regardless of how you insert functions into your worksheets, you will find that using the built-in functions in Excel is a great way to complete sophisticated analyses quickly and easily. It would be impractical to introduce all of the functions available in Excel in this lesson; though we will introduce a number of common statistical and financial functions that illustrate the power and versatility of Excel, our focus is to familiarize you with how to use functions so that you can apply any of the functions available in Excel on your own.

To help with this aim, let’s examine the anatomy of the SUM function in more detail. The syntax for the SUM function is =SUM(number1, [number2], …). As mentioned above, the arguments for any function are entered in parentheses after the name of the function. Each argument is separated by commas. The syntax for the SUM function demonstrates some facts about interacting with functions. First, functions can accept both required and optional arguments. Required arguments appear without brackets. All required arguments must be entered into a function for the calculation provided by the function to be performed. Functions can also accept optional arguments. Optional arguments appear within brackets. These arguments provide additional information or special instructions for how to handle a calculation depending on the nature of the task the function performs. The omission of optional arguments does not prevent a function from completing a calculation or manipulation.

The SUM function has one required argument, "number1", that can be a number or reference to a range of cells that contains numbers. The SUM function will also accept optional arguments in the form of additional numbers or cell references. If the user were to insert the SUM function without any arguments or provide the SUM function with something other than numbers (such as text), the SUM function would return an error (such as #NAME?) or prompt the user to provide at least one appropriate argument.

In , we demonstrate the use of the SUM function with only one argument (the range A1:A7). The SUM function retrieved all of the numbers stored in that range of cells, took the sum of the values, and stored that sum as the value for cell A8. We could use the SUM function in other ways to achieve the same result. First, we could individually list each of the cell references in the range A1:A7 as separate arguments in the SUM function. The syntax of the SUM function for this approach would be as follows: =SUM(A1, A2, A3, A4, A5, A6, A7)

Another approach would be to individually list each of the numbers stored in the range A1:A7. The syntax for the SUM function using this approach would be as follows: =SUM(31, 81, 13, 33, 89, 54, 10). While this would provide the same result, it is usually a good idea to use cell references as the arguments of your function rather than the actual values stored in the range of cells; it may be that the values stored in the range of cells will change. If you have listed the actual numbers stored in the cells as the arguments of the function, you will need to manually change these values in the function as well. However, if you instead reference the cells that store these values, the result of your function will automatically update when the numbers change.
In addition to the SUM function, we will examine other basic statistical functions:

listitem.para: COUNT(value1, [value2], …) – counts the number of cells in a range of cells that contain numbers
COUNTA(value1, [value2], …) – counts the number of cells in a range of cells that are not blank
AVERAGE(number1, [number2], …) – calculates the simple average of a set of numbers
MAX(number1, [number2], …) – returns the largest value in a set of numbers
MIN(number1, [number2], …) – returns the smallest value in a set of numbers

section.para: The COUNT function is used to calculate the number of cells in a specified set of cells that contain a number. The COUNT function does not include blank cells or cells with non-numeric values in calculating the number of items in a range of cells.

imagedata.alt: Count function displayed in B8, results of the same function in A8.

informalfigure.title: COUNT of only numbers in the range.

imagedata.alt: Data contains a letter in each column. Count function displayed in B8, results of the same function in A8.

section.para: By contrast, the COUNTA function differentiates between blank cells and those with any type of data. The COUNTA function returns the number of cells in a set of cells that are not blank.

imagedata.alt: Two columns of data rows contain numbers except for one row with a letter and one blank row. CountA function results displayed in A8 and the CountA function displayed in B8.

section.para: The AVERAGE function returns the simple average of a set of numbers. As long as the AVERAGE function receives at least one number in the set of arguments, any non-numeric data is ignored when the average is calculated.

imagedata.alt: Two columns of numbers with AVERAGE function results displayed in A8 and the AVERAGE function displayed in B8.

section.para: The MAX function calculates the largest value in a set of numbers. This function ignores blank cells and those with non-numeric data.

imagedata.alt: Two columns of numbers with MAX function results displayed in A8 and the MAX function displayed in B8.

section.para: The MIN function calculates the smallest value in a set of numbers. This function also ignores blank cells and those with non-numeric data.

imagedata.alt: Two columns of numbers with MIN function results displayed in A8 and the MIN function displayed in B8.

section.para: Excel also has a feature called AutoSum that automates the use of the SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MAX, and MIN functions. AutoSum is located in the Home menu tab and the Editing group of items. AutoSum automatically populates the arguments for any of these functions with the continuous section of non-blank cells next to an active, or selected, cell. We will demonstrate the use of the AutoSum feature as part of the set of practice problems.

imagedata.alt: AutoSum dropdown displaying the options Sum, Average, Count Numbers, Max and Min
AutoSum dropdown displaying the options Sum, Average, Count Numbers, Max and Min
AutoSum dropdown displaying the options Sum, Average, Count Numbers, Max and Min

section.title: Practice Working with Basic Statistical Functions
Using Financial Functions

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: Excel is often used as a tool for conducting business and financial analyses. There are dozens of functions and features in Excel that aid in these analyses; we will examine six. You will note that each of these functions requires multiple arguments. All but one of them accepts multiple optional arguments as well. We will limit our discussion of each to the required arguments.

listitem.para: PMT (rate, nper, pv, [fv], [type]) – calculates the payment amount for a loan given the interest rate, number of payments to be made to pay off the loan, and the original loan amount.

PV (rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type]) – calculates the current value (accounting for compounding interest) of an investment given the interest rate, number of payments to be made, and the amount of the payment.
FV (rate, nper, pmt, [pv], [type]) – calculates the future value of an investment given the interest rate, number of payments to be made, and the amount of the payment.

RATE (nper, pmt, pv, [fv], [type], [guess]) – calculates the interest rate earned for an investment given the number of payments made as part of the investment, the payment amount, and the current value of the investment.
NPER (rate, pmt, pv, [fv], [type]) – calculates the number of payments that will be made to pay off a loan given the interest rate, payment amount, and original loan amount.
EFFECT (nominal_rate, npery) – calculates the annual percentage rate for an interest rate given the number of times per year that interest is charged.

section.para: Each of the functions described above is based on the notion of the time value of money, or compounding interest. The time value of money recognizes that a dollar earned today is worth more than a dollar earned next year. The reason is that I can earn a year’s worth of interest on the money I earn today. While this concept is pretty intuitive, most people do not know how to make some of the calculations necessary to apply the time value of money. For example, there is a perfect relationship between the person’s mortgage payment, the length (or term) of their loan, the interest rate they pay on their loan, and the amount of money they borrowed when they purchased their home. If I know any three of these, I can calculate the fourth. However, many people do not know how to calculate their own mortgage payment when they buy a house, even if they know the asking price of the home they wish to buy, the interest rate they will be charged on their loan, and the number of months they will take to pay the loan back.
Knowing how to make your own financial calculations in Excel is a valuable skill, and understanding how to use the functions described in this section will put you on the right track.

The PMT function is used to calculate the payment amount for a loan. The arguments for the PMT function are the interest rate charged on the loan (rate), the number of payments made on the loan (nper), and the original loan amount (pv).

imagedata.ALT: Results of PMT function displayed in B4, example of the function entered in B4 is displayed in C4.

section.para: The PV function is used to calculate the current value of a loan or investment. The arguments for the PV function are the interest rate charged or earned on the investment (rate), the number of payments to be paid or earned (nper), and the payment amount (pmt).

imagedata.ALT: Results of PV function displayed in B6, example of the function entered in B6 is displayed in C6.
