section.para: The FV function is used to calculate the future value for a loan or investment after all of the payments are made or earned. The arguments for the FV function are the interest rate charged or earned on the investment (rate), the number of payments to be paid or earned (nper), and the payment amount (pmt).

imagedata.alt: Results of FV function displayed in B7, example of the function entered in B7 is displayed in C7.

section.para: The RATE function helps you understand how much interest you will be paying on a loan (or earning on an investment if payments are being made to you instead of by you) each month. The RATE function requires three arguments: the number of payments to be made (nper), the amount of the payments (pmt), and the original amount, or present value, of the loan or investment (pv). The RATE function will return the interest rate that is paid on the loan or investment described by the arguments.

imagedata.alt: Results of RATE function displayed in B8, example of the function entered in B8 is displayed in C8.

section.para: The NPER function is used to calculate the number of payments made on a loan before it is paid off. The arguments for the NPER function are the interest rate charged on a loan (rate), and payment amount (pmt), and the original loan amount (pv).

imagedata.alt: Results of NPER function displayed in B9, example of the function entered in B9 is displayed in C9.

section.title: The Effect Function
section.para: When you take out a loan, the loan officer will quote you an interest rate. This is the amount of money you will be charged every time interest is charged on your loan. Interest is usually charged more than once a year. When this happens, the quoted interest rate is divided by the number of times per year that interest is charged. The EFFECT function will calculate the actual amount of interest that is charged or paid each month over the number of periods.

Most home and car loan companies charge interest on a monthly basis, or 12 times per year. For example, the interest rate on my car loan might be 6%. If interest is charged every month on my loan, I will pay 1/12 of the 6% in interest every month.

This may seem like a meaningless point, but it has important implications. It turns out the more times per year I am charged interest on my loan, the more money I pay—even though the interest rate charged is divided by the number of times I pay interest. demonstrates how this works using interest earned on a savings account. Let’s say I save $100 in January and will be paid 12% interest. If I am paid interest once a year, I will earn $12 of interest. On the other hand, if 1% (1/12 of 12%) of that interest is paid to me on the balance of the account every month, I will earn $12.68 in interest over the course of the year. The real, or effective, interest rate I earn is 12.68%. This is known as the principle of compounding interest. Compounding interest is a great thing if I am the one earning the interest, but not so great if I am paying the interest.

figure.title: The Real Cost of Compounding Interest.

imagedata.alt: Twelve months of a bank account earning 12% and a starting value of $100. Compounding the interest shows and total of $12.68 earned on the $100.

section.para: Because most loan companies charge interest monthly, the actual interest rates I pay on a loan will be more than the interest rate I am quoted for that loan. The EFFECT function is used to calculate the real, or effective, interest rate paid on a loan accounting for compounding interest. The arguments for the EFFECT function are the interest rate quoted on the loan (nominal_rate) and the number of times per year that interest will be charged (npery).

imagedata.alt: Results of EFFECT function displayed in B10, example of the function entered in B10 is displayed in C10.

section.title: Test Your Skills: Formulas and Functions

listitem.para: Understand the basics of Boolean functions in comparing, combining, and negating functions.
Know how to use the IF function to create flexible functions.
Know how to use each of the conditional functions to perform calculations with references that depend on values in other cells.
Know how to use reference functions to look up frequently used values.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Boolean Logic and Functions
section.para: Boolean logic is an important concept in all forms of computing. It is also a foundational concept for many of the functions that we will discuss in this unit of the class. Boolean logic is rooted in Boolean algebra, which was developed by George Boole in the 1800s. While a full treatment of the concepts related to Boolean algebra is beyond the scope of this lesson, it is important to understand some basic Boolean concepts.

First, Boolean logic is based on only two values—0 and 1. In Excel, the values 0 and 1 are represented by the values FALSE and TRUE, respectively. Boolean logic and Boolean functions in Excel deal with expressions that result in either the value TRUE or the value FALSE. No other outcome is allowed in Boolean formulas or functions.

Boolean logic and Boolean functions use comparison operatorsAny of these symbols (<,>,<=,>=,=,or <>) used to compare two numbers and yield either TRUE or FALSE. to compare both sides of the expression and, based on that comparison, return either TRUE or FALSE.

An example of this is the expression in cell A2 in . As you can see, the value in cell A1 of the worksheet is the number 50. The Boolean formula in cell A2 checks to see if the value stored in cell A1 is equal to the number 5. In this case, since the value in cell A1 is not 5, the result of the Boolean function in cell A2 is FALSE.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 displays the value 50 and cell A2 has the Boolean formula =A1=5.
The boolean formula in cell A2 displays FALSE because 50 is not equal to 5.

section.para: Notice that there are only two possible results for the formula in cell A2. The value stored in cell A1 is either equal to 5 or not equal to 5. Therefore, the formula in cell A2 will either evaluate as TRUE or it will evaluate as FALSE. In this case, because 5 is not equal to 50, the result of the formula in cell A2 is FALSE. All Boolean formulas and functions are designed so that the only possible outcomes of the formula or function are TRUE or FALSE.

Almost any formula or function in Excel can utilize Boolean logic; highlights examples of using Excel functions to take advantage of Boolean logic. In all cases, a function is used to generate a numeric result. This result is compared to another value. When these two numbers are equal, the Boolean result is TRUE; otherwise, the result is FALSE. In each case, we use the first "=" sign in our formula to signal to Excel that a calculation will be performed. The second "=" sign is used to signal to Excel that a comparison must be made between the function result and the stated value (notice that the third example compares the results of two different formulas). If the values in the comparison are equal, the Boolean result is TRUE. When they are not equal, the Boolean result is FALSE.

figure.title: Using Boolean Functions to Take Advantage of Boolean Logic.

informalfigure.title: Boolean comparison between a function and a specific value.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 through A3 display the numbers 1, 2 and 3. The boolean comparison in cell A4 is =Sum(A1:A3)=5.
The boolean results in A4 is FALSE because the sum of 1, 2 and 3 is not equal to 5.

informalfigure.title: Boolean comparison between a function and a specific cell.

imagedata.alt: Cell A4 displays the boolean comparison of =Sum(A1:A2)=A3.
Cell A4 displays TRUE because the sum of A1 and A2 with values 1 and 2 does equal the value 3 stored in cell A3.
Cell A4 displays the boolean comparison of =Sum(A1:A3)=average(A1:A3)
Cell A4 displays FALSE because the sum of the numbers does not equal the average of the numbers.

section.para: The second important Boolean concept covered in this lesson relates to combining Boolean expressions. It is possible to combine the outcomes of more than one Boolean formula or function and then examine the results of the combined expressions as a separate Boolean expression. We do this using the operators AND and OR. outlines the possible outcomes of combining Boolean expressions with the operators AND and OR.

The operator AND combines Boolean expressions to determine if all of the combined expressions are TRUE. If all of the expressions combined by the operator AND are TRUE, then the result of the combined AND expression will also be TRUE. If any of the expressions combined by the AND operator are FALSE, then the result of the combined AND expression will be FALSE.

The operator OR combines Boolean expressions to determine if any of the combined expressions are TRUE. The result of an expression combined with the operator OR will be TRUE if any of the combined expressions are TRUE. The result of an expression combined with the operator OR will be FALSE only if all of the combined expressions are FALSE.

table.title: The Boolean Operators AND and OR

tr.td:
TRUE. Combining these expressions with the operator AND will result in TRUE
because both of the combined expressions are TRUE.

FALSE. Combining these expressions with the operator AND will result in FALSE
because the second expression is FALSE (even though the first expression is TRUE).

FALSE. Combining these expressions with the operator AND will result in FALSE
because both expressions are FALSE.

TRUE. Combining these expressions with the operator OR will result in TRUE
because both expressions are TRUE.

TRUE. Combining these expressions with the operator OR will result in TRUE
because the first expression is TRUE (even though the second expression is FALSE).

FALSE. Combining these expressions with the operator OR will result in FALSE
because both expressions are FALSE.

section.para: Excel has built-in functions that allow you to combine Boolean expressions (notice that Excel labels a Boolean expression "logical"). The AND function will take at least one Boolean expression (though we usually combine at least two expressions) as arguments. It will result in TRUE if all of the combined expressions are TRUE. The AND function returns FALSE if any of the combined expressions are FALSE. demonstrates the use of the AND function. The syntax for the AND function is below:

figure.title: The AND Function (Notice that both expressions are TRUE).

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with two columns of numbers. Cell A5 displays the expression =and(sum(A1:A3)=6,sum(B1:B3)=15.
Cell A5 displays TRUE because both Sum functions were true in the expression.

section.para: Excel will also allow you to combine Boolean expressions using the OR function. The OR function will take at least one Boolean expression as an argument (though, like the AND function, we usually combine at least two expressions). It will result in TRUE if any of the combined expressions are TRUE. The OR function returns FALSE if all of the combined expressions are FALSE. demonstrates the use of the OR function. The syntax for the OR function is below:

figure.title: The OR Function (Notice that only the first expression is TRUE).

imagedata.alt: Cell A5 displays the expression =OR(Sum(A1:A3)=6,Sum(B1:B3)=6).
Cell A5 displays TRUE because the first Sum function in the expression was true.

section.para: The last Boolean concept covered in this lesson is the notion of negation. Negation refers to the situation in which you check to see if a Boolean expression is not evaluated as TRUE. One way to think of negation is to check to see if the opposite of the logical expression is true.

The Excel function NOT is used to evaluate negation, or the opposite of a Boolean expression. The NOT function is used because it is sometimes easier to define what something is not than it is to define what it is. For example, in , we wish to know if the value in cell A1 is not equal to 5. It would be impractical to check to see if the value in cell A1 were equal to any other number than 5 (think about how many comparisons you may have to make to do this). Instead, it is much easier to check to see if the value in cell A1 is equal to 5 and then return the opposite Boolean result.

The syntax for the NOT function is presented below. As the name implies, the NOT function accepts a single Boolean expression as an argument and returns the opposite of that expression. If the Boolean expression evaluates as TRUE, then the result of the NOT function is FALSE. Likewise, if the expression evaluates as FALSE, then the result of the NOT function is TRUE.
The NOT function may seem counterintuitive, but it can be helpful when it is easier to define what something is not than it is to define what something is.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 has a value of 50 and cell A2 displays =NOT(A1=5)
Cell A2 displays TRUE because 50 is equal to 50.

section.title: Using the IF Function

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: Using Boolean expressions and the built-in Boolean functions in Excel can help you model logic in your spreadsheets in powerful ways. The IF function builds on Boolean logic to help you construct flexible formulas. IF statements accept three arguments. The first is a Boolean expression (logical_testA condition test on a cell value to see if it is TRUE or FALSE.) comparing two values. The other two arguments are used to specify the results of the function depending on the results of the Boolean expression. The second argument (value_if_trueThe value that will be reported if the logical test is TRUE.) is used to specify the result of the IF function if the Boolean expression result is TRUE. The third argument (value_if_falseThe value that will be reported if the logical test is FALSE.) is used to specify the result of the IF function if the Boolean expression is FALSE. The syntax of the IF function is below.

highlights the IF function. In this case, you want the result in cell A2 to display the text "Greater than 10" if the value in cell A1 is larger than 10. If the value in cell A1 is not larger than 10, you want the result in cell A2 to display the text "Not greater to 10." To accomplish this, the arguments for the IF statement in cell A2 must be: =if(A1>10, "Greater than 10", "Not greater than 10").

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 displays the value of 5 and cell A2 has the formula =if(A1>10,'Greater than 10','Not greater that 10')
Cell A2 displays Not greater than 10 because 5 is less than 10.

section.para: Because the expression A1>10 is false, the result of the IF statement in cell A2 is the value_if_false argument, "Not greater than 10".

In all cases, the logical_test argument of an IF function must be a Boolean expression. Any Boolean expression can be used. You can build Boolean expressions using specific values (e.g., 1+2=3), cell references (A1>10), and other functions (e.g., SUM(A1:A3)=10). You can also combine multiple Boolean expressions in the logical_test argument using the AND and OR functions.

The value_if_true and value_if_false arguments can also take various forms. As demonstrated in , the result of the IF statement can display a phrase (notice that the text of the phrase is included in quotation marks). Additionally, you can display a number or date as the result of the IF function. You can also insert formulas or functions to perform different calculations depending on whether the Boolean expression in the logical_test is TRUE or FALSE.

section.title: Nesting IF Functions
section.para: Using the IF function allows you to add flexibility to your worksheets. However, because the IF function is based on a single logical test that can only be evaluated as TRUE or FALSE, a single IF function can only result in two possible outcomes. This would not work for any situation where you would like to accommodate more than two possible results. For example, depicts a situation where you would like to check a number in cell A1 of a worksheet and display "Greater than 10" if the value is larger than 10, "Between 5 and 10" if the value is any number between 5 and 10 (including the numbers 5 and 10), and "Less than 5" if the value in cell A1 is below 5.

Excel allows you to nest an IF function within another IF function as the "value_if_true" or "value_if_false" argument to accommodate more than two possible outcomes. The syntax for a solution to the problem presented in is: =IF(A1>10, "Greater than 10", IF(A1<5, "Less than 5", "Between 5 and 10")). Notice that the second IF function is the value_if_false argument for the first IF function.

figure.title: Nesting IF Function (The result of this function is "Between 5 and 10").

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 displays a 5 and cell A2 displays the function.

section.para: The result of the nested IF function in will be "Between 5 and 10." First, Excel will evaluate the logical test in the first IF function. In this case, the logical test will be FALSE (7 is not greater than 10). The value_if_false argument for the first IF function is the second IF function. Excel will evaluate the logical test in the second IF function to determine if the value in cell A1 is less than 5. This logical test will also be FALSE (7 is not less than 5), so the value_if_false for the second IF function ("Between 5 and 10") will be the result of the nested IF function in cell A1.

It is possible to nest dozens of IF functions within a single IF function to accommodate a large number of outcomes. In general, you will need N nested IF functions to accommodate N+1 possible outcomes. Specifically, to accommodate two possible outcomes, you need one IF function. To accommodate 59 possible outcomes, you need 58 nested IF functions.

section.title: Practice Working with the IF Function
Test Your Skills: Boolean Logic and IF Functions
Conditional Functions

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: Conditional functionsCalculations where the cell references used to complete the calculations depend on the values of other cells in a worksheet. allow you to perform calculations where the cell references used to complete the calculations depend on the values of other cells in a worksheet. We will discuss three of these.

section.title: Using the COUNTIF Function
section.para: The first conditional function is COUNTIF. You will recall the COUNT function from the last unit of the class. The COUNT function is used to determine the number of cells that contain numbers in a range of cells. The COUNTIF function allows you to determine the number of cells within a range of cells that contain a specific value. demonstrates the use of the COUNTIF function. The syntax for the COUNTIF function is below. The first argument of the COUNTIF function is the range of cells within which you want Excel to count the number of matches. The second argument defines the specific value that you would like Excel to match to the cells in the range.

informalfigure.title: The COUNT function determines the number of cells in a range that contain numbers. In this case, the result is 10.

imagedata.alt: Cell A1 through A10 display either a 1 or a 2. Cell D2 displays the function -Count(A1:A10).

informalfigure.title: The COUNTIF function determines the number of cells in a range that contain a specific value. The result of this COUNTIF function is 5.

imagedata.alt: Cell D2 displays 10 for the Count function and cell D3 displays the function =countif(A1:A10,1). The results of this function would be 5 because there are 5 ones in column A.

section.para: The "range" argument will always be a reference to a cell or a range of cells. This reference can contain either relative or absolute cell references. If you plan to reuse the COUNTIF function by copying and pasting the function to other cells in a worksheet you should carefully construct the reference in the "range" argument so that when the function is used in a new cell the reference points to the correct range of cells. This often requires an absolute cell reference. The "range" argument can also reference a named range. This may also aid in the reuse of the COUNTIF function.

The "range" argument will always be a reference to a cell or a range of cells. This reference can contain either relative or absolute cell references. If you plan to reuse the COUNTIF function by copying and pasting the function to other cells in a worksheet, you should carefully construct the reference in the "range" argument so that the reference points to the correct range of cells when the function is pasted in a new cell. This often requires an absolute cell reference.

The "criteria" argument of the COUNTIF function can accept specific values such as numbers, words or phrases (in quotations), or dates. The "criteria" argument may also be a reference to another cell. In this case, the value stored in the referenced cell will be used to find matches.

section.title: Using the SUMIF Function
section.para: The SUMIF function is used to calculate the total for a set of values that match a specific criterion. The syntax of the SUMIF function is below. The first two arguments of the SUMIF function are similar to the COUNTIF function. The first argument is the range of cells within which you want Excel to search for a match to the criterion you set. The second argument defines the criterion used to find matches. The last argument defines the range of cells to be included when the total is calculated. The ranges described in the first and third arguments of the SUMIF function must be the same size. Excel will pair the cells in two ranges, based on the relative positions of the cells within the ranges, so that the appropriate cells in the "sum_range" are included in the calculation. demonstrates the use of the SUMIF function.

imagedata.alt: Column A lists car colors and column B lists the cars prices. Cells D3 though D6 list the four car colors. Cell E3 displays a function =Sumif($A$2:$A$20,D3,$B$2:$B$20).

section.para: Assume you own a small car dealership and are interested in determining the total value of the white, black, red, and blue cars on your lot. The first argument of the SUMIF function depicted in defines the range within which Excel will search for matches. In this case, you want Excel to match car color in range A2:A20. The second argument defines the specific value you wish to use to find a match. In cell E3, you want to calculate the total value of the white cars in your inventory. The cell D3 contains the value "White" and may be used as the second argument in the SUMIF function in cell E3. The final argument for the SUMIF function is the "sum_range" In this case, the values you wish to total together are found in the range B2:B20. Notice that the absolute references used in the first and third arguments of the SUMIF function will allow you to copy and paste the formula in cell E2 to cells E3 through E5 without adjusting the "range" and "sum_range" arguments. The use of a relative reference as the second argument allows the criterion to adjust when the formula is pasted down to cells E3 through E5.

When the SUMIF function described in is executed, Excel will search for all of the white cars in range A2:A20. Once the white cars are found, Excel will use the corresponding cells in the range B2:B20 to calculate the total inventory value. Only those values in cells B2:B20 that pair with the matched values in the range A2:A20 will be included when calculating the total.

section.title: Using the AVERAGEIF Function
section.para: The AVERAGEIF function is similar to the SUMIF function. The AVERAGEIF function is used to calculate the average for a set of values that match a specific criterion. The first two arguments for the AVERAGEIF function are the same as the SUMIF function. The third argument is used to define the range of cells that contain the values to be included in the average calculation. The syntax for the AVERAGEIF function is below:

imagedata.alt: Cell E3 displays the formula =Averageif($A$2:$A$20,D3,$B$2:$B$20). To calculate the average of the white cars.

section.para: depicts a situation similar to that portrayed in . Instead of calculating the total inventory value for each color of car, you want to calculate the average value for each car color. Notice that the arguments used in the SUMIF function for are the same as the arguments used for the AVERAGEIF function in .

Conditional functions are often used to summarize information in a large data table. If the data can be segmented into meaningful groups, the conditional functions can be used to easily calculate basic summary statistics for each of the groups. This is the case with the practice problems for this section.

section.title: Reference Functions

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: Reference functions allow you to look up frequently used values on a reference table in Excel. The correct values are found by matching criteria to corresponding values on the reference table. Excel offers several reference functions for this purpose. XLOOKUP is the newest and most flexible, and it's the function you'll reach for first. VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP are older functions that XLOOKUP was designed to replace — but because so many existing workbooks, templates, and job postings still rely on them, you need to be able to read and write them too. This section introduces XLOOKUP first, explores some of the things it can do, and then walks through VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP so you can recognize them when you encounter them.

section.title: Using the XLOOKUP Function
section.para: It is easier to understand the purpose of a reference function by examining a concrete example. demonstrates the use of the XLOOKUP function. In this situation, you are responsible for assigning letter grades to a section of a class. Range A2:A21 of the worksheet contains information about individual students. Specifically, individual student scores are in column B of the table and you need to insert letter grades for each student based on their scores in column C.

imagedata.alt: XLOOKUP example: selecting student letter grades for their numeric score.

section.para: Range F4:G15 of the worksheet contains a reference table that details the possible letter grades that may be assigned and the minimum score that must be earned to achieve each letter grade. You can use XLOOKUP to assign the correct letter grade for each student by pulling that letter grade from the reference table.
XLOOKUP accepts up to six arguments, though only the first three are required (see the syntax of the function below).
=XLOOKUP(lookup_value, lookup_array, return_array, [if_not_found], [match_mode], [search_mode])

The first argument, "lookup_value," refers to the information that will be used to find a match. When calculating the letter grade for student 1 in cell C2 of , the "lookup_value" is the score earned by student 1, stored in cell B2. Excel will use this score and compare it to a range of values you specify to determine which letter grade to assign.

The second argument, "lookup_array," is the range Excel searches to find a match for the "lookup_value." In , this is $F$4:$F$15, the column of minimum scores in the reference table. The third argument, "return_array," is the range Excel pulls the result from once a match is found — in this case, $G$4:$G$15, the column of letter grades. Notice that "lookup_array" and "return_array" are two separate ranges rather than one combined table and do not necessarily need to be located in adjacent ranges.

The fourth argument, "[if_not_found]," is optional and lets you specify what should display if Excel can't find a match at all — useful for catching data entry mistakes, though it isn't needed in this example, since every student's score should fall somewhere in the grading scale.

The fifth argument, "[match_mode]," determines how Excel decides a match is "close enough." Ask yourself whether Excel needs to find the exact value you're looking for, or whether the closest value at or below it is acceptable. In this scenario, it's unlikely that any student will score exactly one of the minimum cutoff values, so Excel needs to find which range of scores a student's score falls into rather than an exact match. Setting "match_mode" to -1 tells Excel to find an exact match if one exists, or otherwise the next smallest value — exactly the behavior needed to assign a grade to a score of, say, 87 when the reference table lists a cutoff of 80. If "match_mode" is left blank, Excel assumes you want an exact match only, which would return an error for any score that didn't land precisely on a cutoff value.
The final argument, "[search_mode]," controls the order Excel searches in and is left out of this example.
The completed formula for cell C2 is:

Notice the relative reference to the "lookup_value" and the absolute references to "lookup_array" and "return_array." The relative reference allows "lookup_value" to change when the formula is copied to assign letter grades to the remaining students, while the absolute references hold the reference table constant as the formula is reused.

section.title: XLOOKUP with Exact Match and Horizontal Lookup Table
section.para: demonstrates a second use of the XLOOKUP function. In this case, students have been encouraged to wear either blue, white, or pink shirts to the football game. You are responsible for assigning seats for each student depending on the color of their shirt. Those wearing blue should be assigned to section 110. Those with white shirts should receive tickets in section 111, and those wearing pink should end up in section 112.

figure.title: XLOOKUP with Exact Match and Horizontal Lookup Table

imagedata.alt: XLOOKUP example: selecting seating section based on shirt color.

section.para: The reference table for this scenario is arranged differently than the one in — shirt colors run across row 3 in range E3:G3, and the corresponding section numbers run across row 4 in range E4:G4. The formula for cell B2 is:

The "lookup_value" is A2, the shirt color of the first student. "lookup_array" is E3:G3 and "return_array" is E4:G4 — both rows rather than columns, which XLOOKUP handles without any change in syntax. Since an exact match is required here (a student's shirt color either matches a section or it doesn't — there's no "in between" the way there was with test scores), "match_mode" can be left out of the formula entirely, since an exact match is XLOOKUP's default behavior. As in the previous example, relative and absolute references allow you to reuse the function to make assignments for each student.

section.title: Additional Capabilities of XLOOKUP
section.para: The two examples above cover the core mechanics of XLOOKUP, but the function includes additional arguments and behaviors that are useful in situations these two examples don't fully demonstrate. The examples below highlight a few of these capabilities.

section.title: Looking up a value in any direction
section.para: Because "lookup_array" and "return_array" are independent ranges, XLOOKUP can search one range and return a result from any other range, regardless of how the two ranges are positioned relative to each other. For example in , suppose a warehouse inventory sheet lists Product ID in column C and Product Name in column A. You can look up a Product ID and return the Product Name in column A even though it sits to the left of the lookup range:
=XLOOKUP(E2, C2:C50, A2:A50)

figure.title: XLOOKUP with "Return Array" to the Left of the "Lookup Array"

imagedata.alt: XLOOKUP Example with the 'Return Array' to the Left of the 'Lookup Array'

section.title: Displaying a message when no match is found
section.para: Sometimes a "lookup_value" won't have a match at all — for example in , an employee ID that has left the company roster. Rather than letting Excel display its default error, you can use the fourth argument, "if_not_found," to specify your own message:
=XLOOKUP(A2, $D$2:$D$16, $E$2:$E$16, "Not on roster")

figure.title: XLOOKUP When No Match Is Found

imagedata.alt: Syntax of an XLOOKUP example that accounts for the possibility that no match is found.

section.para: In this case, if the employee ID in cell A2 is not found in the range $D$2:$D$16, the formula will return the message "Not on roster" instead of an error (see ).

figure.title: XLOOKUP Result When No Match Is Found

imagedata.alt: XLOOKUP result when no match is found.

section.title: Returning several related values at once
section.para: The "return_array" isn't limited to a single column or row. Widening it lets a single formula return several related pieces of information together. For example in , looking up an employee ID and returning that employee's name, department, and salary all at once:

imagedata.alt: Syntax for an XLOOKUP example that returns more than one value.

section.para: Entered in a single cell, this formula "spills" its three results into the cells to the right automatically (see ).

figure.title: XLOOKUP Result That Returns Multiple Values

imagedata.alt: Result for an XLOOKUP example that returns more than one value.

section.title: Matching on two criteria at once
section.para: Some reference tables need to be matched on two criteria simultaneously — for example in , a budget table with regions listed across the top row and months listed down the first column, where you need the value at the intersection of a specific region and month. You can accomplish this by nesting one XLOOKUP inside another: the inner function finds the matching column, and the outer function finds the matching row within it.
Conceptual version of the formula: =XLOOKUP(MonthValue, MonthRange, XLOOKUP(RegionValue, RegionRange, DataRange))
Syntax of the formula: =XLOOKUP(G2,A2:A13,XLOOKUP(H2,B1:E1,B2:E13))
This formula first looks up the month value in the month range, then uses the result to look up the region value in the region range, and finally returns the corresponding data value.

figure.title: XLOOKUP Example Matching Two Criteria at Once (Nesting XLOOKUP)

imagedata.alt: An example of nesting XLOOKUP functions to match two criteria

section.para: These four examples aren't an exhaustive list of what XLOOKUP can do — "match_mode" also supports wildcard matching, and "search_mode" lets you search a range starting from the last item instead of the first — but they illustrate the range of problems a single, flexible reference function can solve.

section.title: Recognizing the VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP Functions
section.para: XLOOKUP was introduced in 2019 and is available only to Microsoft 365 subscribers. Because of this, an enormous number of existing workbooks, shared templates, and job postings still use two older reference functions, VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP, by name. You will regularly need to read, troubleshoot, or write these functions yourself, so the rest of this section walks through them using the same two scenarios from above.

section.title: Using the VLOOKUP Function
section.para: demonstrates the use of the VLOOKUP function. In this situation, you are responsible for assigning letter grades to a section of a class. Range A2:A21 of the worksheet contains information about individual students. Specifically, individual student scores are in column B of the table, and you need to insert letter grades for each student based on their scores in column C.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays a list of students scores in column B and a lookup table of grades that correspond to different grade ranges. Cell C2 displays the function =VLOOKUP(B2,$E$4:$G$15,2,TRUE).

section.para: Range F4:G15 of the worksheet contains a reference table that details the possible letter grades that may be assigned and the minimum score that must be earned to achieve each letter grade. You can use VLOOKUP to assign the correct letter grade for each student by pulling that letter grade from the reference table.

VLOOKUP accepts four arguments (see the syntax of the function below). The first argument, "lookup_value First argument in a lookup function, holds the information that function will look for in the lookup table. ", refers to the information that will be used to pull a value from the reference table. To calculate the letter grade for student 1 in cell C2 of , Excel uses the "lookup_value", or the score earned by student 1 stored in cell B2, and compares it to the values in the first column of the reference table to determine which letter grade to assign.

The second argument, "table_array A grouping of rows or columns that defines the location of the reference table used in a function. ", defines the location of the reference table. In , this is $F$4:$G$15. Two things about the "table_array" reference should be noted. First, when referring to the reference table in the VLOOKUP function, you should not include the table labels at the top of the reference table in your range. This will sometimes cause errors in your function. Second, Excel will always use the first column of the reference table to find a match for the "lookup_value." You need to define the location of the reference table with this in mind.

Once Excel finds the appropriate match in the first column of the reference table, the desired information can be returned. The third argument of the VLOOKUP function is "col_index_numThe column in the lookup table that contains the information that will be returned by the function. ", or the column in the table that contains the information you wish to have returned. In the case of , the letter grades are found in the second column of the reference table, so the "col_index_num" argument should be 2.

The last argument for the VLOOKUP function is "[range_lookup] Optional TRUE or FALSE argument that specifies if an exact match is required. TRUE (default) allows for an approximate match. FALSE will return a value only if an exact match is found. ." This allows you to specify if Excel must find an "Exact Match" for the "lookup_value" in the first column of the reference table or if the "lookup_value" can fall within a range of values in the first column of the lookup table. To determine whether to use TRUE or FALSE as the "range_lookup" value, ask yourself if an approximate match is OK. If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then you should use TRUE as the "range_lookup" value. Notice that "range_lookup" is an optional argument. If you do not specify TRUE or FALSE as the "range_lookup" value, Excel will assume TRUE or that an "Approximate Match" is OK.

In the scenario presented in , it is unlikely that all of the students will end up with exactly the minimum score for the letter grade they earn. The implication of this is that it is unlikely that Excel will find an "Exact Match" for each student in the first column of the reference table. Since we want Excel to return a letter grade for each student, the answer to the question “is an approximate match OK?” is “Yes.” You want Excel to find the right match for your "lookup_value" by determining which range of scores is appropriate. Therefore, the "range_lookup" value should be TRUE.

When Excel applies the VLOOKUP function, it takes the scores for each student and compares that score to the first column of the reference table. Once an appropriate match is found, Excel will return the letter grade from column 2 of the reference table as the result of the VLOOKUP function. Notice the relative reference to the "lookup_value" and the absolute reference to the "table_array." The relative reference to the "lookup_value" allows the "lookup_value" to change when the VLOOKUP formula is pasted to assign letter grades to the remaining students. Using the absolute reference to the "table_array" holds the reference to the reference table constant when the VLOOKUP function is reused.

section.title: Using the HLOOKUP Function
section.para: The HLOOKUP function is very similar to the VLOOKUP function. The syntax for the HLOOKUP function is as follows:

The main difference between the two functions relates to the use of the reference table. VLOOKUP assumes that the first column of the reference table contains the values used to find a match for the "lookup_value." VLOOKUP then returns the related value from the column specified by the "col_index_num" argument. The HLOOKUP function assumes that the reference table has been rotated 90 degrees to the right. HLOOKUP will look in the first row of the reference table for a match to the "lookup_value." Excel will then return the corresponding value from the row specified by the "row_index_num Row in the lookup table that contains the information returned by the function. " argument as the result of the HLOOKUP function.

demonstrates the use of the HLOOKUP function. In this case, students have been encouraged to wear either blue, white, pink shirts to the football game. You are responsible for assigning seats for each student depending on the color of their shirt. Those wearing blue should be assigned to section 110. Those with white shirts should receive tickets in section 111, and those wearing pink should end up in section 112.

imagedata.alt: Column A lists shirt colors and a lookup table in cells D3 to G4 contains the shirt colors and the corresponding section numbers. Cell B2 displays the function =HLOOKUP(A2,$E$3:$G$4,2,FALSE).

section.para: You can use the HLOOKUP function to help you make the appropriate seat assignments. The "lookup_value" for the HLOOKUP function in cell B2 is A2 (the color of the first student’s shirt). The "table_array" is range E3:G4 (notice that the row labels are not included in the "table_array"). The "row_index_num" is 2 because the section number is found in the second row of the reference table. Finally, you need Excel to find an exact match for each student’s shirt color in the first row of the reference table (otherwise, you can’t make a section assignment). Therefore, an "approximate match" is not OK, and the "range_lookup" value is FALSE. Notice the use of relative and absolute cell references allows you to reuse the function to make assignments for each student.

section.title: Comparing the Three Functions
section.para: Now that you've seen all three reference functions applied to the same two scenarios, a few differences are worth calling out directly. VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP each rely on a single "table_array" and a counted column or row number to identify the return value, while XLOOKUP separates the lookup range and the return range, so there's nothing to count. VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP also default to an approximate match (TRUE) when "range_lookup" is left blank, while XLOOKUP defaults to an exact match — a distinction worth double-checking any time you move between the older and newer functions. Finally, VLOOKUP can only return values from columns to the right of its lookup column, and HLOOKUP is bound to the same restriction for rows below its lookup row; XLOOKUP has no such restriction in either direction. Despite these differences, all three functions accomplish the same fundamental task: finding a value in a reference table and returning a related piece of information.

section.title: Test Your Skills: Conditional and Reference Functions
Date and Time Functions

listitem.para: Know how to effectively use dates and times to make professional-looking worksheets.
Know how to use text functions to easily manipulate text in worksheets.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: We use dates and times to provide context to the numbers and calculations in our worksheets. Without these elements, it would be difficult for those who use our worksheets to understand what the numbers and calculations in our worksheets mean. Effectively using dates and times will help you produce professional-looking worksheets.

section.title: Working with Date and Time Elements in Excel
section.para: You have probably noticed as you have been working with Excel that there are several different ways to display dates and times. There are, for example, 17 different ways to display a date in a cell. Additionally, you can display both a date and time in a cell to depict a specific point in time. While this provides a great deal of flexibility with how you can present time and date information in a worksheet, it can cause some confusion about just how Excel deals with date and time information.

The most important thing to know about how Excel deals with dates and times is that behind all of the formatting of date and time information, Excel treats a date like a number. demonstrates this fact. Cell B1 of depicts a specific point in time: 12/25/2001 at 7:37 AM. Cell B1 is formatted as a date and time in the image on the left. The image on the right depicts this date and time using the General format.

figure.title: How Excel Deals with Date and Time Information.

informalfigure.title: Cell B2 formatted with date and time visible.

imagedata.alt: Cell B2 displays the date 12/25/01 at 7:37 AM.
Cell B2 displayed in general format so the date number 37250.31753 is displayed.

section.para: When formatted in this way, you see that Excel stores date and time information as a number. To Excel, 12/25/2001 at 7:37 AM is the number 37250.31753. Any point in time after time 0 (the beginning of 1900) has a specific number associated with it. Each subsequent moment in time is represented by increasing (even if only by a thousandth of a decimal place) the number that represents time. Put another way, 12/25/2001 at 7:37 AM is represented by a smaller number than 12/25/2011 at 7:37 AM. This allows Excel to consistently store and manipulate date and time information. The different formatting options for displaying date and time information are merely cosmetic. To Excel, all dates and times are numbers.

The numbers used to represent dates and times to Excel can be dissected further to describe how Excel deals with dates somewhat differently than times. Dates are represented by the portion of the number to the left of the decimal place. The date portion of the number 37250.31753, for example, is represented by 37250 (the portion of the number to the left of the decimal place). To Excel, the number 37250 represents the date 12/25/2001. The portion of the number to the right of the decimal place represents a specific point in time on that date. In our example, the portion to the right of the decimal place is .31753. This represents the time 7:37 AM. demonstrates how the different portions of the number 37250.31753 store different information about dates and times.

figure.title: The Different Portions of the Numbers Used to Store Date and Time Information.

imagedata.alt: Image displays the date number portion only for 12/25/01 in a cell and the time portion for 7:37:15 AM in another cell.

section.para: The fact that Excel stores date and time information as a number has important implications. The most relevant implication for our purposes is that it is possible to perform arithmetic on dates and times. Because Excel uses the "whole number" portion of a number to represent dates, we can easily interpret the arithmetic we perform on dates. For example, if I were to add 1 to the number 37250, I would obviously have the number 37251. If I were to format this number as a date in Excel, I would have the date 12/26/2001.

figure.title: Performing Simple Arithmetic on Dates in Excel.

imagedata.alt: Cell B2 displays the date number 37250, cell B3 displays a one and cell B4 has displays the two numbers added together to display 37251. In cell the date number 37251 is displayed as the date 12/26/2001.

section.para: Because Excel treats dates and times as numbers, you can literally perform any arithmetic or other calculation on dates and times that you see fit. Given the nature of how we conceptualize time, it probably makes sense to limit the calculations done on dates and times. As you see in , you can use addition to increment dates. This can be particularly useful if you are setting up a worksheet to track information at regular intervals (like a weekly budget) and don’t want to type in a number of dates. Using subtraction with dates can also be helpful. demonstrates using subtraction to find the number of days that have elapsed between two dates.

imagedata.alt: B2 displays the date 12/25/2011 and cell B3 displays 12/25/2001. The formula =B2-B3 is displayed in cell B4.
Cell B4 displays the number 3652 as the number of days between the two dates.

section.para: You can also perform arithmetic and other calculations on time. demonstrates using subtraction to determine the amount of time that elapsed between two different times in the same day. As you can see, it can be difficult to interpret the results of calculations done on time. In this case, I would expect to see 1 hour and 15 minutes as the resulting elapsed time. The result of subtracting 7:45 AM from 9:00 AM is 0.0520833. This is unexpected unless you understand how Excel deals with time.

imagedata.alt: 9:00AM is displayed in cell B2 and 7:45 AM is displayed in cell B3. The formula =B2-B3 is displayed in cell B4.
The elapsed time unformatted between the two times is 0.05208 displayed in cell B4.

section.para: Excel stores time as the portion of a number to the right of the decimal place. Therefore, a specific time of the day is really just a fraction of a day. makes this point. The time 9:00 AM is represented in Excel as the number .375. The time 7:45 AM is represented as the number .3229167. It is no wonder that the calculated difference between these two times is 0.0520833.

With this in mind, how should you interpret the results of subtracting one time from another? The answer is that the difference between two times (that are both really fractions of a day) is a fraction of a day. The amount of time that elapses between 7:45 AM and 9:00 AM is 0.0520833 days. To determine how many hours or minutes elapse between these two times becomes a matter of converting a fraction of a day to another time frame. To determine how many minutes elapse between 7:45 AM and 9:00 AM, take the difference between the two times (0.0520833), multiply it by 24 (because there are 24 hours in a day), and then multiply it by 60 (because there are 60 minutes in an hour). Multiplying 0.0520833 by 24 and then again by 60 results in 75 (75 minutes elapse between those two points in time).

figure.title: Times as Fractions of a Day.

imagedata.alt: Cell B4 displays the number 3652 as the number of days between the two dates.

informalfigure.title: Times as a fraction of a day.

imagedata.alt: Cell B5 shows the time between the two times. Calculated by multiplying by 24 then by 60.

section.title: Using Date Functions
section.para: In addition to the arithmetic and calculations you can perform on dates and times, there are a number of built-in functions in Excel that help you manipulate dates and times. We will examine seven date functions and three time functions. Here are the date functions we will cover:

listitem.para: TODAY () – returns the current date
NOW() – returns the current date and time formatted as a date and time
DAY (serial_number) – returns the day portion of a date (a number between 1 and 31)
MONTH (serial_number) – returns the month portion of a date (a number between 1 and 12)
YEAR (serial_number) – returns the year portion of a date (a number between 1900 and 9999)
WEEKDAY (serial_number, [return_type]) – returns the day of the week for a date
WEEKNUM (serial_number, [return_type]) – returns the week of the year for a date

section.para: The TODAY function returns the current date by checking with the operating system. You will notice that the TODAY function has no arguments. This is because it requires no information from the user to check with the operating system to retrieve the current date. The TODAY function will update every time that Excel recalculates the cells in the worksheet. If you use the TODAY function to insert a date in a field, the date will be updated every time you open the workbook and probably more often than that.

imagedata.alt: Cell B5 shows the results of the TODAY function

section.para: The NOW function works much like the TODAY function. The now function returns the current date and time by checking with the operating system. The NOW function also does not have any arguments and will refresh when the worksheet is recalculated. You will notice in the images that follow that the time is changing as you scroll down this page.

imagedata.alt: Cell B6 shows the results of the NOW function

section.para: The DAY function returns the day portion of a date. It requires a number as the only argument. The DAY function expects that the number provided as an argument corresponds with a date. The DAY function returns a number between 1 and 31 that represents the appropriate day of the month associated with a given date.

imagedata.alt: Cell B7 shows the results of the DAY function

section.para: The MONTH function behaves much like the DAY function. The MONTH function requires a number as its only argument. It also expects that number to correspond with a specific date and will return a number between 1 and 12, which represents the month associated with that date.

imagedata.alt: Cell B8 shows the results of the MONTH function

section.para: The YEAR function works like both the DAY and MONTH functions. The YEAR function will return a number between 1900 and 9999 that corresponds to the year for the date provided as the argument for the function.

imagedata.alt: Cell B9 shows the results of the YEAR function

section.para: The WEEKDAY function returns a number, typically between 1 and 7, that corresponds with the day of the week for a particular date. The WEEKDAY function requires a number (the function expects the number to correspond with a date) as the first argument. The second, optional argument is return_type. This allows the user to define the day on which the week starts. details the various options for the return_type argument. Essentially, a user can define any day of the week as the first day of the week. While this may seem odd, it is not unusual for an organization to define a day in the middle of the week as the first day of the week for accounting purposes. The return_type argument is useful when this is the case. The default return_type for the function is that Sunday is the first day of the week and that the days of the week are numbered from Sunday (1) to Saturday (7).

imagedata.alt: Cell B10 shows the results of the WEEKDAY function

section.para: The WEEKNUM function is similar to the WEEKDAY function. The WEEKNUM function returns the week of the year for a particular date. The WEEKNUM function requires a number (that corresponds with a date) for the first argument. The second argument also allows the user to define the first day of the week as this may impact the week number for a particular date. The various options for the return_type for this function are detailed in .

imagedata.alt: Cell B11 shows the results of the WEEKNUM function

section.title: Using Time Functions
section.para: In addition to the seven date functions just described, we will discuss three time functions:

listitem.para: HOUR (serial_number) – returns the hour portion of a time as a number from 0 to 23
MINUTE (serial_number) – returns the minute portion of a time as a number from 0 to 59
SECOND (serial_number) – returns the second portion of a time as a number from 0 to 59

section.para: The HOUR returns the hour portion of a time. The HOUR function requires one argument: a number that corresponds with a particular time. It returns the hour as a number from 0 to 23 to avoid confusing times in the morning with times in the afternoon or evening.

imagedata.alt: Hour function references cell B2 that contains a date and time. Cell B5 shows the hour value.

section.para: The MINUTE function returns the minute portion of a time. It also requires a number that corresponds with a specific time as the only argument. It will return a number between 0 and 59.

imagedata.alt: Minute function references cell B2 that contains a date and time. Cell B5 shows the minute value.

section.para: The SECOND function behaves like the HOUR and MINUTE functions. It returns a number between 0 and 59 that represents the second portion of a time.

imagedata.alt: Second function references cell B2 that contains a date and time. Cell B5 shows the second value.

section.title: Practice Working with Dates and Times
Text Functions

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: We use text to provide meaning to the numbers and calculations in our worksheets. Interestingly, text is usually meaningless to Excel. In fact, Excel typically just displays text to the user without any regard for the content of the text it displays. Excel treats text as a string, or collection, of characters. These characters can be letters, spaces, numbers, and other symbols. Excel can store up to 32,767 characters in a single cell, though only 1,024 will be visible or can be printed.

section.title: Using Text Functions
section.para: Excel is not the preferred application for processing large amounts of text, though Excel does have some basic language tools, such as spell check and a thesaurus. Excel also provides a number of built-in functions to help you manipulate text in your worksheets. In this lesson we will examine ten of these functions:

Excel is not the preferred application for processing large amounts of text, though Excel does have some basic language tools, such as spell check. Excel also provides a number of built-in functions to help you manipulate text in your worksheets. In this lesson we will examine ten of these functions:

listitem.para: LEN (text) – returns the length, in number of characters, of a block of text
SEARCH (find_text,within_text, [start_num]) – returns the position of a specific character, word, or phrase within a block of text
LEFT (text,[num_chars]) – returns a specified number of characters starting from the beginning of a block of text
MID(text,start_num,num_chars) – returns a specified number of characters from the middle of a block of text
RIGHT(text,[num_chars]) – returns a specified number of characters starting from the end of a block of text
UPPER (text) – converts a block of text to all upper-case characters
LOWER (text) – converts a block of text to all lower-case characters
PROPER (text) – converts a block of text to title case (the first letter of each new word is capitalized)
CONCAT (text1,[text2],…) – combines blocks of text
SUBSTITUTE (text,old_text,new_text,[instance_num]) – replaces specified characters, words, or phrases within a block of text with new characters, words, or phrases

section.para: The LEN function returns the length of a block of text as the number of characters, including spaces, within that block of text. The LEN function requires only one argument and expects that argument to be a block of text. demonstrates the use of the LEN function. Notice that the LEN function can return the length of a block of text defined in quotes or the contents of another cell.

imagedata.alt: Cell B2 displays the function =Len('Hello World')
The results of the Len function is 11 displayed in cell B2.

informalfigure.title: Length of the text in cell C2.

imagedata.alt: Len function in cell C3 referencing a string stored in cell C2.
Len function returns 36 as the number of characters in the string.

section.para: The SEARCH function returns the position of a specified character, or set of characters, within a block of text. There are three arguments for the SEARCH function. The "find_text" argument is used to specify the characters you would like to find within the block of text. The "within_text" argument is used to specify the block of text you want Excel to search. The third argument, [start_num], is optional. You use the [start_num] argument to tell the Excel where to start searching the block of text. By default, the SEARCH function will start searching at the beginning of a block of text. demonstrates the use of the SEARCH function. Notice that the word, "glad" starts with the tenth character of the phrase, "I’m sure glad I took an Excel class".

imagedata.alt: Cell C3 contains a function to search for the string glad in cell C2.

informalfigure.title: The result of the SEARCH function.

imagedata.alt: Search function returns 10 as the starting position for the word glad.

section.para: The LEFT function returns a specified number of characters from the beginning of a block of text. There are two arguments for the LEFT function: text and [num_chars]. The "text" argument allows you to specify the block of text from which you return the specified number of characters. You specify the number of characters to return using the optional [num_chars] argument. If you don’t specify the number of characters to return, Excel will return the first character.

The MID function returns a specified number of characters from the middle of a block of text. The text argument of the MID function allows you to specify the block of text from which you wish to return some characters. You specify where to begin returning characters from within the block of text using the "start_num" argument. Finally, the "num_chars" argument is used to specify how many characters to return.

The RIGHT function is used to return a number of characters from the end of a block of text. You use the text argument of the RIGHT function to specify the block of text from which you want to return text. The optional [num_chars] argument is used to define how many characters to return. As with the LEFT function, if the [num_chars] argument is omitted, the RIGHT function will return one character.
demonstrates the use of the LEFT, MID, and RIGHT functions.

figure.title: The LEFT, MID, and RIGHT Functions.

imagedata.alt: Left function used to find first three characters in the string.
I'm is returned by the function as the first three characters.
Mid function =Mid(C2,10,4) will return 4 characters starting at the tenth character in the string.
Mid function returns glad.
The Right function Right(C2,5) will return the last 5 characters in a string.
Right function returns class from the string.

section.para: The UPPER function is used to convert the characters in a block of text to all upper-case letters. The LOWER function is used to convert the characters in a block of text to all lower-case letters. Similarly, the PROPER function is used to capitalize the first character of each word in a block of text. All three functions require a block of text as an argument. All three functions return the modified block of text. highlights the use of all three functions.

figure.title: The UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER Functions.

informalfigure.title: Using the UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER functions.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the upper, lower and proper functions referencing cell B1.

informalfigure.title: The results of UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER functions.

imagedata.alt: Results of the three functions show the string in B3 as all upper cased, B3 in all lower case and B4 with first letter caps.

section.para: The CONCAT function is used to combine blocks of text. You can combine several blocks of text using the CONCAT function. The arguments for the function are the blocks of text you wish to combine. outlines the use of the CONCAT function. You may notice the spaces at the beginning of cells B2 and B3. They help ensure that the combined text in cell B4 has spaces in the correct places. You will also notice that the CONCAT function allows you to combine text stored in cells in the worksheet or text that is defined within quotes as an argument to the function.

imagedata.alt: Cell B4 displays the function =Concat(B1,B2, ' an',B3).

informalfigure.title: The result of the CONCAT function.

imagedata.alt: B4 displays the results of the function as I'm so glad I took an Excel class.

section.para: The SUBSTITUTE function is used to replace characters in a block of text with other characters. The first argument, text, is used to define the block of text you wish to alter. The "old_text" argument is used to define the characters you wish to replace. The "new_text" argument is used to specify the text you wish to use as the replacement characters. The final argument, [instance_num], is used to choose which instance of the old_text to replace. If instance_num is omitted, the SUBSTITUTE function will replace all instances of the old_text. describes the use of the SUBSTITUTE function.

imagedata.alt: The substitute function replacing glad with happy is displayed in cell C3.

informalfigure.title: The result of the SUBSTITUTE function.

imagedata.alt: The results of the substitute function returns I'm so happy I took an Excel class.

section.title: Test Your Skills: Date, Time, and Text Functions
The Charting Environment

listitem.para: Know the differences between each type of chart commonly used in Excel.
Determine when it is appropriate to use each chart type.
Create each type of chart using data in a worksheet.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: There are times that raw numbers in a spreadsheet don’t tell the whole story. It is often beneficial to create a visual representation of a dataset to fully appreciate what the numbers mean. Excel has a powerful and comprehensive set of tools that allow you to create professional charts and graphs.

section.title: The Excel Environment for Working with Charts
section.para: You may have noticed that the Excel menu scheme is dynamic. You can customize the menus to fit your needs and your personal work habits. You may not have noticed that the menu schemes also change depending on the tasks that you perform. This is certainly true of the charting menu options. Most of these options only appear when you work with charts.

imagedata.alt: Insert menu with Column chart option dropdown displayed.
Insert menu with Column chart option dropdown displayed.
Insert menu with Column chart option dropdown displayed.

section.para: To access these menu items, you must first insert a chart into your spreadsheet. To insert a chart into a worksheet, click on the Insert menu tab. You will notice that there are a number of different types of charts that you can add to a worksheet. Later in this lesson, we will discuss column, bar, stacked column, pie, and line charts. At this point, any of the chart options will suffice to demonstrate different chart options available in Excel. To add a chart, click on the icon that represents the type of chart you wish to insert and select the specific chart layout you wish to use (see ).

Once you have added a chart to your workbook, the Chart Tools menu items appear (see ). The Chart Tools menu items are available when a chart is selected or highlighted in your worksheet. If you click away from the chart to select any other element of the worksheet (for example a cell), you will notice that Chart Tools menu items disappear. They reappear when a chart is selected.

Once you have added a chart to your workbook, the Chart menu items appear (see ). The Chart menu items are available when a chart is selected or highlighted in your worksheet. If you click away from the chart to select any other element of the worksheet (for example a cell), you will notice that Chart menu items disappear. They reappear when a chart is selected.

Once you have added a chart to your workbook, the Chart menu items appear (see ). The Chart menu items are available when a chart is selected or highlighted in your worksheet. If you click away from the chart to select any other element of the worksheet (for example a cell), you will notice that Chart menu items disappear. They reappear when a chart is selected.

imagedata.alt: Chart menu option that displays when a chart is selected.
Chart menu option that displays when a chart is selected.
Chart menu option that displays when a chart is selected.

section.para: There are two menu tabs associated with the Chart Tools menu scheme: Design and Format. The menu items in both of these groups contain all of the tools you will need to design a chart from scratch or modify a chart.

There are two menu tabs associated with the Chart menu scheme: Chart Design and Format. The menu items in both of these groups contain all of the tools you will need to design a chart from scratch or modify a chart.
The menu items are grouped into categories for all of the tools you will need to design a chart from scratch or modify a chart.

section.title: Design
section.para: The Design tab of the Chart Tools menu scheme contains the menu items used to make high-level adjustments to your charts. depicts the menu items in the Design tab. The Design tab contains the items you would use to change the chart type, select or modify the data depicted in the chart, select from various templates for the layout and color scheme of the chart, and select the location of the chart in the workbook. We will use some of these items in later sections of this lesson to change or enhance the charts we create. Some of the more advanced options for manipulating charts are only available in the installed versions of Excel for Windows or Macintosh. These options are discussed in the appendix.

The Chart Design tab of the Chart menu scheme contains the menu items used to make high-level adjustments to your charts. depicts the menu items in the Chart Design tab. The Chart Design tab contains the items you would use to change the chart type, select or modify the data depicted in the chart, select from various templates for the layout and color scheme of the chart, and select the location of the chart in the workbook. We will use some of these items in later sections of this lesson to change or enhance the charts we create. Some of the more advanced options for manipulating charts are only available in the installed versions of Excel for Windows or Macintosh. These options are discussed in the appendix.

The Data and Change Chart Type groups of the Chart menu scheme contain the menu items used to make high-level adjustments to your charts. highlights these menu items. We will use some of these items in later sections of this lesson to change or enhance the charts we create. Some of the more advanced options for manipulating charts are only available in the installed versions of Excel for Windows or Macintosh. These options are discussed in the appendix.

imagedata.alt: Data and Change Chart Type options used to make high-level manipulations to a chart
Data and Change Chart Type options used to make high-level manipulations to a chart
Data and Change Chart Type options used to make high-level manipulations to a chart

section.para: The Add Chart Element icon allows you to change important detail elements of your chart: titles, legends, data labels, and the axes of your chart. The image below highlights the various chart options that are available to you in this menu scheme.

The Labels and Axis groups allows you to change important detail elements of your chart such as: titles, legends, data labels, and the axes of your chart. The image below highlights the various chart options that are available to you in this menu scheme.

informalfigure.title: The Add Chart Elements menu options.

imagedata.alt: The add chart elements menu options used to add detail elements to a chart.
The add chart elements menu options used to add detail elements to a chart.
The labels and axis groups of menu options used to add detail elements to a chart.

section.para: The Axes group of menu items is used to format the appearance of each of the axes in your chart. You can choose how to display the units for each axis and the intercept for each axis.

The Chart Title and Axis Titles menu items allow you to change where and how each of the titles in your chart will appear. You can also use these menu items to remove a title from your chart. Likewise, the Legend menu item allows you to change the positioning and the display options for the chart legend.

Other menu items allow you to control more detailed elements of your charts or perform and display statistical analyses. For example, you can use the Data Labels and Data Table menu items to display the numeric values for the various elements of your chart and add a trendline to demonstrate the statistical relationships in your data.

section.title: Formatting
section.para: The Format menu items are used to modify the colors and textual elements of your charts. depicts the menu items available in the Format tab of the Chart menu options. While these elements can make your charts more professional looking, we will not address these options in this lesson. Instead, we will focus on how to build and modify charts.

The Format menu items are used to modify the colors and textual elements of your charts. depicts the menu items available in the Format tab of the Chart menu options. While these elements can make your charts more professional looking, we will not address these options in this lesson. Instead, we will focus on how to build and modify charts.

The Format option on the Chart menu will open a Format window on the right side of the screen. This window will allow you to modify the colors and textual elements of your charts. depicts the window with items available on the chart. While these elements can make your charts look more professional, we will not address these options in this lesson. Instead, we will focus on how to build and modify charts.

Chart Format menu used to change the appearance of a chart.

section.title: Working with Column Charts
section.para: Column chartsA chart that compares different categories of items or the same category over time. They depict the types of items (or different times) across the horizontal axis and the values for each category as a pillar rising into the vertical axis. are used to compare different categories of items or the same category over time. Column charts depict the types of items (or different times) across the horizontal axis and the values for each category as a column rising into the vertical axis. contains an example of a column chart that displays the percent of each education level attained for adults 25 years old or older in the United States.

figure.title: Education Attained in the United States.

imagedata.alt: Chart displaying the education attainment in the United States. Seven columns representing the percent of people that attained each level of education from less then 9th grade to graduate or professional degrees.

section.para: To create this chart, you first need to have the data typed in a worksheet (see ).

figure.title: Education Attainment Data in a Worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Data for the education attainment in the United States. Columns for male, female and the entire population of the dataset. Row data representing the percent of people that attained each level of education from less then 9th grade to graduate or professional degrees.

section.para: Select the data that you want to be displayed in the chart. In this case, we want to display the population data in the chart and the various levels of education attained as the labels on the x-axis of the chart. You will need to select both ranges, B5:B12 and E5:E12, before inserting the chart. To select nonconsecutive ranges, you first select one of the ranges and then select the second range while pressing the control button on the keyboard.

Select the data that you want to be displayed in the chart. In this case, we want to display the population data in the chart and the various levels of education attained as the labels on the x-axis of the chart. You will need to select both ranges, B5:B12 and E5:E12, before inserting the chart. To select nonconsecutive ranges, you first select one of the ranges and then select the second range while pressing the command button on the keyboard.

With the data selected, you then insert the chart into the worksheet by selecting a two-dimensional column chart from the Column menu item in the Charts group of the Insert menu tab (). While the chart used in this lesson is a two-dimensional column chart, you could choose any of the other column chart options and achieve a similar result. Notice that you can select from two-dimensional and three-dimensional options as well as 2-D and 3-D Bar charts.

With the data selected, you then insert the chart into the worksheet by selecting a two-dimensional column chart from the Column menu item in the Charts group of the Insert menu tab (). While the chart used in this lesson is a two-dimensional clustered column chart, you could choose any of the other column chart options and achieve a similar result.

Once you select the chart type you wish to use, the chart will be inserted into the worksheet. Notice that Excel automatically arranges the chart for us. Generally, when you select the data you wish to use before creating the chart, Excel will build your chart correctly. In this case, the number of adults in each level of education are correctly displayed as the columns of the chart and the descriptions of each level are correctly included as the x-axis values.

Finally, we will add data labels to the chart that display the percent of people who attained each education level near the top of each of the columns in the chart (). section.title: Working with Bar Charts
section.para: A bar chartA chart with horizontal groupings; a column chart rotated 90 degrees to the right. is essentially a column chart rotated 90 degrees to the right. Everything that we have discussed about column charts also applies to bar charts. contains an example of a bar chart that displays the percent of each first major attained for adults 25 years old or over in the United States who have earned a bachelor’s degree.

imagedata.alt: Bar chart that displays data for the first major for bachelor’s degrees earned by adults 25 years or over in the United States.

Selecting the Bar option will show a preview of how the data will look displayed as a Bar chart. Click OK to change the chart type.
