section.title: When to Use Column and Bar Charts
section.para: Column charts are particularly useful when comparing different categories of items or the same category over time. The education chart compares the level of education for adults 25 years old or older. Column charts are not particularly useful when comparing a large number of categories. A good rule of thumb would be to limit the number of categories (or time intervals) examined to between five and ten.

Bar charts are particularly useful when the category labels are long. A bar chart is also useful when you want to show a distribution from highest to lowest. From the bars, we can compare items or groups to graphically determine which are the largest.

section.title: Working with Stacked Column Charts
section.para: Stacked Column Charts A column chart that breaks the column bars into segments that represent subcategories that are consistent across the columns. are used to compare categories of items or the same category over time. However, a stacked column chart breaks the column bars into segments that represent subcategories that are consistent across the columns. For example, we could construct a stacked column chart to compare education attainment levels for different age groups. We could break each of the bars into segments that represent the percent of people who attained each education level in each age group. demonstrates what this chart might look like.

figure.title: Stacked Column Chart Comparing Education Attainment.

imagedata.alt: Stacked column chart of the level of education by age range from 18 to over 64.

section.para: The bar representing the 18-24 year-olds provides a great example of how a stacked column chart works. The complete bar represents 100% of 18-24 year-old adults. The small grey portion of the bar represents the percentage of 18-24 year-olds (approximately 10%) with a bachelor's degree or more. The orange section of the bar represents the roughly 80% of 18-24 year-olds with a high school diploma. The small blue section correspond with percentage (around 10%) of 18-24 year-olds with less than a high school diploma.

To create the chart, we first type the data in a worksheet. We then select the data we wish to include in the chart. In this case, we will select range B3:G6. This range includes both the education attainment data and the description of each of the three education levels. With the data selected, insert the 2-D column, stacked column chart.
Next, we want to edit the title of the chart (see ). The title of the chart should be “Level of Education by Age Range.”

A chart legend is used to help the user differentiate between the various data series depicted in a chart. There are three data series in the education chart, but it is difficult to know what each color represents without referring back to the original data. Adding a legend to clearly label what each color represents makes it much easier to interpret the chart. To add the legend, click the plus icon, choose the arrow head after Legend, and select bottom from the Legend menu.

A chart legend is used to help the user differentiate between the various data series depicted in a chart. There are three data series in the education chart, but it is difficult to know what each color represents without referring back to the original data. Adding a legend to clearly label what each color represents makes it much easier to interpret the chart. To add the legend, click the Add Chart Element icon on the Chart Design menu, choose Legend, and then select bottom from the Legend menu.

A chart legend is used to help the user differentiate between the various data series depicted in a chart. There are three data series in the education chart, but without a legend, it would be difficult to know what each color represents without referring back to the original data. Adding a legend to clearly label what each color represents makes it much easier to interpret the chart. highlights the legend on the chart.

para.para: The final step is to add axis titles to the chart. Axis titles will further clarify the chart for the user by labeling what each axis of the chart represents. Select the Axis Titles option from the plus icon and then rename the x-axis "Age Range" and the y-axis "Percentage".

The final step is to add axis titles to the chart. Axis titles will further clarify the chart for the user by labeling what each axis of the chart represents. Select the Axis Titles option from the Add Chart Elements icon, select Primary Horizontal, and rename the x-axis title "Age Range". Next, choose Axis Titles and Primary Vertical from the Add Chart Elements menu and rename the y-axis title "Percentage".

The final step is to add axis titles to the chart. Axis titles will further clarify the chart for the user by labeling what each axis of the chart represents. Select the Axis Titles option from the Chart menu, select Primary Horizontal Axis Title, then select "Title Below Axis" and rename the x-axis title "Age Range". Next, choose Axis Titles, select Primary Vertical Axis Title, then click Vertical Title and rename the y-axis title "Percentage".

imagedata.alt: Drop down menu for the Axis titles.
Drop down menu for the Axis titles.
Drop down menu for the Primary Horizontal Axis titles.
Drop down menu for the Primary Vertical Axis titles.
Completed Stacked Column chart with a title, legend and axis titles.

section.title: When to Use
section.para: Notice that the chart compares one hundred percent of the population. The total length of each of the columns represents everyone in the dataset for each age category. The individual segments of the columns represent the percent of people who attained each level of education. The blue segments correspond with the people who do not have at least a high school education. The orange segments represent the percent of people who have graduated from high school but don’t have a bachelor’s degree or higher. The grey segments represent the percent of people who have a bachelor’s degree or higher education.

A stacked column chart is well suited to situations when you need to compare categories (or the same category over time) and when the categories can be decomposed into smaller, common segments across the columns. Because segments of columns are combined into stacks, a stacked column chart can be used to compare more categories than a column chart; however, there are still limitations to the number of categories that can be effectively depicted on a single chart.

section.title: Practice Working with Stacked Column Charts
Working with Pie Charts
section.para: The next type of chart we will cover in this lesson is a pie chart. Pie chartsA chart used to compare the parts of a category (expressed as percentages) to the whole. are used to compare the parts of a category to the whole. Typically, each of the parts is expressed as a percentage of the whole. For example, we could use a pie chart to display the percentage of people in each level of education attainment. We created a chart of this data in the column chart lesson, but a pie chart of the data might be more meaningful.

imagedata.alt: Column chart of Education attainment in the United States for people 25 years and over.
Pie chart of Education attainment in the United States for people 25 years and over.
Pie chart of Education attainment in the United States for people 25 years and over.

section.para: We create this chart by first adding the data to a worksheet. With the data in the worksheet, we select the data we wish to display in the chart. We then insert the pie chart using the 2-D pie chart options in the Insert menu.

In this chart we wish to display the percentage of adults who have attained each level of education as the wedges of the chart. A pie chart is different than the other charts we have examined because it does not have an x or y axis. Any descriptions that would normally appear as x-axis values are called category labels. We will display the descriptions of the different education levels as the category labels for this chart.
Next, we will edit the title of the chart (). The title of the chart should be “Education Attainment in the United States.”

figure.title: Edit the Title for the Pie Chart.

imagedata.alt: Chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.
Chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.
Chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.

section.para: The next step in creating this chart is to add data labels to the chart. This can be done using the Data Labels menu item after clicking the plus icon next to the chart. It can also be done by selecting an appropriate chart layout in the Design tab. You may need to scroll through the chart layout options to find the appropriate template. In this case, we are interested in a title centered at the top of the chart, a legend to the right of the chart, and data labels expressed as percentages centered in each wedge of the chart. This layout isn’t always displayed but can be found under the Quick Layout option. Click on the appropriate layout option to update the chart.

The next step in creating this chart is to add data labels to the chart. This can be done using the Data Labels menu item after clicking Add Chart Element. It can also be done by selecting an appropriate chart layout in the Chart Design tab. You may need to scroll through the chart layout options to find the appropriate template. In this case, we are interested in a title centered at the top of the chart, a legend to the right of the chart, and data labels expressed as percentages centered in each wedge of the chart. This layout isn’t always displayed but can be found under the Quick Layout option. Click on the appropriate layout option to update the chart.
The next step in creating this chart is to add data labels to the chart. This can be done using the Data Labels menu item and selecting Outside End.

imagedata.alt: Data label option available for pie charts.

section.para: We also want to move the display of the legend to the right. This can be done by using the Legend icon and selecting the Show Legend at Right option.

Drop down menu of quick layout options available for the pie chart

The chart now shows the Data Labels next to the slices and the Legend on the right side of the chart. Moving the legend to the right allowed the pie to be displayed in a larger size.

Pie chart with data labels and legend display on the right.

section.para: The last step in creating this chart is to fix the color on the Graduate or professional degree slice. The black text on the dark blue makes it difficult to read. To fix the color, select the slice by clinking on it twice. The sizing handles will be displayed on only that slice. Choose Fill color on the Home menu or Shape Fill on the Format menu. Select the standard color Yellow as the new fill color.

imagedata.alt: Completed pie chart with graduate and professional degree slice displayed in a light yellow so that the data label is readable.
Completed pie chart with graduate and professional degree slice displayed in a light yellow so that the data label is readable.

section.para: At this point, the chart is complete.

section.title: Displaying Data Labels as Percentages
section.para: The default option for data labels is to show the raw values for each of the different categories. Because a pie chart represents the data categories as the percentage of the whole, it generally makes more sense to show the data labels as a percentage of the total values rather than as the raw values. The raw data for the “Education Attainment in the United States” chart in this example are already expressed as a percentage, so the values for the data labels do not need to be changed. When you have raw numbers as the source data for your pie chart, you will need to modify the data labels options to display data labels as a percentage.

This is done by double-clicking on one of the data labels on the chart. This will open the Format Data Labels window. Click on the Label Options tab in the window. Uncheck the box next to Value and check the box next to Percentage. This will display the data labels as a percentage.

This is done by double-clicking on one of the data labels on the chart. This will open the Chart window. Click on the Series "Population" drop-down menu to expose the data labels options. Click on Data Labels to expand the data labels options. Uncheck the box next to Value and check the box next to Percentage. This will display the data labels as a percentage.

figure.title: Displaying Data Labels as a Percentage.

imagedata.alt: Configure data labels to be displayed as a percentage.
Configure data labels to be displayed as a percentage.
Configure data labels to be displayed as a percentage.

section.title: When to Use
section.para: As mentioned above, pie charts express the parts of a data series as percentages of the total for that series. Because of this, pie charts are used to compare within a category of data. Also, because a pie chart expresses values as percentages of the whole, it is typically best to use pie charts when all of the values for a category are known.

Pie charts can be used to compare the parts of the whole even when there are a number of parts for a given category. However, if you have a number of small parts, it may be best to combine these small parts into a larger category. You can then use a special chart type, Pie of Pie, to further decompose the small segments of this new, larger category.

section.title: Working with Line Charts
section.para: We use a line chartA chart that compares the values of a particular category over time by connecting individual data series values. to compare the values of a particular category over time. Because line charts connect individual data series values (the dots), line charts are particularly helpful for uncovering trends in a dataset or relationships between two things. For example, we could use a line chart to examine the relationship between the poverty rate and the level of education attained. (see ).

imagedata.alt: Line chart of poverty rate by educational attainment levels.
Line chart of poverty rate by educational attainment levels.

section.para: To construct this chart, we first select the data that we need in the spreadsheet (). The data that we will use is in range B4:C8. This includes both the data for the chart and the x-axis description values.

figure.title: Highlight the Data in the Spreadsheet.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with the data selected that will be used in the chart.

section.para: With the data series selected in the worksheet, we insert a line chart into the worksheet ().

figure.title: Insert the Line Chart into the Worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Dropdown menu of the line chart options.
Dropdown menu of the line chart options.
Dropdown menu of the line chart options.

section.para: A Linear Trendline can be inserted into the chart to display the statistical relationship between the poverty rate and the education level. To do this, click on the plus icon and select Linear from the Trendline menu options. Notice that a second line is added to the chart. This line represents the linear regression equation that describes how education relates to poverty. It is interesting to note that both the chart line and the trendline are very similar. This means that there is likely a strong relationship between the poverty rate and the education level.

A Linear Trendline Displays the statistical relationship between the values displayed in a chart. can be inserted into the chart to display the statistical relationship between the poverty rate and the education level. To do this, double click on the chart to reveal the chart menu. Click on Series "Poverty Rate" to expand the series options and set the Trendline switch to on. Notice that a second line is added to the chart. This line represents the linear regression equation that describes how education relates to poverty. It is interesting to note that both the chart line and the trendline are very similar. This means that there is likely a strong relationship between the poverty rate and the education level.

A Linear Trendline can be inserted into the chart to display the statistical relationship between the poverty rate and the education level. To do this, click on the Add Chart Element icon and select Linear from the Trendline menu options. Notice that a second line is added to the chart. This line represents the linear regression equation that describes how education relates to poverty. It is interesting to note that both the chart line and the trendline are very similar. This means that there is likely a strong relationship between the poverty rate and the education level.

section.para: The final step in creating this chart is to edit the title for the chart (). The title for the chart should be “Poverty Rate by Educational Attainment Level.”

figure.title: Edit the Title for the Chart.

imagedata.alt: Line chart with chart title Poverty rate by educational attainment level.

section.title: When to Use
section.para: Line charts are particularly useful for identifying trends or relationships between variables in a dataset. Line charts are typically used to compare categories of items over time or to visually depict how two different things are statistically associated.

section.title: Building Charts from Part of a Dataset
section.para: Often you will want to include only part of a dataset in a chart. To build all the charts in this lesson, we have first selected the data for the chart. In most of these charts, the data were located in a range of adjacent cells. When the data were not located in a single range, we selected multiple ranges at the same time before inserting the chart. While this approach works, it can be cumbersome if we need to select many non-adjacent cells in a large dataset.

There are at least two other options for creating charts using data from part of a dataset. The first involves manually adding data series to the chart. This approach is described in the appendix. A second simpler approach involves creating a smaller side table that includes only the data for the chart.

For example, we may want to create a line chart to examine the population trends for selected states in the United States. We have located and imported a large dataset that lists the population for each state each decade from 1950 to 2020 (see ). We would like to select four states at a time to include in the chart. Additionally, we have determined that we don’t want each decade represented in the chart. Instead, we will plot the data from 1960, 1980, 2000, and 2020.

imagedata.alt: Data table that contains population totals by decade for states in the United States from 1950 to 2020.

section.para: We wish to include the states of California, Florida, Illinois, and New York in the chart. Because selecting only the data we need for the chart all at the same time before inserting the chart would prove to be extremely difficult, we should create a smaller data table from this dataset to use for the chart. shows how this data might be constructed.

figure.title: Smaller Table with Only the Needed Data.

imagedata.alt: Smaller Table with only the data needed to create the chart.

section.para: We can use formulas to help us add the data to the table (see ). This will make it easy to change what is included in the chart. The population data is added to the chart using the VLOOKUP function based on the state entered in column L. If the state is changed, the chart data will automatically update with the data for that state. Notice that we use a drop-down list to select the states included in the data table. We use data validation to create the drop-down list. While using data validation makes it easier to change the state included in the chart, the user could just manually change the states in column L.

informalfigure.title: Formula view of the small data table.

imagedata.alt: Formula view of the smaller data table for the chart.

informalfigure.title: Drop-down lists to select the state.

imagedata.alt: Data validation to make it easier to change the data in the smallere table.

section.para: With the chart data located in a continuous range of cells, it is much easier to create the chart. We first select the chart data. With the data selected, we can insert a line chart of the data. With the chart inserted, we can adjust the elements of the chart that we wish. In this case, we would want to change the chart title to something like “Population in Selected States” (see ).

figure.title: Creating a Chart from a Side Data Table.

imagedata.alt: Select the data used in the chart.
Insert a line chart.
The completed line chart.

section.title: Excel Table Features and Elements

listitem.para: Understand the purposes of creating and using tables.
Plan effective Excel tables.
Create a table with existing data.

section.para: Excel has a number of tools for analyzing data. We will discover many of them throughout the remainder of this book. In this lesson, we will examine Excel Tables. Tables provide an easy way to manage and examine related data in a worksheet. Excel tables should not be confused with data tables (one of the what-if analysis features) or PivotTables, which are also tools for analyzing data in an Excel workbook.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Purpose of Tables
section.para: Excel tables are used to effectively store and organize data in Excel. The sorting and filtering tools embedded in tables make it easy to reorganize data and focus on specific data elements. Formatting themes can be applied to tables to make them visually appealing and easy to read.

Using tables also simplifies the process of adding new columns and rows to the dataset. New columns can include raw data or calculations. When a new calculation is added to a table, that calculation is added to all of the cells in that column, eliminating the need to copy and paste the formula to complete the column. When a new row is added to the table, all calculated columns are automatically updated to include those calculations.

section.title: Excel Table Elements
section.para: An Excel table includes both data and other features and elements that are used to manage the data in the table. These features and elements allow the user to quickly format, manipulate, and analyze the data in the table. depicts a table that includes data from a course gradebook.

imagedata.alt: Excel table with gradebook data. Student id in first column, midterm, final, HW1 through HW5 in next columns

section.para: In this section, we will discuss these features and elements.

section.title: Table Headings
section.para: Table headings appear in the top row of the table. Effective table headings provide a concise and clear description of the data stored within each column of a table. Note the table depicted in . The headings clearly describe to the user the individual assignment and exam data in each of the table columns.

imagedata.alt: Gradebook data with first row highlighted in red to indicate that it is the header row of the table.

section.para: Additionally, table headings allow the user to apply sorting and filtering to the data in the table.

section.title: Sorting Data in an Excel Table
section.para: The data in an Excel table can be sorted easily via the table heading tools. For example, the gradebook in includes student first and last names. When you click on the upside-down triangle next to “Last Name” in the table header, the header tools appear. When you click on the Sort Ascending icon, the data in the entire table is rearranged to display the data in alphabetical order based on the student’s last name. Notice that you can sort in either ascending or descending order. shows the resulting order of the rows after sorting the table.

The data in an Excel table can be sorted easily via the table heading tools. For example, the gradebook in includes students’ first and last names. When you click on the upside-down triangle next to “Last Name” in the table header, the header tools appear. When you click on the Ascending icon, the data in the entire table is rearranged to display the data in alphabetical order based on the student’s last name. Notice that you can sort in either ascending or descending order. shows the resulting order of the rows after sorting the table.

The data in an Excel table can be sorted easily via the table heading tools. For example, the gradebook in includes students’ first and last names. When you click on the upside-down triangle next to “Last Name” in the table header, the header tools appear. When you click on the Sort A to Z icon, the data in the entire table is rearranged to display the data in alphabetical order based on the student’s last name. Notice that you can sort in either ascending or descending order. shows the resulting order of the rows after sorting the table.

imagedata.alt: Sort dropdown menu options
Sort dropdown menu options
Sort dropdown menu options
Results of table with last name sorted in ascending order.

section.para: It is also possible to sort the table by multiple table columns by clicking on Sort in the Data menu tab. When sorting a table by more than one column, you will need to determine the order in which the sorting should occur. Notice that the Sorting Options dialog window allows you to define which column will be sorted first. Each subsequent sorting level will be applied once the prior level sort is completed. In this way, a multi-level sort allows the user to “group” the table data by the values in the first level of the sort and then arrange the values of the table within each group of the primary sort.

It is also possible to sort the table by multiple table columns by clicking on Custom Sort in the Data menu tab. When sorting a table by more than one column, you will need to determine the order in which the sorting should occur. Notice that the Sorting Options dialog window allows you to define which column will be sorted first. Each subsequent sorting level will be applied once the prior level sort is completed. In this way, a multi-level sort allows the user to “group” the table data by the values in the first level of the sort and then arrange the values of the table within each group of the primary sort.

imagedata.alt: Data menu with Sort icon highlighted in red.
Data menu with Sort icon highlighted in red.
Data menu with Sort icon highlighted in red.

figure.title: Sort by Last Name then by First Name.

imagedata.alt: Sort dialog box with last and first names added to sort levels
Sort dialog box with last and first names added to sort levels
Sort dialog box with last and first names added to sort levels

section.title: Adding a Table Filter
section.para: Table filters effectively display table rows based on values in a specific column. When a filter is applied, the rows that are not selected for display are effectively hidden from the user and remain in the table. The filter can also be easily cleared to once again display all of the data in the table. In other words, applying a table filter will not delete any data from the table, but it will make the data invisible to the user.

To filter a table, you select the Filter tool in the table header and then specify the rows you would like to display by selecting the desired values from the list. For example, you can display only those students with the last name of “Smith” by filtering as depicted in . Once the filter is applied, only those students with the last name “Smith” will be visible to the user.

To filter a table, you select the filter tool in the table header, select Filter from the drop-down menu and then specify the rows you would like to display by selecting the desired values from the list. For example, you can display only those students with the last name of “Smith” by filtering as depicted in . Once the filter is applied, only those students with the last name “Smith” will be visible to the user.

figure.title: Configuring the Table Filter to Display only Smith.

imagedata.alt: Filter dropdown configured to display only records with the last name of Smith.
Filter dropdown configured to display only records with the last name of Smith.
Filter dropdown configured to display only records with the last name of Smith.
Table results after filter applied. Table now shows two records for Emma and Mason Smith

section.para: You can apply filters to more than one column at a time. Suppose you wish to apply a second filter to the student gradebook table. In addition to seeing only those students with the last name of “Smith,” you also wish to display only “Mason Smith.” After applying the filter on the Last Name column, you can apply a filter to the First Name column, as depicted in . Note that because the Last Name filter has already been applied, there are only two options available for First Name (“Emma” and “Mason”) when you apply the second filter. shows the resulting table once all filters have been applied.

figure.title: Applying a Filter to the First Name Field.

imagedata.alt: First Name filter dropdown with only Mason Smith selected.
First Name filter dropdown with only Mason Smith selected.
First Name filter dropdown with only Mason Smith selected.

figure.title: Result after the First Name Filter is Applied.

imagedata.alt: Table Results after first name filter is applied. Table now only shows Mason Smith’s record

section.para: You can also remove filtering from an Excel table. To remove a filter, click on the upside-down triangle next to the filtered fields and select the “Clear Filter From” option for that field (see ). Filters will need to be cleared individually from each column that contains a filter.

You can also remove filtering from an Excel table. To remove a filter, click on the upside-down triangle next to the filtered fields and select the “Clear Filter” option for that field (see ). Filters will need to be cleared individually from each column that contains a filter.

You can also remove filtering from an Excel table. To remove a filter, click on the upside-down triangle next to the filtered fields, select Filter from the drop-down menu, and put a checkmark in front of the “(Select All)” option (see ). Filters will need to be cleared individually from each column that contains a filter.

imagedata.alt: First Name filter dropdown with Clear Filter option highlighted in Red.
First Name filter dropdown with Clear Filter option highlighted in Red.
First Name filter dropdown with Clear Filter option highlighted in Red.

section.para: We can also perform more advanced filtering. We would use the Text Filters options to apply more advanced filters to columns that contain letters and words (such as student names). Likewise, we would use the Number Filters options to apply more advanced filters to columns with numbers (like scores on assignments or exams). For example, the gradebook contains a column that stores the points earned by each student on the Final exam. If we wanted to see only those students who scored less than 130 points in the exam, we would use a number filter. displays the drop-down menu for the Number Filters.

We can also performance more advanced filtering. We would use the Choose One option to apply more advanced filters to columns that contain letters and words (such as student names) or to columns with numbers (like scores on assignments or exams). For example, the gradebook contains a column that stores the points earned by each student on the Final exam. If we wanted to see only those students who scored less than 130 points in the exam, we would use a less than filter. displays the drop-down menu for the Choose One option.

imagedata.alt: Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than option highlighted in red.
Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than option highlighted in red.
Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than option highlighted in red.

section.para: To display only those students who scored less than 130 points on the final exam, we select the Number Filters menu item then the Less Than... option. This will invoke the Custom Filter dialog box. Making sure that "is less than" is selected in the drop-down list, we enter 130 as the value in the text box (see ). shows the worksheet with only those students who scored less than 130 points.

To display only those students who scored less than 130 points on the final exam, we select the Choose One menu item then the Less Than... option. This will move the insertion point to a text box to the right waiting for the value. Making sure that "Less Than" is displayed to the left, we enter 130 as the value in the text box (see ). Close the dialog box. shows the worksheet with only those students who scored less than 130 points.

imagedata.alt: Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than value of 130 entered.
Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than value of 130 entered.
Filter dropdown for final exam scores with less than value of 130 entered.
Results of filter with only final exams that scored 130 or below.

section.title: Banded Rows and Table Formatting
section.para: Excel tables are often formatted with alternating colors and shading for each row to make it easy to find data across rows. You can edit the color theme for an Excel table using the Table Styles options in the Table Design menu (see ). You will need to select one of the cells in the table for the Table Design menu to appear. The table formatting will be automatically applied to any new rows added to the table.

Excel tables are often formatted with alternating colors and shading for each row to make it easy to find data across rows. You can edit the color theme for an Excel table using the Table Styles options in the Table menu (see ). You will need to select one of the cells in the table for the Table menu to appear. The table formatting will be automatically applied to any new rows added to the table.

Excel tables are often formatted with alternating colors and shading for each row to make it easy to find data across rows. You can edit the color theme for an Excel table using the Table Styles options in the Table Design menu (see ). You will need to select one of the cells in the table for the Table Design menu to appear. The table formatting will be automatically applied to any new rows added to the table.

imagedata.alt: Menu options to change the table color theme.
Menu options to change the table color theme.
Menu options to change the table color theme.

section.title: Calculated Columns
section.para: One of the most useful features of an Excel table is a calculated column. When a calculation is created in any of the cells in a table, the calculation is automatically applied to all of the cells in that column. Notice that we have added an empty column to the gradebook table. If we insert a function to calculate the total points across all exams and assignments for the first student (), the formula will be automatically calculated for all of the other students ().

imagedata.alt: Total points column with formula =sum(Table[@[Midterm].[HW5]]) displayed.

figure.title: Calculation Automatically Applies to All Rows.

imagedata.alt: Results of formula being automatically populated in the rest of the total points column.

section.title: Total Row
section.para: You can easily add a Total Row to an Excel table that will summarize the rows in the table. As the name implies, the default setting for a Total Row summarizes the data by SUM. You can also configure the table to complete other calculations, including COUNT, AVERAGE, MAX, and MIN.

You add a Total Row using the Table Style menu options in the Table Design tab. Recall that to see the Table Design tab, you need to select a cell within the Table. To add a Total Row, check the Total Row box (See ). Notice that the cell below the bottom right corner of the table (in this case cell L16) is populated with the SUM of the values in the last column. To add totals to a different column, select a cell in that column. A drop-down list will appear in the box directly below that column. Select the calculation you would like to perform in that cell for that column.

You add a Total Row using the Table Style menu options in the Table tab. Recall that to see the Table tab, you need to select a cell within the Table. To add a Total Row, check the Total Row box (See ). Notice that the cell below the bottom right corner of the table (in this case cell L16) is populated with the SUM of the values in the last column. To add totals to a different column, select a cell in that column. A drop-down list will appear in the box directly below that column. Select the calculation you would like to perform in that cell for that column.

imagedata.alt: Table menu with Total Row option identified in Red. Table displays the total row with the dropdown options available for the total points column.
Table menu with Total Row option identified in Red. Table displays the total row with the dropdown options available for the total points column.
Table menu with Total Row option identified in Red. Table displays the total row with the dropdown options available for the total points column.

section.para: When you add a Total Row to an Excel table, the calculation will exclude any values that are not displayed in the table because they have been filtered out. If you apply a filter to the table to show only those students with the last name of “Smith” again, any Total Row calculations will include only the values for the students with the last name “Smith” (see ). If you want the Total Row to include all of the rows of the table regardless of whether they are not displayed due to filtering, you can alter the SUBTOTAL function used to create the Total Row to include all values in the summary calculation. See a detailed discussion of the parameters used in the SUBTOTAL function at Microsoft Support: SUBTOTAL function.

imagedata.alt: Total row values display only total points for two records that are filtered based on the last name of Smith.

section.title: Planning Effective Excel Tables
section.para: The first thing you should determine when building a table is what exactly your data needs are. Your data needs will be driven by the types of decisions you need to make or the business problems you need to solve. Often your data needs will require you to collect different types of data. For example, if you are tasked with determining which products to place on an upcoming sales promotion, you would likely need to store data on past product sales, customer preferences, and other things. The right table can meet all these data needs. Once you have effectively designed your tables, you can continue to add data points over time as new data are collected.

section.title: Types of Data
section.para: There are essentially two types of data stored in Excel tables: qualitative and quantitative. Qualitative and quantitative data represent important and distinct aspects of a dataset. Often, qualitative and quantitative data will be used in concert to help uncover an interesting pattern in data.

Qualitative data refer to data that are descriptive. Qualitative data are often used to identify data or classify data into groups. contains the first few rows of a large dataset that includes information about company employees. Several of the columns contain qualitative data. These include employee number, gender, name, location, region, and position. The data in each of these fields describe an aspect of each employee. Some of these fields can also be used to classify the data. You may wish to group your employees by region, for example, to examine if there are any interesting regional patterns regarding your employees.

imagedata.alt: Table of data with columns that contain qualitative data outlined in red.

section.para: Quantitative data represent data that are numerical and are used as the basis for calculations. In this example, the quantitative data are Experience and Salary. Experience refers to the number of years of experience for each employee, and Salary refers to the annual salary of each employee. Either of these fields can be used in calculations. Managers may wish to calculate a total of the salary field to determine how much money the company spends annually on employee wages. Alternatively, they might calculate the average number of years of experience for each employee.

imagedata.alt: Table of data with columns that contain quantitative data outlined in red.

section.para: It is often easy to identify if a measure is quantitative or qualitative. Quantitative data must be numerical. Therefore, any non-number data (such as location or region in this example) must be qualitative measures. However, just because a measure contains numeric data does not necessarily make it a quantitative measure. Quantitative measures are also used for calculations. If it does not make sense to use values of a measure in a calculation, that measure is a qualitative measure.

For example, it makes sense to calculate the average salary of all of the employees in our data. The result of this calculation can be easily interpreted and would convey interesting information. In contrast, the Employee # field contains numerical values. The average of these values can be calculated, but the result of this calculation would not be interpretable and would not convey any meaningful information. Since it makes sense to calculate the average salary, the salary field is considered a quantitative measure. Likewise, because it does not make sense to calculate the average Employee #, the Employee # field is a qualitative measure.

section.title: Principles of Table Design
section.para: Once you have determined an appropriate set of qualitative and quantitative data for your table, you can begin to build an effective table. The next step is to appropriately organize the data in your table. There are five principles of good table design that will greatly improve the organization of the data in your table.

section.title: Column Headings
section.para: The first row of your table should contain a description of the data stored in each column. This description is called a headingThe descriptive text at the top of the data column for each field in a table. . Placing a heading at the top of a column clearly notes for the user what the values in each column mean. In the employee data example, the values in the first row of the table clearly describe what the data in each column mean.

section.title: Each Column Contains Different Information
section.para: The next principle of good table design is having different information in each column. Essentially, this means that you would not place information about a single quality in more than one column. This makes it easier for a user to find all the information for that element. In this example, you would not store information about the number of years of experience for each employee in more than one column.

section.title: No Column Headings Should Be Duplicated
section.para: This principle is related to the last principle. Just as each column should contain different information, none of the column headings should be duplicated. When column headings contain similar information, it becomes more difficult for users to understand the data contained in these columns. For example, the data in the employee example could be enhanced to include information about employee bonuses and commissions (see ).

imagedata.alt: Bonus and Bonus2 headings outlined in red to indicate that similar or duplicated column names can be confusing to the user.

section.para: If the table headings for the two new fields are "Bonus" and "Bonus2" it would be difficult for the user to know how to interpret these columns. Although these descriptions imply that the columns contain data about money paid employees in addition to their salary, these headings do not provide enough information to allow the user to distinguish between the columns. Instead, column headings such as "Bonus" and "Commission" would be more appropriate. Using these headings clearly signals to the user what each column means.

imagedata.alt: Column headings of Bonus and Commission are better identifiers of the data presented in the columns.

section.title: Each Row Contains Information about Only One Instance
section.para: The next principle of good table design is that each row of the table should only contain information about one instance of the data on the table. This means that you should not mix data points from more than one measurement on each row. In the example, this means that you would not include information about more than one employee on each row of the table. If information from more than one employee exists on the same row of the table, it would be difficult to effectively analyze the data in the table.

section.title: Each Instance Is Entirely on Only One Row
section.para: The last principle of good table design is related to the previous principle. This last principle states that all information about an instance on a table should be contained on the same row. If the information for one instance of a table is split across the table, it may be difficult to analyze data effectively or determine which data elements belong to each instance. For example, if information about the bonus for Angie Alvarado were split across rows, it may become problematic for the user to determine how to interpret and use the data for a few reasons:

listitem.para: Having more than one row for a single employee would make it difficult to know if the duplicate rows are for one employee or more than one employee. In the case of , both of the duplicated rows contain the same value for the employee ID. This would lead the user to conclude that both rows contain information about the same employee. If the employee ID was missing from one of the rows or was not the same in both rows, it may be difficult for the user to understand whether the rows contain information about one employee or two.

Having more than one row for a single employee may cause information to be duplicated. If the duplicate rows repeat information for the same column in both rows, it would be difficult to know if the data from the duplicated row should be ignored (to avoid the possibility of double-counting) or included from both rows (to avoid the possibility of missing information).

Having more than one row for a single employee may lead to missing information. As you can see from , each of the duplicated rows contains blank cells. If there is not a complete set of data between the two rows, some important data may be missing.

Finally, having duplicated rows may contribute to inconsistent information across rows. For example, if the value for the commission field for the duplicated rows contained different numbers, it would be difficult for the user to know which of the values is correct.

figure.title: Ensure that All Information for an Instance Appears on Exactly One Row.

imagedata.alt: Two rows highlighted in red first row has incomplete data the second row has complete data. Duplicate rows make it difficult for the user to identify correct data.

section.para: Ensuring that all the data for a single instance of a table exists on exactly one row of the table will eliminate all these sources of confusion.

section.title: Working with Excel Tables
section.para: Excel tables simplify working with related data in Excel. In this section, we will discuss the process of creating and modifying tables. We will also discuss how to reference cells in a table using structured references.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Creating an Excel Table
section.para: There is no limit to how many tables you can add to a workbook. To create a table, select a cell within the range of the table you wish to create and click on Format as Table in the Style menu group in the Home tab. You then select the color theme for the table.

imagedata.alt: Table options dropdown displayed under the Table option on the home menu.
Table options dropdown displayed under the Table option on the home menu.
Table options dropdown displayed under the Table option on the home menu.

section.para: Once the color is selected, the “Format As Table” dialog window appears (). You can confirm the range for the table and indicate whether the data range includes header labels in the first row.

figure.title: The Format as Table Dialog Window.

imagedata.alt: Create Table dialog box with the selected range and My table has headers checked.
Create Table dialog box with the selected range and My table has headers checked.
Create Table dialog box with the selected range and My table has headers checked.

section.para: When you click OK, the table will be created with the formatting you selected (). The table is created in the range of the original data source. In fact, the cells within the table can still be referenced using the column letter/row number (for example, cell F5) method.

imagedata.alt: Data converted to an Excel Table. With dark blue heading row and borders between each row.

section.title: Adding Rows and Columns
section.para: After the table is created, it may be necessary to add rows or columns to the table. There are several ways to add columns and rows to a table. For example, we may wish to add a column at the end of the table to calculate the total points earned across all exams and homework assignments for each student. To do this, select a cell within the last column of the table and choose the Insert Table Columns to the Right option in the Insert drop-down menu in the Cells area of the Home tab. This will add a column to the end of the table ().

After the table is created, it may be necessary to add rows or columns to the table. There are several ways to add columns and rows to a table. For example, we may wish to add a column at the end of the table to calculate the total points earned across all exams and homework assignments for each student. To do this, select the cell next to the last column of the table and type the heading for the new column. Press Enter, and a new column will be added to the end of the table.

imagedata.alt: Insert dropdown menu with Insert Table Columns to the Right highlighted.
Insert dropdown menu with Insert Table Columns to the Right highlighted.
Insert column as last column in Table.

section.para: Notice that Excel may attempt to create a column header for the new column based on the other headers on the table. You can edit the header as you would edit any other cell in a workbook.

imagedata.alt: New column added to Table with default title HW6.

section.para: Once you have the correct column heading, you can add a formula to the first (or any) cell in the column to calculate the total number of points across exams and homework assignments. As described in the last section of the book, the remaining cells in the column will automatically populate with the formula you use to calculate the total points ().

imagedata.alt: Table with total points column updated with formula.

section.para: You likely noticed that the Insert drop-down list allowed you to use a similar method to add a row to the table. A simpler method for adding a row to a table involves just typing the new row into the row directly below the last used row of the table. demonstrates this method. As soon as new data is entered into the row directly below the last used row of the table, the table automatically expands to include this new data. This method also works for adding columns. Once data is entered into the column directly to the right of the last used column in the table, the table automatically expands to include the new column.

Just like inserting the new column, a row can be added to the table with a similar method. The simplest method for adding a row to a table involves just typing the new row into the row directly below the last used row of the table. demonstrates this method. As soon as new data is entered into the row directly below the last used row of the table, the table automatically expands to include this new data.

imagedata.alt: New row a data inserted at the bottom of the table for student 99-9999-9999.

section.title: Deleting Rows and Columns
section.para: There are also times that it is necessary to delete a column or row from an Excel table. Again, there are several methods for deleting rows and columns. To delete the row we just added to the end of the table, select one or more cells in the row you want to delete. The Delete option for Table Columns and Rows is on the Home tab or can be accessed through the right-click shortcut menu. displays the right-click menu of actions that can be completed on that range. Select Delete and Table Rows from the menu options. The row will now be deleted from the table. A similar process can be used to delete columns from the table.

imagedata.alt: Shortcut menu options with Delete Table Row selected.
Shortcut menu options with Delete Table Row selected.
Shortcut menu options with Delete Table Row selected.

section.title: Referencing Excel Tables
section.para: As mentioned earlier in this section, the cells in a table can be referenced using the cell address just like any other cell in Excel. Cells, columns, and rows can also be accessed using Structured References. Structured references allow you to reference the data in a table using the names of the table elements. demonstrates the syntax for a structured reference. Notice that the formula in cell L3 will calculate the sum of the midterm and final exams for the first student using references to cell addresses. The formula in cell L4 completes the same calculation using structured references to the table data.

figure.title: Example of Cell Reference and Structured Reference Formulas.

imagedata.alt: Example of a standard sum function in cell L3 and a structured reference for the same columns in cell L4.

section.para: The key benefit of using structured references is that the references will automatically adjust when you add or delete elements from the table. In most cases, when a cell within a table is referenced, structured references are used by default. This means that you will likely not need to create your own structured references. Visit Microsoft Support: Using Structured References to read more about structured references.

section.title: Introduction

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: The formatting tools in Excel are used to change the appearance of the cells in our worksheets without changing the actual data in the cells. These tools allow us to change both the text color and the background fill color of the cells that contain the text. Changing cell formats generally improves the aesthetic appeal of the worksheets. Using formatting tools can be a great way to make worksheets that look professional and are easy to use.

Just as we use cell formatting to improve the appearance of a cell, we also use formatting on important cells to send users clues about the structure of the data in the worksheet or to simply draw increased attention to the cells. Notice how centering and bolding the cells at the top of signals to the user that these cells serve as column headings that describe the data immediately below them. Likewise, changing the appearance of the last row of the data (changing the font size and color coupled with adding the top border) draws the users’ attention to the calculated summary of the data.

figure.title: Formatting Text to Draw User Attention to Focal Cells.

imagedata.alt: Table of data with the appearance of the last row of the data (changing the font size and color coupled with adding the top border) draws the users’ attention to the calculated summary of the data

section.para: The example in remains static, or unchanging, even if the data values change. The top two cells will remain bolded and centered to signify that they contain column headings. Likewise, the last row will contain a larger red font to draw user attention to the calculated summary of the data. In many cases, static formatting such as this is sufficient to maintain the aesthetic appeal and effectiveness of the worksheet. The highlighted cells will always contain the data that should be highlighted for the user.

There are times, however, when it is difficult to predict which cells should be highlighted for the user. In , the cells containing the two largest sales amounts for both January and February are highlighted with a green font and background fill. Additionally, the two smallest sales amounts are formatted with a yellow font and background fill. For any given month, it would be difficult to predict which of the locations would produce the largest and smallest sales amounts. While there may be some consistency in sales across months, it seems likely that different locations would occupy the highest and lowest sales positions from month to month.

figure.title: Locations with Highest Sales in Each Month.

imagedata.alt: Table of data with high sales values highlighted in green and low sale values highlighted in yellow.

section.para: In the case of , some of the formatting used in the cells is applied based on the values contained in the cells. Formatting that is applied to a range of cells based on the values in the cells is called Conditional FormattingFormatting that is applied to a range of cells based on the values in the cells.. While it would be possible to inspect data and apply conditional formatting manually, Excel contains a number of powerful yet simple tools for automatically applying conditional formatting to a range of cells.

Conditional formatting can be extremely helpful. Formatting cells based on the values contained in those cells helps a user to visually examine a dataset quickly and easily. When exceptional or unique data are automatically formatted in a range of cells, these unusual values are immediately apparent to the user. This allows the user to identify patterns or trends in a dataset or unexpected data that might need further examination.

In this lesson, we will discuss how to use these tools to automatically change the formatted appearance of cells based on the values in those cells. First, we will discuss how to use these tools to create rules for changing the font and background fill colors of cells. We will then describe how conditional formatting can be used to add symbols or icons (such as arrows or colored bars) to cells based on the values of those cells. The addition of symbols and icons to cells can help the user make inferences about the highlighted data. In the last section of this lesson, we will describe how to clear conditional formatting from a range of cells and manage the conditional formatting rules used in a workbook.

section.title: Using Conditional Formatting
section.para: It’s time to start using conditional formatting to highlight ranges of cells based on the values in those cells. Conditional formatting rules can be based on specific target values, such as highlighting all cells with a value greater than 10. Rules can also be created to highlight cells based on how the values in the cells compare with the other values in the same range of cells. For example, we could construct a rule to highlight the 10 largest values within a range of cells. After discussing how to navigate to the conditional formatting tools in Excel, we will describe how to create both types of rules.

section.title: The Conditional Formatting Rules in Excel
section.para: We access the conditional formatting tools on the Home menu tab in Excel.

imagedata.alt: Home menu with Conditional Formatting icon highlighted in red.
Home menu with Conditional Formatting icon highlighted in red.
Home menu with Conditional Formatting icon highlighted in red.

section.para: When you click on the conditional formatting menu icon, a summary list of the conditional formatting tools appears. These tools can be divided into three different categories. The first category (see ) contains the rules used to change the text and background fill colors of the target range of cells. This category is comprised of the “Highlight Cells Rules” and “Top/Bottom Rules” menu items.

When you click on the conditional formatting menu icon, a summary list of the conditional formatting tools appears. These tools can be divided into three different categories. The first category (see ) contains the rules used to change the text and background fill colors of the target range of cells. This category is comprised of the Highlight Cell Rules and Top/Bottom Rules menu items.

figure.title: Rules for Changing the Text and Background Fill Colors.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with Highlight Rules and Top/Bottom Rules highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Highlight Rules and Top/Bottom Rules highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Highlight Rules and Top/Bottom Rules highlighted in red.

section.para: The second category of tools (see ) is used to add color bars, symbols, and icons to the target range of cells. This category contains the tools for Data Bars, Color Scales, and Icon Sets.

figure.title: Rules for Adding Color Bars, Icons, and Symbols to Cells.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with Data Bars, Color Scales and Icon Sets highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Data Bars, Color Scales and Icon Sets highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Data Bars, Color Scales and Icon Sets highlighted in red.

section.para: The last category of conditional formatting tools includes those tools used to clear and manage the rules in a workbook. The tools in this category include the wizard for creating a new rule, clearing existing rules, and managing an existing rule.

The last category of conditional formatting tools includes those tools used to clear and manage the rules in a workbook. The tools in this category include clearing existing rules and managing an existing rule.

figure.title: Tools for Clearing and Managing Conditional Formatting Rules.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with New Rule, Clear Rules and Manage Rules highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with New Rule, Clear Rules and Manage Rules highlighted in red.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules and Manage Rules highlighted in red.

section.para: In most cases, you will find it easiest to select the range of cells you wish to apply the conditional format to before you add the rule. Once the range of cells is selected, you will select the appropriate conditional formatting tool to define how the conditional formatting will be applied to the range. In this section of the lesson, we will discuss how to apply conditional formatting to change the text and background fill color of the cells in a range based on the values of those cells. In the final two sections of the lesson, we will demonstrate how to add conditional formatting with color bars, icons, and symbols as well as manage the conditional formatting rules in the worksheet.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Target Values
section.para: The first set of conditional formatting rules that we will demonstrate in this lesson is based on predetermined target values. To access these rules, we navigate to the Home menu tab, click on Conditional Formatting, and select Highlight Cells Rules. We can base conditional formatting rules on target values that are numbers, text, and dates. Rules based on target numbers can change the format of cells with values that are “Greater Than” or “Less Than” the target value, “Between” two target values, or “Equal To” the target value. Rules based on target text will change the format of cells that contain specific text. Rules based on dates change the format of cells that occur within a specified proximity of a target date.

The first set of conditional formatting rules that we will demonstrate in this lesson are based on predetermined target values. To access these rules, we navigate to the Home menu tab, click on Conditional Formatting, and select Highlight Cell Rules. We can base conditional formatting rules on target values that are numbers, text, and dates. Rules based on target numbers can change the format of cells with values that are “Greater Than” or “Less Than” the target value, “Between” two target values, or “Equal To” the target value. Rules based on target text will change the format of cells that contain specific text. Rules based on dates change the format of cells that occur within a specified proximity of a target date.

The first set of conditional formatting rules that we will demonstrate in this lesson are based on predetermined target values. To access these rules, we navigate to the Home menu tab, click on Conditional Formatting, and select Highlight Cell Rules. We can base conditional formatting rules on target values that are numbers, text, and dates. Rules based on target numbers can change the format of cells with values that are “Greater Than” or “Less Than” the target value, “Between” two target values, or “Equal to” the target value. Rules based on target text will change the format of cells that contain specific text. Rules based on dates change the format of cells that occur within a specified proximity of a target date.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Target Numbers
section.para: It’s time to work through a few examples. We will work with daily sales data to examine whether our company is achieving sales targets. Suppose we want to highlight those days with unusually high sales of more than 110 units sold. We will create a rule to change the text and background fill color of the cells with values greater than 110 to green. To do this, we first highlight the range that includes the number of units sold. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting, then to Highlight Cells Rules, and then we select the Greater Than… option (see ).

It’s time to work through a few examples. We will work with daily sales data to examine whether our company is achieving sales targets. Suppose we want to highlight those days with unusually high sales of more than 110 units sold. We will create a rule to change the text and background fill color of the cells with values greater than 110 to green. To do this, we first highlight the range that includes the number of units sold. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting then Highlight Cell Rules to select the Greater Than… option (see ).

Highlight Cells Rules dropdown with Greater Than option highlighted.
Highlight Cell Rules dropdown with Greater Than option highlighted.

section.para: This will open the Greater Than dialog box already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain values that are greater than the target value (see ). To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to 110 and select the appropriate value for the “with” drop-down list.

This will open the Conditional Formatting window with Greater Than... already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain values that are greater than the target value (see ). To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to 110 and select the appropriate value for the Format with drop-down list.

This will open the New Formatting Rule wizard already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain values that are greater than the target value (see ). Notice that the following options have been selected: “Format only cells that contain”, “Cell Value”, and “greater than”. The combination of these options will highlight cells that contain values that are greater than the target value. To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to 110 and select the appropriate value for the Format with: drop-down list.

Once the rule is applied, the cells with the value for units sold that exceed 110 are formatted with green text and background fill color. All other cells retain the original formatting with black text and no background fill color. The results of this formatting rule are found in .

We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cells Rules to select the Less Than… option (see ).

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Target Text
section.para: Up to this point in the lesson, we have used conditional formatting to highlight cells according to a numeric target value. Conditional formatting can also be used to highlight cells based on text found in the cell. In our daily sales example, we have included a column that displays “Yes” for each day that the sales target is met and “No” when the target is not met. Suppose we want to highlight those days when the daily sales have been met. We will create a rule to change the text and background fill color of the cells with the text “Yes” to yellow. To do this, we first highlight the range that identifies if the target is met. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cells Rules to select the Text that Contains… option (see ).

figure.title: Text that Contains... Conditional Formatting Tools.

imagedata.alt: Highlight Cells Rules dropdown with Text that Contains highlighted.

Highlight Cells Rules dropdown with Text that Contains highlighted.

section.para: This will open the Text That Contains dialog box already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain specified text (see ). Notice that the following options have been selected: “Format only cells that contain”, “Specific Text”, and “containing”. The combination of these options will highlight cells that contain the specified text. To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to “Yes” and select the appropriate value for the “with” drop-down list.

This will open the New Formatting Rule wizard already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain specified text (see ). Notice that the following options have been selected: “Format only cells that contain”, “Specific Text”, and “containing”. The combination of these options will highlight cells that contain the specified text. To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to “Yes” and select the appropriate value for the Format with: drop-down list.

This will open the Conditional Formatting window already configured to create a formatting rule to highlight cells that contain specified text (see ). Notice that the following options have been selected: “Format only cells that contain”, “Specific Text”, and “containing”. The combination of these options will highlight cells that contain the specified text. To complete the rule, we need to set the target value to “Yes” and select the appropriate value for the Format with: drop-down list.

Once the rule is applied, the cells with the text “Yes” are formatted with yellow text and background fill color. All other cells retain the original formatting with black text and no background fill color. The results of this formatting rule are found in . It is interesting to note that this will highlight all cells that contain the text “Yes” even if the cell contains more than just the text “Yes”. As long as the specified text is found in the cell, the cell will be highlighted.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Specific Date
section.para: We can also apply conditional formatting to highlight cells based on dates. The first column in our daily sales example has the date the sales occurred. Suppose we want to highlight those days that occurred in the last week. We will create a rule to place a red border around the cells with dates that occurred last week. To do this, we first highlight the range that contains the dates. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cells Rules to select the A Date Occurring… option (see ).

We can also apply conditional formatting to highlight cells based on dates. The first column in our daily sales example has the date the sales occurred. Suppose we want to highlight those days that occurred in the last week. We will create a rule to place a red border around the cells with dates that occurred last week. To do this, we first highlight the range that contains the dates. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cell Rules to select the A Date Occurring… option (see ).

To complete the rule, we need to set the appropriate value for the “with” drop-down list.

section.para: Notice that this conditional formatting rule is different than those we have seen up to this point in the lesson. The cells highlighted by this rule will change over time because the dates that occurred within the last week will change over time. If today’s date were September 26th (see ), then only those dates that occurred during the last week (between September 19th and 25th) would be highlighted. With the passing of each day, the cells highlighted by this rule will change.

We can also configure rules that highlight dates that occur in the past, present, or future. The list of options can be seen in .

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Specific Dates
section.para: The final conditional formatting rule based on specific values in cells is the duplicate values rule. As the name implies, conditional formatting based on duplicate values will highlight cells within a range that are duplicated. summarizes a list of individual sales that all occurred on the same day, including the customer identifier, the transaction number and the amount of the transaction.

figure.title: Sales Occurring on the Same Day.

imagedata.alt: List of sales that occur on the same day. List includes the customer id, transaction number and the amount.

section.para: You are curious to see how often customers complete more than one sale on the same day. We can use conditional formatting to highlight the cells in the range of customer IDs that contain duplicate values. We will highlight these customer IDs with green text and background fill. To do this, we first highlight the range that contains the CustomerID. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cells Rules to select the Duplicate Values… option (see ).

You are curious to see how often customers complete more than one sale on the same day. We can use conditional formatting to highlight the cells in the range of customer IDs that contain duplicate values. We will highlight these customer IDs with green text and background fill. To do this we first highlight the range that contains the CustomerID. We then navigate to Conditional Formatting and then Highlight Cell Rules to select the Duplicate Values… option (see ).
