Duplicate Values dialog box with the green fill and green text option displayed.
Duplicate Values dialog box with the green fill and green text option displayed.

section.para: The result from applying this formatting rule is displayed in . The highlighting rule identifies the cells that contain values that are repeated within the range of cells. In this case, there are two pairs of repeated values. In other words, there are two customers who both completed two sales on the same day. All four cells with the repeat customer IDs are highlighted by the rule.

figure.title: Result of Duplicate Values Format Rule.

imagedata.alt: Duplicate values displayed with green background and green text. Red arrow pointing to two 4116 customer records and blue arrows pointing the two 5466 customers records.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Exceptional Values
section.para: In the last section, we discussed conditional formatting rules based on specific values in cells. Except for the Duplicate Values rule, all the conditional formatting we have discussed is used to highlight cells based solely on the values in those cells regardless of the values found in the other cells in the range. In this section, we will describe how to apply conditional formatting rules that highlight comparisons in the values found among the cells in a range.

Rules based on exceptional values, such as the 10 largest values within a range, are defined using the Top/Bottom Rules of the conditional formatting set of tools. These rules are dynamic. As the cell values within the range change, the cells containing the exceptional values may also change. When this happens, the cells that are conditionally formatted within that range will also change to reflect the cells that currently contain the exceptional values.

imagedata.alt: Top/Bottom rules dropdown menu.
Top/Bottom rules dropdown menu.
Top/Bottom rules dropdown menu.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on a Specified Number of Exceptional Values
section.para: We can create conditional formatting to highlight the cells containing the largest or smallest values in a range. The number of cells formatted can be customized. lists salespeople, the location of their sales office, and monthly sales totals for each salesperson from January to June.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with monthly sales records for sales people in different locations for the months of January through June.

section.para: Suppose we’d like to know who the top 10 salespeople are in the month of January. We can use conditional formatting to highlight the cells with the 10 largest sales amounts for the month of January with a green background and green text. To do this, we first select the range of cells for the January sales totals. We then select Conditional Formatting and Top 10 Items… from the Home menu tab (see ).

imagedata.alt: Top/Bottom rules dropdown with the Top 10 items option highlighted.
Top/Bottom rules dropdown with the Top 10 items option highlighted.
Top/Bottom rules dropdown with the Top 10 items option highlighted.

section.para: This will open the Top 10% dialog box configured to create a top 10 conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule, we ensure that the Top option is selected. We also make sure that we will highlight “10” cells. We then select the appropriate “with” option for our rule.

This will open the New Formatting Rule wizard configured to create a top 10 conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule, we ensure that the “Format only top or bottom ranked values” and “Top” options are selected. We also make sure that we will highlight “10” cells. We then select the appropriate Format with: option for our rule.

This will open the Conditional Formatting window configured to create a top 10 conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule, we ensure that the “Top” option is selected. We also make sure that we will highlight “10” cells. We then select the appropriate Format with: option for our rule.

We could just as easily make a Bottom 10 Items… rule. If we wanted to highlight the cells with the smallest values, we would change the “Top” option to “Bottom.” Also, notice that the number of cells highlighted is configurable. We could easily select the number of “top” or “bottom” items we wish to highlight by changing the “10” to another number.

figure.title: New Formatting Rule to Highlight Cells Containing the Top 10 Values.

imagedata.alt: Top 10 items dialog box with 10 as the number of items and green fill and green text as the formatting.
Top 10 items dialog box with 10 as the number of items and green fill and green text as the formatting.
Top 10 items dialog box with 10 as the number of items and green fill and green text as the formatting.

section.para: shows the results of the sales table with the top 10 sales values highlighted for the month of January. This is a dynamic rule. If the sales values for January were to be edited so that different cells contained the top 10 sales totals, those new top cells would be automatically highlighted.

figure.title: Result of Top 10 Items... Format Rule.

imagedata.alt: Results of top 10 rule applied to data in the January column.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on a Specified Percentage of Exceptional Values
section.para: We may also want to highlight the cells that contain the top or bottom percentage of values. For example, we may wish to highlight the bottom 25% of sales totals for the month of February with a red background and text color. To do this, we first select the range of cells for the February sales totals. We then select Conditional Formatting and Bottom 10 %… from the Home menu tab (see ).

imagedata.alt: Top/Bottom rule dropdown with Bottom 10% highlighted
Top/Bottom rule dropdown with Bottom 10% highlighted
Top/Bottom rule dropdown with Bottom 10% highlighted

section.para: This will open the Bottom 10% dialog box configured to create a bottom % conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule (to highlight the bottom 25% of values), we make sure that we will highlight “25” cells. We then select the appropriate “% with” option for our rule.

This will open the New Formatting Rule wizard configured to create a bottom % conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule (to highlight the bottom 25% of values), we ensure that the “Format only top or bottom ranked values” and “Bottom” options are selected. We also make sure that we will highlight “25” cells and that the percent box is checked. We then select the appropriate Format with: option for our rule. As before, we could just as easily make a Top 10 %… rule. If we wanted to highlight the desired percent of cells with the largest values, we would change the “Bottom” option to “Top.”

This will open the Conditional Formatting window configured to create a bottom % conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule (to highlight the bottom 25% of values), we ensure that the “Bottom” option is selected. We also make sure that we will highlight “25” cells and that the percent box is checked. We then select the appropriate Format with: option for our rule. As before, we could just as easily make a Top 10 %… rule. If we wanted to highlight the desired percent of cells with the largest values, we would change the “Bottom” option to “Top.”

figure.title: New Formatting Rule to Highlight Cells Containing the Bottom 25% of Values.

imagedata.alt: Bottom 10% dialog box configured to create a bottom 25% formatting.
Bottom 10% dialog box configured to create a bottom 25% formatting.
Bottom 10% dialog box configured to create a bottom 25% formatting.

section.para: shows the results of the sales table with the bottom 25% of sales values highlighted for the month of February. This rule is also dynamic. If the sales values for February were to be edited so that different cells contained the bottom 25% of sales totals, those new cells would be automatically highlighted.

imagedata.alt: Results of a bottom 25% for the values in the February column of data.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Average Values
section.para: We now turn to our final set of conditional formatting rules that highlight cells based on how the values in those cells compare with the values in other cells. These rules highlight the cells that are either above or below the average cell value for that range. For example, we may wish to highlight the cells that contain the March sales totals that are above the average for the month of March with yellow text and background color. To do this, we first select the range of cells for the March sales totals. We then select Conditional Formatting and Above Average… from the Home menu tab (see ).

imagedata.alt: Top/Bottom dropdown with Above Average option highlighted.
Top/Bottom dropdown with Above Average option highlighted.
Top/Bottom dropdown with Above Average option highlighted.

section.para: This will open the Above Average dialog box configured to create an above-average conditional formatting rule. We need to select the appropriate “for the selected range with” option for our rule.

This will open the New Formatting Rule wizard configured to create an above-average conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule, we ensure that the “Format only values that are above or below average” and “Above” options are selected. We then select the appropriate Format with: option for our rule. As before, we could just as easily make a Below Average… rule. If we wanted to highlight cells with below-average values, we would change the “Above” option to “Below.”

This will open the Conditional Formatting window configured to create an above-average conditional formatting rule. To create this new rule, we ensure that the “Above” option is selected. We then select the appropriate Format with option for our rule. As before, we could just as easily make a Below Average… rule. If we wanted to highlight cells with below-average values, we would change the “Above” option to “Below.”

figure.title: New Formatting Rule to Highlight Cells Containing the Above Average Values.

imagedata.alt: Above Average dialog box with the yellow fill and yellow text option selected.
Above Average dialog box with the yellow fill and yellow text option selected.
Above Average dialog box with the yellow fill and yellow text option selected.

section.para: shows the results of the sales table with the above-average sales values highlighted for the month of March. This rule is also dynamic. If the sales values for March were to be edited so that different cells contained the above-average sales totals, those new cells would be automatically highlighted.

figure.title: Result of Above Average... Format Rule.

imagedata.alt: Results of above average values in March formatted with yellow background and yellow text.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Based on Exceptional Values Practice Problems
More Advanced Conditional Formatting Options
section.para: In this lesson, we have just begun to scratch the surface of what we can do with conditional formatting in Excel. In this last section, we will discuss how to clear and edit existing conditional formatting rules. We will also demonstrate how to create rules that use data bars, color scales, and icons as conditional formatting rather than just changing the text, border, and background color of cells.

section.title: Editing and Clearing Conditional Formatting Rules
section.para: Occasionally, we want to remove a conditional formatting rule that we have applied to a range. We can either clear a formatting rule from a specific range of cells or clear all the rules at once from an entire worksheet. To clear the conditional formatting rules from a specific range, select that range of cells. Then select Conditional Formatting, Clear Rules, and Clear Rules from Selected Cells (see ). This will remove the conditional formatting rules from the selected range of cells. As you likely expect, to remove all conditional formatting rules from an entire worksheet, select Conditional Formatting, Clear Rules, and Clear Rules from Entire Sheet.

informalfigure.title: Clear conditional formatting from a range of cells.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Selected Cells highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Selected Cells highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Selected Cells highlighted.

informalfigure.title: Clear conditional formatting from an entire worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Entire Sheet highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Entire Sheet highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Clear Rules from Entire Sheet highlighted.

section.para: We may also need to edit an existing conditional formatting rule. For example, we may wish to change the “Bottom 25%” rule we created in the last section to format only the bottom 20% of values. To edit a conditional formatting rule, it is generally easiest if we first select the range of cells that contains the rule we wish to edit. We then select Conditional Formatting and Manage Rules….

figure.title: Editing an Existing Conditional Formatting Rule.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dropdown with Manage Rules highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Manage Rules highlighted.
Conditional Formatting dropdown with Manage Rules highlighted.

section.para: This will open the Conditioanal Formatting Rule Manager dialog window. If we have selected the range of cells that contain the rule we wish to edit, we can use the Current Selection option associated with Show formatting rules for: to easily find and select the rule we wish to edit. If we did not preselect the range, we can still identify the appropriate rule using the This Worksheet option for Show formatting rules for:. Once we have identified and selected the rule we wish to edit, we click on Edit Rule.

This will open the Manage Rules dialog window. If we have selected the range of cells that contain the rule we wish to edit, we can use the Current Selection option associated with Show formatting rules for: to easily find and select the rule we wish to edit. If we did not preselect the range, we can still identify the appropriate rule using the This Worksheet option for Show formatting rules for:. Once we have identified and selected the rule we wish to edit, we click on Edit Rule.

This will open the Conditional Formatting window. If we have selected the range of cells that contain the rule we wish to edit, we can use the Manage Rules in selection option to easily find and select the rule we wish to edit. If we did not preselect the range, we can still identify the appropriate rule using the Manage Rules in this sheet option. Once we have identified and selected the rule we wish to edit, we click on Edit Rule.

informalfigure.title: Identify the rule to edit using the current selection.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting Rules manager with rule displayed for current selection.
Manage Rules manager with rule displayed for current selection.
Conditional Formatting Rules manager with rule displayed for current selection.

informalfigure.title: Identify the rule to edit using this worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting Rules manager with rule displayed for this worksheet.
Manage Rules manager with rule displayed for this worksheet.
Conditional Formatting Rules manager with rule displayed for this worksheet.
Edit icon highlighted in red.
Edit icon highlighted in red.
Edit icon highlighted in red.

section.para: After clicking Edit Rule, the Edit Formatting Rule wizard opens. This wizard will present the same options that were available when we first created the rule. In this case, we wish to change the rule to highlight the bottom 20% of values rather than the bottom 25% of values. Once we have made this change, we click OK once to close the Edit Formatting Rule window and again to close the Manage Rules window.

After clicking Edit Rule, the Conditional Formatting window opens. This window shows the options that were available when we first created the rule. In this case, we wish to change the rule to highlight the bottom 20% of values rather than the bottom 25% of values. Once we have made this change, we click Done and close the Conditional Formatting window.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting dialog box with Bottom percent rule selected.
Manage Formatting dialog box with Bottom percent rule selected.
Conditional Formatting dialog box with Bottom percent rule selected.

section.para: This will edit the rule to highlight only the cells that contain the bottom 20% of values. Notice that you can edit elements of the conditional formatting rule, including the formatting style that will be applied to the rule. As you become comfortable using conditional formatting, you will likely use the editing tools to create more complex or customized rules.

We now turn our attention to conditional formatting that uses images and sophisticated color schemes to highlight cells. In particular, we will discuss data bars, color scales, and icon sets. These options highlight cell values as they compare to the other values in the selected range. To demonstrate how to use these rules, we will use the data presented in . The data summarize total sales by location for January through June. The data also include sales projections for each location in July, August, and September.

imagedata.Alt: Data that summarizes total sales by location for January through June. Projected data for July through September is also displayed in the table.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Using Data Bars
section.para: First, we will examine conditional formatting that uses data bars. This option highlights the individual cells in a range with a colored bar, much like a bar chart. The length of each of the bars in the cells corresponds with the relative size of the value in the cell. The cell with the largest value will have the longest colored bar. The cell with the smallest value will have the shortest colored bar. The length of the other bars will be directly proportional to the relative size of the value in that cell.

For example, we could apply data bars conditional formatting to the sales projections for July. To do this, we select the range for the July projections, click on Conditional Formatting, select Data Bars, and choose a color for the data bars. We will choose the green option in the Gradient Fill section for this example. This will apply data bars conditional formatting to the July projections.

figure.title: Add Data Bars Conditional Formatting to the July Projections.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting menu with Data Bars highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with Data Bars highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with Data Bars highlighted.

section.para: presents the results of applying data bars to the July projections. Notice that the largest projection is $4,587 in Toledo, OH. The data bar for this cell is also the longest. The smallest value is $1,168 for Nashville, TN. The data bar for this cell is the shortest. Each of the other data bars has different lengths. The length of each bar corresponds with the relative size of the value in each cell.

figure.title: Data Bars Applied to the July Projections.

imagedata.alt: Results of green data bars being applied to the July projected data.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Using Color Scales
section.para: Conditional formatting with color scales applies a different background color for each cell based on the relative size of the value in a cell. For example, let’s apply color scale conditional formatting to the August projections. Specifically, we will use a three-color scale (green, white, and red). The top values in the range will be formatted with a green color. The bottom values in the range will be formatted with a red color. The middle values will be formatted with a white background.

One of the hallmarks of the color scale formatting is that the intensity of the colors is also used to highlight differences in values. Specifically, the top values will all be green. However, the highest values will use the most intense green. Moreover, the bottom values will all be formatted with a red background. The lowest values will have the most intense red background.

To apply color scale formatting to the August projections, we first select the range of cells that contain the projections. We then select Conditional Formatting, Color Scales, and a color scheme for the rule. We will use the green, white, and red option for this example.

figure.title: Add Color Scales Conditional Formatting to the August Projections.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting menu with the Color Scales option highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with the Color Scales option highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with the Color Scales option highlighted.

section.para: Once the color scales formatting is applied, the table will appear as in . Notice that the cells with the top four values are all formatted with a green background. The most intense green background is applied to the cell with the Toledo, OH sales, which also has the largest value. The cells with the smallest four values have a red background. The cell with the most intense red hue corresponds to Nashville, TN, and contains the smallest value. The middle values are all formatted with a white background.

figure.title: Color Scales Applied to August Projections.

imagedata.alt: Results of table with August projected data displayed with a white, green, red color scale.

section.para: Although it is beyond the focus of this lesson, we can edit the options of the color scales formatting rule. This would allow us to define precisely how the background coloring of the cells will be determined. The figure below shows the options available when editing the color scales formatting rule.

imagedata.alt: Edit option for the Conditional Formatting color scales.
Edit option for the Conditional Formatting color scales.

section.title: Conditional Formatting Using Icon Sets
section.para: We can also use conditional formatting to add icons to the cells in a range to describe the values in the cells. There are a number of sets of icons that can be applied to a cell using conditional formatting (see ). For this example, let’s apply colored arrow icons to the September projections. The largest values will be formatted with an upward-pointing green arrow. The smallest values will be formatted with a downward-pointing red arrow. The middle values will be formatted with a side-pointing yellow arrow.

To create the colored arrow formatting rule, we first highlight the range of cells with the September projections. Then, we choose Conditional Formatting and Icon Sets and select from among the options for icon sets. In our example, we will use colored directional arrows.

figure.title: Icon Sets Conditional Formatting Applied to September Projections.

imagedata.alt: Conditional Formatting menu with the Icon Set option highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with the Icon Set option highlighted.
Conditional Formatting menu with the Icon Set option highlighted.

section.para: With the conditional formatting applied, the table will appear as in . Notice that the cells with the top values contain an upward-pointing green arrow. The cells with the bottom values contain a downward-pointing red arrow. The cells with the middle values contain a side-pointing yellow arrow.

figure.title: Icon Set Applied to September Projections.

imagedata.alt: Results of icon sets arrows being applied to the September data in the table.

section.para: It is interesting that only the top two cells contain an upward-facing green arrow while there are six cells that contain a downward-facing red arrow. To understand how the placement of the arrows is determined, we must edit the icon sets rule. The figure below shows the options available for editing our icon sets rule.

We can edit all aspects of the icon sets rule, including changing the icons used in the formatting rule. Of particular interest for our discussion are the Display options. The Display options are used to determine which icons will be used to format each of the cells. In our example, those cells with values that are greater than or equal to 67% of the values in the range will receive an upward-pointing green arrow. Those cells with values that are less than 33% of the values in the range will receive a downward-pointing red arrow. The cells with values that are greater than or equal to 33% and less than 67% of the values in the range will receive a side-pointing yellow arrow. We can edit the display options to change the cells that receive each of the icons.

In this lesson, we have discussed how to use conditional formatting tools to automatically adjust the appearance of cells based on the values contained in those cells. Conditional formatting provides a powerful and dynamic way to highlight exceptional values for spreadsheet users. Using conditional formatting will help users to identify patterns or trends in their data that may be difficult to find in other ways.

section.title: Practice with More Advanced Conditional Formatting
Building Spreadsheet Models

listitem.para: Be able to build a spreadsheet model.
Become familiar with the different what-if analysis tools in Excel.
Be able to analyze spreadsheet models using what-if analyses.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: One of the most powerful things you will do with spreadsheets is model real-world processes. Building models allows you to use spreadsheets to make good decisions based on sophisticated and relevant analyses of the problems you are trying to solve. Models can be very useful for decision-making in many different settings. Once you are able to build spreadsheet models of a process, you will likely find that Excel is one of the first tools you will use in your own problem-solving.

Learning how to build spreadsheet models takes practice. You must have a basic understanding of how spreadsheets work and how to use the formulas and functions necessary to complete the calculations in the model. Additionally, you will need problem-solving skills as you identify how the process you are modeling works and capture the logic of that process in your model.

section.title: What Are Spreadsheet Models?
section.para: A model is a physical representation of a larger or more complicated thing or idea. A spreadsheet model A model that represents the logic of a more complicated logical process through functions and calculations on a spreadsheet. is generally used to represent the logic of a more complicated logical process. Just as a good model contains enough detail to accurately depict the thing or idea it represents, a good spreadsheet model must be detailed enough to capture the salient elements of the logical process it represents. Constructing good spreadsheet models takes practice, but it can be an extremely valuable skill.

section.title: Building Blocks of Spreadsheet Models
section.para: Every good spreadsheet model will have three common elements: changeable inputs, outputs, and intermediate calculations. To illustrate each element and discuss how the elements work together to make a good model, let’s consider the following process. A loan underwriter for a mortgage company approves or rejects mortgage applications based on several criteria. One important criterion relates to the percentage of an applicant’s income that will go toward making their new mortgage payment and other existing debts. Generally, a loan application will not be approved if an applicant will need to pay more than 40% of their gross monthly income for their mortgage payment and other debt payments.

presents a spreadsheet model that represents this process and the formulas used to build the model. Notice that the model contains cells in which the user can input customer information, including annual income, information about the loan (loan amount, interest rate, and term), and information about their monthly debt payments. The model also has an area with calculations concerning the applicant’s income to determine the maximum payment an applicant can make given their gross monthly income and monthly debt payments. The model calculates the monthly payment for the mortgage given the loan amount, interest rate, and term. Finally, the model suggests that the loan be approved or not approved by comparing the projected monthly payment to the maximum payment for which an applicant can be approved.

figure.title: Spreadsheet Model of Mortgage Approval Process.

imagedata.alt: Completed model with red boxes around the difference sections of the model.
Worksheet displays the formulas used to complete the model.

section.title: Changeable Inputs
section.para: The changeable inputs to this model can be found in the customer information section. These are the elements of the model that the user will change every time a new application is processed. In general, inputs are placed at the top of a model or another place where they would be easy for the user to spot and modify. Also, it is generally a good idea to include a changeable input for any element of the process that might change, even if it is unlikely that the user will change it.

For example, the user will likely not change the “Acceptable % of Income” input in this model. The 40% limitation for monthly income is a longstanding rule in the mortgage industry. Nearly all mortgages are subject to this limitation. As a developer, you may be tempted not to include this as an input to the model (instead hard-coding 40% in cell C14 for the “Acceptable Income Servicing Debt” calculation) since it likely will not change for any application processed. Although it is unlikely that the percentage will change, if it were hard-coded in cell C14, the user would have to change the formula if this assumption were to change. If there were several calculations that depended on the percentage, each of them would also need to be changed. This step would be both tedious and unnecessary for the user. In general, any element of a model that could possibly change should be included as an input for that model.

section.title: Model Outputs
section.para: Model outputs represent the outcome of the process that is modeled in the spreadsheet. Outputs need not be a single calculation or recommendation, but they are the end-state of the model. Model outputs are often placed at the bottom of the spreadsheet model because most people work in a top-down manner for completing a calculation. This need not be the case, though model outputs should occupy a prominent place in the spreadsheet model where the user can easily identify them. In a complex spreadsheet model, this may be near the changeable inputs or on a separate summary worksheet.

In our example of the mortgage underwriter, the output is the recommendation to approve or not approve the loan. This appears at the end of the set of calculations for the model. Notice that the formatting of both the label, “Approval”, in cell B20 and the suggested action in cell C20 makes these cells stand out next to the other parts of the spreadsheet model. The output calculation in cell C20 compares the monthly loan payment to the maximum payment the applicant can make (see description of both of these elements below). If the monthly payment for the proposed loan is less than or equal to the maximum payment that the applicant can afford, then cell C20 will indicate “Yes” that the applicant should be approved. Otherwise, the model will suggest that the applicant not be approved (cell C20 will display “No”).

section.title: Intermediate Calculations
section.para: As the name implies, the “Intermediate Calculations” section of a spreadsheet model comprises the area of the worksheet where the logical steps required to complete the process are modeled. The number and sophistication of the intermediate calculations for a complex spreadsheet model can be quite significant and span multiple worksheets. It is in the intermediate calculations that the nuances of the process can be modeled effectively. The intermediate calculations should generally break the process into enough detail that the user can reconstruct the final outcomes of the model. Skipping or consolidating the steps that lead to the outcome can be problematic for the user if the user cannot determine how the final outcome is achieved.

In the mortgage underwriter example, there are several intermediate calculations:
Monthly Income (cell C13) – The monthly income is based on the annual income inputted by the user. The monthly income is the annual income divided by 12.
Acceptable Income Servicing Debt (cell C14) – The maximum dollar amount that the applicant can spend each month on debt to be approved for the loan. It is calculated by multiplying the monthly income by the acceptable percentage of monthly income allowed to go toward debt defined in the model inputs.
Monthly Debts (cell C15) – The monthly debts is included from the inputs to be used as part of the maximum payment calculation in the model.
Max Payment (cell C16) – The max payment is the total loan payment the applicant can make and still be approved for the loan. It is calculated as the acceptable income servicing debt minus the other monthly debts the applicant must pay.
Monthly Payment (cell C18) – The monthly payment is the monthly payment for the proposed loan. It is calculated using the PMT function that accounts for the loan amount, interest rate, and term of the loan as defined in the inputs section of the model.

Each of the intermediate calculations represents important elements of the approval decision. While it is possible to arrive at the final recommendation without showing each intermediate step, the user can easily see how the outcome is derived. This provides the user with enough detail to understand the outcome and see how the outcome would be different with different inputs.

section.title: Building Spreadsheet Models
section.para:
Admittedly, there is a conceptual gulf between understanding the parts of a spreadsheet model and the ability to apply this knowledge to build them on your own. The most important requirement for building an effective model is a thorough understanding of the process you are modeling. There are several things to consider when creating a new spreadsheet model:
What does the model need to accomplish?
How much detail does the model need to show?
From where does the data for the model come?
Who else might provide insight into the process being modeled?
Will others be using the spreadsheet model?
Can the spreadsheet model be divided into smaller models? (Combining the results of smaller models can sometimes be a helpful strategy for building a more complex model.)
How will you evaluate the effectiveness of the spreadsheet model once it is built?

Once you understand why and how you are going to use the model, the next step is to identify the elements of the process that you need to include in your model. One method for identifying the important elements of the model is to work backward from the outputs of the model until you work through the intermediate calculations to the changeable inputs for the model.

Using the mortgage underwriting example as a guide, we can demonstrate how working backward from outputs to inputs works. The final output for this model is a recommendation on whether to approve the application or not approve the application. After identifying this as the output, we then determine the information needed to make the recommendation. In this case, we need to check whether the (1) monthly payment for the proposed loan exceeds the (2) maximum allowable mortgage payment for the applicant. Both of these are intermediate calculations that require information from another element of the model.

The monthly payment calculation requires information from the user (loan amount, interest rate, and the term of the loan). This information would be modeled as a changeable input that the user can modify.

The maximum allowable mortgage payment for the applicant is a function of the acceptable income servicing debt for the applicant and other monthly debts paid by the applicant. The monthly debt payment made by the applicant is another changeable input that can be modified by the user. The level of acceptable income used to service monthly debt is an intermediate calculation that relies on the monthly income of the applicant and the acceptable % of income allowed by the underwriter.

This process would continue until all of the outputs, intermediate calculations, and changeable inputs are identified. You would stop once all elements of the model are identified and incorporated into your spreadsheet model.

section.title: Using Multiple Worksheets

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: At times it becomes necessary to build large spreadsheet models. Large spreadsheet models can span multiple worksheets and become quite complex. When this is the case, it becomes necessary to create references that also span across multiple worksheets. In this lesson, we describe how to work with spreadsheet models that span across worksheets. We also describe the process for assigning names to individual cells or ranges of cells to simplify cell references.

section.title: Creating Cell References that Span across Worksheets
section.para: There are several reasons why you may choose to use multiple worksheets in your spreadsheet models. For example, a large model may be decomposed into a few smaller models, each of which can be easily put on separate worksheets. The combined results of these parts can be displayed on a summary worksheet. This approach simplifies the presentation of the overall spreadsheet model and provides a way to include all of the complexity necessary for the large model to be meaningful and accurate.

An example of such a model would be conducting a financial analysis of a company. This analysis would rely on calculating a series of financial ratios based on the various financial statements (e.g., Balance Sheet, Statement of Cash Flows, etc.) published in the annual reports of the company. Each of the financial statements could be included on a separate worksheet to simplify the presentation of the analysis. A summary worksheet would then be created where the financial ratios can be calculated to complete the analysis.

Regardless of your reasons for using multiple worksheets in your spreadsheet models, you will need to define references in your spreadsheet that also span worksheets. The good news is that there are not any limitations on the calculations or functions you can use when working with multiple worksheets. You just need to know the syntax for referencing cells from one worksheet on another worksheet.

As you are already aware, the syntax for referencing a cell on a single worksheet has two parts: the letter in the reference points to the column of the target cell and the number in the cell reference defines the row of the target cell. Therefore, the cell reference “C12” defines the cell in row 12 of column C.

When Excel encounters the cell reference “C12”, it assumes that the user is referring to the cell in row 12 of column C in the active worksheet (the worksheet that the user is currently working in). To create a reference to a cell on a different worksheet, you will need to provide the worksheet name as part of your reference. As an example, to reference cell C12 on a worksheet named “New Worksheet”, you would use the following syntax:
='New Worksheet'!C12

Notice that the syntax is quite intuitive. To signal to Excel that you are referencing a cell on the “New Worksheet” worksheet, you add that information to the front of the cell reference. Notice that the name of the worksheet appears in single quotes and that there is an exclamation point after the worksheet name. The single quotes are used because there is a space between the words of the worksheet name. Using the single quotes groups all of the text for the worksheet name together. The exclamation point tells Excel that you have finished defining the worksheet for your cell reference and are ready to provide the column and row information for the cell reference.

Once you are comfortable working with references that span worksheets, you will see that the calculations you perform across worksheets are really not much more difficult than those that you complete within a single worksheet. It’s time to look at an example (see ). Let’s suppose that you are building a spreadsheet model to complete a summary of several orders completed at a local fast food restaurant. Specifically, you want to calculate the number of orders filled, the average time it takes to complete an order, the longest order fulfillment time, and the number of orders that included ice cream (since ice cream orders tend to take a little longer). You will complete your calculations on the “Order Summary” worksheet in the workbook, but you have stored all of the transaction detail in the “Order Details” worksheet to keep the summary worksheet free from the transaction details.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with labels to describe the values that will be entered in column C.
First four rows of the table of data that will be used in the calculations

informalfigure.title: The “Order Summary” and “Order Details” Worksheet Tabs.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet tabs Order Summary and Order Details found at the bottom of the worksheet window.
Worksheet tabs Order Summary and Order Details found at the bottom of the worksheet window.
Worksheet tabs Order Summary and Order Details found at the bottom of the worksheet window.

section.para: The formulas used to complete the model are relatively straightforward. To calculate the number of orders filled, we will use the COUNT function on the “Prep Time” column from the table of transactions on the “Order Details” worksheet. We will use the AVERAGE function and MAX function to calculate the average and longest fulfillment times from the “Prep Time” column. Finally, we will use the COUNTIF function on the “Ice Cream” column of the table to determine how many orders included ice cream. Notice that we would use the same functions to complete these calculations if the transaction details were listed on the “Order Summary” worksheet. The only difference will be how we reference the transactions for completing our calculations.

includes both the completed calculations and the formulas used to complete the calculations. Notice the cell references in the formulas. When defining ranges as arguments for functions, you can type the reference directly in the cells, you can use your mouse to select the ranges you want to reference, or you can use the function wizard just as you would to define a range within a single sheet. When you use the mouse or wizard to select cells to reference, Excel will automatically insert the necessary worksheet syntax to complete your reference.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with the completed fast food model calculations.

informalfigure.title: Formulas used to complete the Fast Food Calculations.

imagedata.alt: Show formula option used to display the functions used to complete the fast food model.

section.para: As you can see, the process for working with models that span worksheets is not much more difficult than working with models on a single worksheet. The only difference is that your cell references must account for both the cell you want to reference and the worksheet that it is on. Of course, as the size of your spreadsheet models increases, so likely does the complexity of your models.

section.title: Introduction
section.para: One of the most valuable aspects of using a spreadsheet model in decision-making is that once a model is created, it can be reused to support making similar decisions. Often a well-built model is shared with colleagues who also repeatedly use the model. When a spreadsheet model is shared with others, care should be taken to ensure that other users know how to operate the model correctly and effectively.

In the first section of this lesson, we described the elements of a spreadsheet model: changeable inputs, model outputs, and intermediate calculations. When sharing a spreadsheet model, a brief description of each is generally necessary for others to know how to use the model. In particular, users must understand the inputs to the model that are needed for the model to process correctly.

Even with this understanding, users can mistakenly provide incorrect inputs. If users provide improper inputs to a spreadsheet model, they should have no hope of that model producing outputs that will be helpful for decision-making. Thankfully, there is a set of tools in Excel that we can use to help to ensure that the information entered into a spreadsheet model fits with the expectations of the creator of that model. Collectively, we label these tools as Data Validation.

In this section, we will discuss the data validation features of Excel. We will also highlight how we can use data validation to help ensure that the inputs users provide when using our spreadsheet models match what we intended. To illustrate how to implement data validation into a spreadsheet model, we will use the model in .

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with a model to calculate a Car Loan Payment.

section.para: The model in might be used to help a car salesperson highlight the terms of a car loan to a prospective customer. This model requires several inputs from the salesperson. The salesperson must enter the current date and time and an appropriate expiration date for the offer. The salesperson must also enter the customer’s name and credit score. Finally, the salesperson will enter the amount of the loan, the annual interest rate of the loan, and the number of payments to be made by the customer. The model will calculate the monthly payment of the loan and disclose to the customer the total of the payments made and interest paid over the life of the loan.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.title: Elements of Data Validation
section.para: The data validation tools in Excel are found in the Data menu tab and the Data Validation set of menu options (see ).
The data validation tools in Excel are found in the Data menu tab and the Data Validation menu option (see ).

imagedata.alt: Data Validation option highlighted in Red on the Data menu.
Data Validation option highlighted in Red on the Data menu.
Data Validation option highlighted in Red on the Data menu.

section.para: There are three common elements to the data validation options we can use in Excel: Settings, Input Messages, and Error Alerts. The settings are the restrictions we place on the data that can be entered into a specific cell. For example, we may set a cell to only accept integer (whole number) data. Once set, Excel will prevent a user from entering anything else into that cell. If a user tries to enter text (any non-number) or even a number with digits to the right of the decimal (like 3.14), Excel will reject that entry.

Input messages and error alerts communicate how the settings are configured to the user. An input message becomes visible to users when they select a cell with data validation applied. This message explains to the user what will be allowed in that cell. An error alert appears in a warning window if a user attempts to enter data that is not permitted in a cell. We can control what the input messages and error alerts say to users.

section.title: Settings
section.para: Data validation settings are used to restrict what can be entered into a cell to fit certain characteristics. These characteristics can include the type of data that are entered and the range of values that are allowed. These elements can be combined to create customized checks on the inputs used in a spreadsheet model. We configure data validation settings by selecting Data then Data Validation. This will invoke the Data Validation wizard shown in . Settings are applied using the Settings tab of this wizard.

imagedata.alt: Data Validation settings tab.
Data Validation settings tab.
Data Validation settings tab.

section.para: Excel offers flexibility with how restrictions are made to the types of data that will be allowed in a cell (see ). A cell can be set to allow only whole numbers, decimal numbers, predefined values selected from a drop-down list, a date, a time of the day (e.g., 1:15 PM), or text of a certain length. We can even use Excel formulas to create custom data validation.

imagedata.alt: Allow dropdown on the Data Validation settings tab.
Allow dropdown on the Data Validation settings tab.
Allow dropdown on the Data Validation settings tab.

section.para: When setting data validation in a cell, we can also create restrictions on the range of values that will be allowed in a cell. For example, in our car loan example, the salesperson must enter the FICO (credit) score of the customer. The range for these scores is 300 to 850. We can set data validation to allow only a number that is greater than or equal to 300 and less than or equal to 850 to be entered into a cell. We will discuss how to configure data validation settings in the next section.

section.title: Input Message
section.para: The input message is used to describe to the user the values that are allowed in a cell with data validation. It is displayed when a user selects the cell. To add an input message to a cell, we first add data validation. Click on Data and Data Validation. This will open the Data Validation Wizard. To define the input message, select the Input Message tab. Ensure that the Show input message when cell is selected box is checked. An input message is comprised of both a title and the message. For this example, we will use “Input Message Title” as the title of the message and “Input message text” as the message text ().

informalfigure.title: Adding an input message to a cell.

imagedata.alt: Input Message tab on the Data Validation dialog box.
Input Message tab on the Data Validation dialog box.
Input Message tab on the Data Validation dialog box.
Worksheet with date cell displaying the input message for the Data Validation
Worksheet with date cell displaying the input message for the Data Validation

section.para: Notice the appearance of the input message when the cell is selected. The title appears at the top of the message in bold. The message is displayed below the title. When the user selects a different cell, the input message is no longer displayed.

section.title: Error Alert
section.para: An error alert is a message that appears in a new window when the user attempts to enter data that is inconsistent with the data validation selected for a cell. We add an error alert using the Data Validation wizard in the Error Alert tab. Make sure that the Show error alert after invalid data is entered box is checked. Just like the input message, we can define an error alert title and message. For this example, we will use “Error Title” as the error alert title and “Error message text” as the text of the alert (see ).

An error alert is a message that appears in a new window when the user attempts to enter data that is inconsistent with the data validation selected for a cell. We add an error alert using the Data Validation wizard in the Error Alert tab. Make sure that the Show Alert box is checked. Just like the input message, we can define an error alert title and message. For this example, we will use “Error Title” as the error alert title and “Error message text” as the text of the alert (see ).

figure.title: Adding an Error Alert to Data Validation.

imagedata.alt: Data Validation Error Alert tab.
Data Validation Error Alert tab.
Data Validation Error Alert tab.

section.para: Notice that there is also an option for Style. Style refers to the strictness that Excel will use when enforcing the data validation. There are three strictness levels for enforcing data validation. highlights these options and the dialog windows displayed to the user with each option.

Notice that there is also an option for Type. Type refers to the strictness that Excel will use when enforcing the data validation. There are two strictness levels for enforcing data validation. highlights these options and the dialog windows displayed to the user with each option.

The strictest option is “Stop”. When the stop style is selected and a user attempts to enter invalid data, the error alert is displayed in a dialog window with the option to “Retry” or “Cancel”. If the user selects “Retry”, they will be prompted to immediately edit the invalid entry. If the user selects “Cancel”, the invalid entry is removed. In either case, Excel will prevent the invalid entry.

The strictest option is “Blocking”. When the blocking type is selected and a user attempts to enter invalid data, the error alert is displayed in a dialog window with the option to “Retry” or “Cancel”. If the user selects “Retry”, they will be prompted to immediately edit the invalid entry. If the user selects “Cancel”, the invalid entry is removed. In either case, Excel will prevent the invalid entry.

figure.title: The Setting of an Error Alert.

informalfigure.title: Error Alert configured with the “Stop” style setting.

imagedata.alt: Data Validation Error Alert tab configured to create a Stop error message for incorrect input.
Data Validation Error Alert tab configured to create a Stop error message for incorrect input.

informalfigure.title: Error Alert configured with the “Blocking” style setting.

imagedata.alt: Data Validation Error Alert tab configured to create a Block error message for incorrect input.
Stop error alert dialog box.
Stop error alert dialog box.
Block error alert dialog box.

section.para: The style next option is “Warning”. If the user attempts to enter invalid data and the “Warning” option is selected, the user is presented with the error alert in a “Yes/No” dialog window. If the user selects “Yes”, Excel allows the invalid data to be entered in the cell. If the user selects “No”, the invalid data is removed from the cell. This type of alert works well when you just want to send a message to the user to confirm that they may have just entered invalid data.

The type next option is “Warning”. If the user attempts to enter invalid data and the “Warning” option is selected, the user is presented with the error alert in a “Yes/No” dialog window. If the user selects “Yes”, Excel allows the invalid data to be entered in the cell. If the user selects “No”, the invalid data is removed from the cell. This type of alert works well when you just want to send a message to the user to confirm that they may have just entered invalid data.

figure.title: The Setting of an Error Alert.

informalfigure.title: Error Alert configured with the “Warning” style setting.

imagedata.alt: Error Alert tab of the Data Validation dialog box configured for a warning error.
Error Alert tab of the Data Validation dialog box configured for a warning error.
Error Alert tab of the Data Validation dialog box configured for a warning error.
Warning error dialog box.
Warning error dialog box.
Warning error dialog box.

section.para: The final style option is “Information”. If the user attempts to add invalid data when the “Information” option is selected, they will be presented with the error alert that can be dismissed with an OK button. When the user dismisses the error alert, the invalid data is recorded in the cell.

section.title: Removing Data Validation
section.para: Adding data validation can be a powerful tool to help ensure that the spreadsheet models you create are used as you designed them to be. However, you may at some point want to remove the data validation you have applied to a cell or cells. To remove data validation from a range of cells, you first select the range. Select the Data tab and Data Validation icon. If you have selected a range of cells with different forms of data validation applied, you may be prompted with a warning message (see Figure 16). Select OK to continue. The Data Validation window is opened. To remove the data validation, click Clear All. This will remove all the data validation in the range.

figure.title: Removing Data Validation from a Range.

informalfigure.title: Warning if range with multiple types of validation are selected.

Warning dialog box if the selected range has multiple data validation set.
Warning dialog box if the selected range has multiple data validation set.
Clear all option highlighted in red used to clear all the data validation from selected cells.
