section.title: Determining Model Inputs

listitem.para: Know how to gather process information.
Be able to estimate inputs in a variety of ways.
Be able to use statistical estimates as model inputs.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: One of the most difficult parts of doing effective analysis in Excel is getting the inputs to your spreadsheet models correct. This relates to the famous expression “garbage in, garbage out,” which means that even if you are completing the right calculation, you will not get the right results if the data in your calculation are incorrect. The same is true of the models you build in Excel. Even if you have good models with correct calculations, you cannot make good decisions using those models if the data you are analyzing are incorrect.

Getting model inputs correct often requires you to sample the process you are modeling. Using sample information, you can estimate appropriate values for your model inputs. In this lesson, we will discuss what it means to gather sample data and use that data to approximate appropriate values for your spreadsheet models.

section.title: Gathering Process Information
section.para: The key to understanding how to gather data about a process is the distinction between a populationThe complete set of entities for an item of interest. and a sample A part of a population or set of entities. . In statistical terms, a population is the complete set of entities for an item of interest. A sample is part of the population. For example, if I were interested in the height of all of the people in the United States, the population would be all people in the United States. A sample would be some subset of the population, or a smaller group of people that live in the United States.

We use samples of populations to make informed decisions about our populations, particularly when it would be impossible or too costly to gather information about the entire population. For example, we may want to know the average height of everyone in the United States. We could measure the height of everyone in the United States and calculate the average of all of these measurements. We could also measure a sample of the population (say 100 people) and use the average height of the people in our sample as an estimate of the height of the population. Of course, it would be extremely time-consuming and costly to travel everywhere in the United States and measure the height of every person.

As you can see, gathering data about a population can be much more costly than gathering information about a sample of the population. The good news is that if we build our sample correctly, it can do a pretty good job of approximating our population. The key to effective sampling is that our sample must be taken in such a way that every element of the population has the same likelihood of being included in the sample. We also want to make sure that our sample size is large enough.

section.title: Estimating Inputs with Averages, Standard Deviations, and Confidence Intervals
section.para: Once we have gathered a good population sample, we are ready to compute some statistics on our sample data to help us understand the population. The first statistic is the sample average. The sample average is calculated using the AVERAGE function in Excel. The average is a measure of the most likely or typical value for the things that we are measuring.
=AVERAGE(number1, [number2],…)

Another important piece of information we can calculate is the standard deviationA measure of how diverse the elements in our dataset are. . The standard deviation is a measure of how diverse the elements in our dataset are. We calculate the standard deviation of a sample using the STDEV.S function and the standard deviation of a population using the STDEV.P function. A large standardization implies that there is a lot of diversity in our data, while a small standard deviation means that the individual pieces of data are relatively similar.
=STDEV.S(number1, [number2],…)
=STDEV.P(number1, [number2],…)

When we calculate the average or standard deviation on a sample of data, we estimate these values for the population. This is an important point. Since we do not have all of the data from the entire population, it is impossible to calculate the true average or standard deviation of the population.

For example, you may estimate the average height for people in the United States as 5 feet 8 inches with a standard deviation of 8 inches. While this may be a good estimate of the true average height of people in the United States, we do not know if it is actually the true average height—it is very likely not the true average. In fact, we do not even know if this is a good estimate.

To examine the quality of our estimates, we construct confidence intervalsThe range of values within which we believe the “true” value for the population falls. . A confidence interval is the range of values that we believe that the true value for the population falls within. We calculate a confidence interval using the CONFIDENCE.NORM function in Excel.
=CONFIDENCE.NORM(alpha, standard_dev, size)

Alpha The level of confidence that we want to have with our estimate; it is calculated as 1, so 95% confidence is noted as .05 (or 1-95%). deals with the level of confidence that we want to have with our estimate. Alpha is actually calculated as 1 – our confidence level. So, if we want to be 95% confident in our estimate, we would set alpha to be .05 (or 1-95%). This would mean that we are willing to accept a 5% chance that the true value will fall outside our confidence interval. The standard_dev argument is the sample standard deviation for our analysis. Size represents the size of our sample. Using the CONFIDENCE.NORM function, we are able to calculate the range of values within which the true value can be found.

Let’s go back to our height example. If we calculate the average of our sample to be 68 inches (5 feet 8 inches), we can construct a confidence interval around this estimate to tell us the range within which the true average height is likely to fall. If we want to be 95% confident about our estimate, we will set alpha to .05. The standard deviation was calculated to be 8 inches, and the sample size is 100. The result of the CONFIDENCE.NORM function would be about 1.5. We then add and subtract this result from our sample average to construct our confidence interval. The lower limit for this interval would be 66.5 inches (68-1.5) and the upper limit would be 69.5 (68+1.5). We would therefore conclude that the true average height in the population is between 66.5 inches and 69.5 inches. We would be 95% confident in our prediction.

Let’s look at another example (see ). Let’s assume that we own an ice cream shop. We are interested in how the temperature might impact our ice cream sales. We randomly selected the ice cream sales from 11 days from the last year and searched the internet to determine the high temperature for each of our selected days. The table in highlights the data we have collected as well as some statistics about this data sample.

informalfigure.title: Ice Cream Sales and Temperature data and statistics.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet that displays a column of temperatures and ice cream sales for selected days. Statistical calculations have been done to the right of the data.

informalfigure.title: Formulas used in the statistical calculations.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the statistical formulas and functions.

section.para: Specifically, we have calculated the count, average, standard deviation, and 95% confidence intervals for both temperature and sales. We estimate that the average outside temperature for the region is 69.27. With 95% confidence, we believe that the true average temperature will fall between 59.14 and 79.41. We also calculate the average sales as $5,185.96. The 95% confidence interval for sales is $4,460.89 and $5,911.03. This is a good first start to understand the relationship between ice cream sales and outside temperature.

section.title: Estimating Inputs with Correlations and Forecasts
section.para: Excel can also be used to complete more advanced statistical calculations. In fact, Excel can be used to perform most of the common statistical analyses. One important analysis you can complete in Excel is forecasting. Forecasting Making predictions about one set of values in your spreadsheet using information you know about another set of values. allows you to make predictions about one set of values in your spreadsheet using the information you know about another set of values.

Of course, for a prediction to be any good, there must be an underlying relationship between the two sets of values. Therefore, before we can discuss how to complete a forecast in Excel, we must first discuss how to determine if there is a relationship between two sets of values. To determine the strength of a relationship between two sets of values in Excel, we will calculate a correlation coefficient.

A correlation is a statistical relationship between two things. Correlation coefficients A statistical relationship between two things; the coefficient ranges between -1 and 1. range between -1 and 1. A positive correlation When values for one thing increase, the values for the other also increase. indicates that when values for one thing increase, values for the other also increase. An example of two things that are positively correlated would be ice cream sales and outside temperature. Using the data gathered for the example presented in , you can see that as the outside temperature rises, ice cream sales are also likely to rise. When the outside temperature is low, ice cream sales are also likely to be low. When things are strongly positively correlated, the correlation coefficient will be close to 1.

A negative correlation When values for one thing decrease, values of the other increase. means that when values for one thing decrease, values of the other increase. A negative correlation can also be strong. For example, there may also be a strong correlation between outside temperature and hot chocolate sales. As temperatures go down, hot chocolate sales go up. Strong negative correlations are close to -1.

If a correlation coefficient is close to 0 (between -.3 and about .3), the relationship between the sets of values is said to be weak. A moderate correlation exists when the absolute value of the correlation coefficient is between .3 and .7 (between -.3 and -.7 or between .3 and .7). When it is close to 1 or -1 (between .7 and 1 or between -.7 and -1), the relationship is strong.

The CORREL function in Excel calculates a correlation coefficient. Notice that the CORREL function requires two arrays as arguments. An array A set of values made of at least two numbers. is a set of values. There have to be at least two numbers in the set for it to qualify as an array.
=CORREL (array1, array2) – calculates the correlation coefficient between two sets of values

demonstrates the use of the CORREL function. The table in contains two sets, or arrays, of values. The first set contains our information from before about the outside temperature for a number of days. The second set details the ice cream sales, in dollars, for those days. We can calculate the correlation coefficient in cell B14 using the CORREL function. We calculate the correlation coefficient to be .926. There is a strong correlation between ice cream sales and temperature.

figure.title: Correlation between Ice Cream Sales and Temperature.

imagedata.alt: Correl function =correl(A2:A12,B2:B12) displayed in cell B14.

section.para: Once the strength of the relationship between two sets of values is known, it is possible to predict a value in one set using a corresponding value in the other set. There must be at least a moderate correlation between two things before it makes sense to make predictions based on the correlation between things.

Let’s look at an example. If I know that there is a strong correlation between ice cream sales and outside temperature, I can predict the ice cream sales for a specific day by using the outside temperature for that day. This is done using the FORECAST function:
FORECAST (X, known_y’s, known_x’s) – predicts a future value for a set of numbers based on a known relationship and trend in another set of values.

The FORECAST function predicts a future value for a set of numbers based on a known relationship and trend in another set of values. This function requires three arguments: x, known_y’s, and known_x’s. In statistical terms, y is the dependent variableThe value that a function attempts to predict., the value we are trying to predict. X refers to the independent variableThe value that a function uses to predict the other variable., or the value that we are using to predict the y variable. The "known_x’s" argument refers to the set of values that corresponds to all of the values for x. The "known_y’s" argument refers to the set of values that corresponds to all of the values for y

In our example, y is the ice creams sales for the day we are trying to predict. X represents the outside temperature for that specific day. The known_x’s represent all known values of the outside temperature, and the known_y’s are all of the known ice cream sales.

demonstrates the use of the FORECAST function to predict ice cream sales (y) given the outside temperature. In this case, x is 70, or the outside temperature for the day we are trying to use to predict the ice cream sales. The "known_y’s" argument is the set of values for ice cream sales, and the "known_x’s" argument is the set of values for the outside temperature. Using the FORECAST function, we predict that the ice cream sales for a day when the outside temperature is 70 degrees will be $5,234.15.

figure.title: Using Outside Temperature to Predict Ice Cream Sales.

imagedata.alt: Correlation of .926 displayed in cell B14 and Forecast function =forecast(A17,B2:B12,A2:A12) displayed in B16. Cell A17 contains the value 70.

section.para: We must be careful in interpreting a forecast. As we have already mentioned, a forecast should not be calculated when there is not at least a moderate correlation between the x’s and y’s. The FORECAST function will calculate a prediction for items with a low correlation, but the results will be unreliable if there is not at least a moderate relationship between the x and y values.

Also, the result of the FORECAST function is an estimate for the thing being predicted. In our ice cream sales example, the result was $5,234.15 for ice cream sales on a day with an outside temperature of 70 degrees. Because this is an estimate, it is extremely unlikely that the forecast value will be the actual value when the temperature is 70 degrees. The forecast value is our best guess, given the information that we have. It makes sense, then, to construct a confidence interval around the forecast. This way, you can get a feel for the range of likely values for your estimate.

section.title: Using Statistical Estimates as Model Inputs

videodata.be:name: All-Using Statistical Estimates to Determine Inputs.mp4

section.para: Once you have used statistics to make estimates for your model inputs, you should use those estimates to aid decision-making. For example, you may use a confidence interval in conjunction with an average or forecast calculation to construct a data table or create scenarios that will examine several what-if calculations for your models. The example below describes how this might work.

Let’s assume that you are planning to apply for a mortgage to buy a home. You have done some research and found the credit scores and interest rates for 40 recent loan applicants. Let’s also assume that this sample is good (it is a random sample of applicants). You complete some simple statistics on the sample and find a strong correlation between credit score and interest rate, so you also project your own interest rate and a 95% confidence interval around your forecast. The results of your analysis are in below.

informalfigure.title: Partial Interest Rate and Credit Score data.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays two columns of data. Column B has credit scores and column C has a list of Interest Rates.

informalfigure.title: Interest Rate and Credit Score statistical calculations.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the results of the statistical functions done on the worksheet data.

informalfigure.title: Formulas used in the statistical calculations.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the statistical functions used in the worksheet.

section.para: Now that you have an estimate for the interest rate and have calculated lower and upper limits for that calculation, you can use that information to determine your likely payment amounts for your mortgage. highlights a model that calculates an applicant’s mortgage payment based on their loan amount, interest rate, and term (the number of years needed to pay off the loan). Notice that the starting point for this calculation is the average interest rate calculated in .

figure.title: Using Statistical Calculations as Model Inputs.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the results of a payment function for a loan of 275000 at 5.103% interest and a term of 30 years. Below the payment data is a data table of three interest rates and there corresponding payments for the loan.

section.para: Using the forecasted interest rate as well as the upper and lower limits for that rate that you calculated in , you can construct a one-way data table to calculate three likely payment amounts for your loan. The most you are likely to pay for your loan is $1,503.63 because that is the upper limit of the 95% confidence interval you calculated on the loan. Likewise, the lowest likely payment would be $1,444.35 because this is the lower limit for your interest rate. The most likely amount is $1,473.85 since this is based on the forecasted amount for your interest rate.

section.title: PivotTables

listitem.para: Understand what PivotTables are and how they are created.
Know what elements make up PivotTables and how to manipulate them.
Use calculations and filtering in a PivotTable to analyze data.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: We live in an age where information is all around us. In many cases, the data that we need to solve a problem are available to use, but they are not organized well, or there is so much information available that it is difficult to make sense of it all. PivotTable reports (or PivotTables, as we will call them throughout the lesson) provide a powerful and flexible way to analyze large amounts of data quickly and easily in Excel.

PivotTablesA table used to organize and summarize large amounts of data; allows for different summarization and data-viewing options. are used to organize and summarize large amounts of data. PivotTables also allow us to change how we summarize data to look at it in different ways—that’s where the name comes from. We can look at the data one way and then easily “pivot” or change how we examine the data. This allows us to perform some fairly sophisticated analyses quickly and easily. In this lesson, we will discuss how to build a PivotTable, how to use the different parts of a PivotTable to summarize data, how to perform calculations on data in a PivotTable, and how to set up filters to screen the data displayed on the PivotTable.

section.title: Constructing a PivotTable
section.para: The first step in creating a PivotTable is preparing the source data. The first row of the source data for a PivotTable should contain labels for the data. These labels will be used to define the information you wish to summarize in your PivotTable. The source data should be free of any subtotals. The data should also be consistent. For example, if I were to summarize data based on the region of the company, I would make sure that the data consistently refer to each region (e.g., the “East” region is consistently referred to as the “East” region rather than something else).

contains part of the source data for PivotTable that we will use throughout this lesson. Use the link below to download the source data used in this lesson. We will examine a large sample of gas prices from a major metropolitan area. The data contain sample prices per day for a year from across four regions of the city. The data describe the date the sample was taken and detailed columns of this date, including the quarter, month, month #, and the day. The data also contains whether the gas station was part of a national franchise or a locally owned station, the gas price, and the region of the city where the sample was taken. Additionally, the data contain the daily sales and number of gallons sold for that gas station. There are 1,200 different samples represented on the table.

imagedata.alt: First seven rows of the source data that will be used to create the PivotTables.

section.para: Notice that the first row of the table clearly describes the data for each column. The data have been screened to ensure that each data entry consistently refers to the elements of the data for that column (dates, station type, and region are all consistently named).
To create a PivotTable, we select the PivotTable icon from the Insert group of menu items. The Create PivotTable dialog window appears.

imagedata.alt: Insert menu Table options that are available for the worksheet data.
Insert menu Table options that are available for the worksheet data.
Insert menu Table options that are available for the worksheet data.

section.para: The Create PivotTable dialog window () allows you to select the data source for the PivotTable. In this lesson, the data are located within a range within the open workbook. The data for this PivotTable are found in range A1:E1241 of the “Gas Data” worksheet. Notice that Excel will try to guess the worksheet range where the data are located. You can also manually select the range if Excel does not guess it correctly.
It is important to note that the source for a PivotTable can be another Excel workbook or a company database. The data can even be located on another computer.

The Create PivotTable dialog window also allows you to specify where the PivotTable will be located in the current workbook. You can either create a new worksheet for the PivotTable or specify a location on an existing worksheet. We will place the PivotTable on a new worksheet.
To create a PivotTable, we select the PivotTable icon from the Table group on the Insert menu. The Create PivotTable dialog window appears.

imagedata.alt: Create PivotTable dialog box window with data range A1:E1241 displayed.
Create PivotTable dialog box window with data range A1:E1241 displayed.
Create PivotTable dialog box window with data range A1:E1241 displayed.

section.para: When you click OK, a new worksheet is inserted in the workbook for the PivotTable, and a blank PivotTable is created.

imagedata.alt: Blank PivotTable report displayed on worksheet. Arrow point to PivotTable report, to data fields and the area where elements will be defined.
Blank PivotTable report displayed on worksheet. Arrow point to PivotTable report, to data fields and the area where elements will be defined.
Blank PivotTable report displayed on worksheet. Arrow point to PivotTable report, to data fields and the area where elements will be defined.

section.para: shows the blank PivotTable report that is created.
shows the blank PivotTable report that is created.

section.title: The Elements of a PivotTable
section.para: The right side of the workbook contains the PivotTable Field List area, where you can define how the PivotTable will organize the data (see ). If you do not see the PivotTable Field List, you can display it by clicking on the Field List icon in the Show group on the Analyze tab of the PivotTable Tools menu ().

The right side of the workbook contains the PivotTable Field List area, where you can define how the PivotTable will organize the data (see ). If you do not see the PivotTable Field List, you can display it by clicking on the Field List icon in the Show group of the PivotTable menu ().

figure.title: Accessing the Field List Menu Item.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable menu displaying the Field List option.
PivotTable menu displaying the Field List option.
PivotTable menu displaying the Field List option.

section.para: Before we start to define how the PivotTable will organize the data, it is important to describe each element of a PivotTable. The four elements of a PivotTable are Filters, Columns, Rows, and Values.

imagedata.alt: Blank PivotTable Elements boxes. Box for Filters, Columns, Rows and Values.
Blank PivotTable Elements boxes. Box for Filters, Columns, Rows and Values.
Blank PivotTable Elements boxes. Box for Filters, Columns, Rows and Values.

section.para: As the name implies, a Filter To screen the data used in a PivotTable. is used to screen the data as they are placed in a PivotTable. When a data field is used as a filter, we can select which elements of that data field will be displayed on the PivotTable. For example, if I were to use “Region” as a filter for the data used in this lesson, I could choose which regions will be displayed on the PivotTable.

Columns and Rows are used to define how the data will be summarized and arranged on the PivotTable. When a data field is defined as a column or row, each different value for that field will be displayed as a column or row heading on the PivotTable, and the source data will be categorized by those values. For example, in the data for this lesson, a gas station is classified by whether it is part of a national chain or a locally owned station. If a gas station is part of a national chain, the word “National” is included in the “Owner” column of the data table. If it is locally owned, the word “Local” is included in the “Owner” column. Therefore, the “Owner” column has only two different values for all of the entries in the data table (“National” or “Local”). When “National” is included as a Column, the values “National” and “Local” will be displayed as the Column headings of the PivotTable. All of the source data will be automatically categorized and summarized on the table by whether the gas station is part of a national chain or locally owned.

The Values Defines which data will be displayed on the PivotTable and can display more than one field at a time. element of the PivotTable defines which data will be displayed on the PivotTable. More than one field can be displayed at the same time as the Values for the PivotTable. You can also perform calculations on the data in the PivotTable and define how the data will be formatted.

It’s time to start using the PivotTable to analyze our data. For our first analysis, let’s examine whether the gas sales are different in the four regions of the city. We’ll add “Region” as the Rows and “Sales” as the Values for the PivotTable. To do this, drag the appropriate fields from the Choose fields to add to the report: section of the PivotTable Field List to the right area in the Drag fields between areas section. The PivotTable is automatically populated with the Region and Sales data.

It’s time to start using the PivotTable to analyze our data. For our first analysis, let’s examine whether the gas sales are different in the four regions of the city. We’ll add “Region” as the Rows and “Sales” as the Values for the PivotTable. To do this, drag the appropriate fields from the Choose fields: section of the PivotTable Field List to the right area in the Drag fields between areas below: section. The PivotTable is automatically populated with the Region and Sales data.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable elements with Region displayed in rows and Sum of Sales displayed in values.
PivotTable elements with Region displayed in rows and Sum of Sales displayed in values.
PivotTable elements with Region displayed in rows and Sum of Sales displayed in values.
PivotTable with regions as the rows and the sum of sales as the values.
PivotTable with regions as the rows and the sum of sales as the values.

section.para: When you add “Region” as the Rows of the PivotTable, the different regions in the data are displayed as the row headings of the table. When the daily sales are added as the values field of the PivotTable, the sales are categorized into each of the regions and the PivotTable displays the sum of the sales for each region. shows the resulting PivotTable. The value in cell B4 of the PivotTable is 8,302,056 and represents the total daily sales for all gas stations sampled in the East region. Notice that the PivotTable also automatically calculates a grand total for all values on the table.

When you first construct a PivotTable, you will notice that the values in the table are not formatted. In this case, we want the values to be formatted as currency. To make this change, we need to manipulate the Value Field Settings for the PivotTable. This is accessed by clicking on the upside-down triangle by “Sum of Sales” in the Values element of the PivotTable options. The Value Field Settings dialog window has an option to change the formatting for the PivotTable to currency.

When you first construct a PivotTable, you will notice that the values in the table are not formatted. In this case, we want the values to be formatted as currency. To make this change, we need to manipulate the PivotTable Field Settings. This is accessed by clicking on the circle with an i inside it by “Sum of Sales” in the Values element of the PivotTable options. The PivotTable Field Settings dialog window has an option to change the formatting for the PivotTable to currency.

When you first construct a PivotTable, you will notice that the values in the table are not formatted. In this case, we want the values to be formatted as currency. To make this change, we need to use the Number Format option on the right click shortcut menu. First, right click on any numbers in the PivotTable, choose Number Format and then choose Currency from the Number Format dialog box.

figure.title: Changing the Format of Data on the PivotTable.

informalfigure.title: Click on the upside-down triangle next to “Sum of Sales.”

section.para: The Value Field Settings dialog window also has options for changing the calculation performed on the sales values in the PivotTable. shows the Summarize Values By options for the PivotTable. By default, Excel will summarize the values of the PivotTable by taking the sum of the categories defined by the Rows and Columns. However, the values in these categories can be summarized in the following ways:

The PivotTable Field Settings dialog window also has options for changing the calculation performed on the sales values in the PivotTable. shows the Summarize By options for the PivotTable. By default, Excel will summarize the values of the PivotTable by taking the sum of the categories defined by the Rows and Columns. However, the values in these categories can be summarized in the following ways:

The right click shortcut menu also has options for changing the calculation performed on the sales values in the PivotTable. shows the “Summarize Values By” options for the PivotTable. By default, Excel will summarize the values of the PivotTable by taking the sum of the categories defined by the Rows and Columns. However, the values in these categories can be summarized in the following ways:

tr.td: The values in each category will be added together.
The number of different values will be displayed for each category.
The values in each category will be averaged.
The largest value for each category will be displayed.
The smallest value for each category will be displayed.
The values for each category will be multiplied together.
The number of values in each category that are numbers will be displayed.
The sample standard deviation for each category will be displayed.
The population standard deviation for each category will be displayed.
The sample variance for each category will be displayed.
The population variance for each category will be displayed.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable Value Field Settings window with Summarize Values By options displayed.
PivotTable field window with Summarize by options displayed.
PivotTable Value Field Settings field window with Summarize value field by options displayed.

section.para: Being able to summarize the values in the PivotTable using a variety of calculations provides a lot of flexibility for how the PivotTable is presented. PivotTables are also flexible enough to allow you to examine your data from multiple dimensions at the same time. At this point, we are summarizing the data by region. We can add the “Owner” data field to the table to summarize the sales by both region and type of ownership at the same time. This is done by dragging “Owner” into the Columns element of the PivotTable.

figure.title: Sales by Region and by Owner.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable fields with Owner as the columns, Region as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.
PivotTable fields with Owner as the columns, Region as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.
PivotTable fields with Owner as the columns, Region as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.
Two-dimensional PivotTable with region as the rows, owner as the columns and the sum of sales as the values.

section.para: The resulting PivotTable now categorizes the sales data by both region and type of ownership. The resulting value in cell B5, for example, is $1,198,955. This represents the total sales in the East regions for gas stations with a local owner. The value in C7 represents the total sales in the South region for gas stations owned by a national chain. Notice that both column and row totals are automatically calculated on the PivotTable.

Although it is difficult to visualize examining data from more than two dimensions at the same time, it is possible to do so in a PivotTable. Let’s add the Date data field in with the region as a row label for this analysis. Something unexpected but logical happens when you add “Date” to the analysis. We might expect that each of the different dates in the data is added separately as row labels. This is what is done for all other types of data in a PivotTable.

When we add a date field to a PivotTable in the desktop version of Excel, the PivotTable automatically groups all the transactions into logical periods of time, such as months or quarters (see ). Notice that Excel has added a new field to the PivotTable setup for Months and that the PivotTable automatically includes this field in the report. The resulting PivotTable values represent the aggregated monthly sales in each region for each type of owner.

informalfigure.title: A new field is automatically added to the PivotTable.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable fields window with Owner as the columns, Region and Date as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.
PivotTable fields window with Owner as the columns, Region and Date as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.
PivotTable fields window with Owner as the columns, Region and Date as the rows and Sum of Sales as the values.

informalfigure.title: The new Months field automatically appears in the PivotTable report.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable with months as the rows and an a plus icon on each month used to expand to see more details

informalfigure.title: New Date Field added to PivotTable report.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable with dates as rows values show total sales for that day.

section.para: It is unlikely, however, that we want to have so many categories for date in our analysis. In fact, it will likely be much more useful to aggregate the sales date into a familiar grouping for date like by month or by quarter. Notice that the original data contains columns for Quarter, Month, Month (#), and Day. The values for these fields are calculated based on the date column. The formulas for each calculation are shown in .

figure.title: Formulas Used to Aggregate by Data.

informalfigure.title: Formula for the "Day" calculation in cell E2.

imagedata.alt: Day function in E2 that gets the day from A2

informalfigure.title: Formula for the "Month (#)" calculation in D2.

imagedata.alt: Month function in D2 that gets the month from A2

informalfigure.title: Formula for the "Month" calculation in cell C2.

imagedata.alt: VLookup function used to display the month spelled out based on the date in A2 and the lookup table.

informalfigure.title: Formula for the "Quarter" calculation in cell B2.

imagedata.alt: VLookup function used to display the quarter based on the date in A2 and the lookup table.

section.para: To calculate the day when a transaction occurs, we use the DAY function with the associated data in column B as the argument. The function will return the day of the month when the transaction occurred. Similarly, the MONTH # function is used to return the month number of the transaction. A transaction that occurs in January would have a month number of 1 and a transaction occurring in December would have a month number of 12.

Calculating the month is a little more difficult. Here we use a lookup table to help associate the month name with the month number we calculated in the Month # column. Using the VLOOKUP function with the value for each Month #, we return the corresponding month name for each date.

We also calculate the quarter column using the same method we used to calculate the month column. A transaction occurs in Q1 if it happens during month numbers 1-3, Q2 during months 4-6, Q3 during months 7-9, and Q4 during the last three months of the year.

We do not need to stop here. In a larger dataset that contains transactions from multiple years, we would likely add a column to our data for the year in which the transaction occurs. We might also want to aggregate into much smaller time segments. For example, if the time of the day when a transaction occurs is important to our analysis, we might also create columns in our data the define a specific part of the day – such as morning, afternoon, and evening.

Once we have fields that describe the quarter, month, and day in our dataset, we can now use them in our PivotTable to summarize our data by dates in a more meaningful way. For example, we could replace the Date field we just added to the PivotTable with the Quarter and Month fields (in that order).

figure.title: Adding Aggregated Data Fields to a PivotTable.

informalfigure.title: PivotTable Setup with Aggregated Date Fields.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable fields with Owner as the columns, Region, Quarter and Month as the Rows with Sum of Sales as the values.
PivotTable that displays the four quarters and each months values within the quarter. Total rows for each region and quarter are displayed in bold

section.para: Notice how the PivotTable organizes the data. Since the first field in the row fields is Region, the PivotTable is first divided into regions. Within region, the PivotTable is further organized by date. The data are first summarized by quarter and then by month within each quarter. Each of the values represents the total sales for that region and owner type that occurred during the specified quarter and month. Adding the aggregate calculations for month and quarter allows us to perform quick and powerful analyses on our dataset with only a modest amount of effort. This process is much easier if you are using the installed version of Excel on a Mac or PC, since Excel will automatically create Quarterly and Monthly date fields as soon as you add the Date field to your PivotTable.

The PivotTable can be further manipulated to show more detail. If you click on the plus icon next to any of the months, the PivotTable will display all of the transactions that occurred within that month. The Figure below shows all results of expanding the month of February on the PivotTable.

figure.label: Figure 12
figure.title: Expanding a Data Field on a PivotTable.

imagedata.alt: PivotTable with February expanded to show the details for that month.

section.para: Excel does a good job of guessing which new aggregated fields you might need in your analysis. Since the data we are using in this lesson contain a set of transactions that all occurred within the same year, Excel automatically aggregates the data into months. If a dataset has date information that spans year, Excel will automatically add aggregated fields for years and quarters.

You may find that it would be helpful to aggregate a date field into other types of groups. For example, you may wish to see weekly sales rather than monthly sales. This is done by right-clicking on one of the dates in the PivotTable and selecting Group from the menu that appears. This opens the Grouping dialog window. In this dialog window, you can select the beginning and ending dates for the dates shown in the PivotTable, and you can choose the granularity with which dates are shown. In this case, we want to group dates into weeks. Notice that this is not one of the prebuilt options for the PivotTable, but we can group by “Days” and then set the number of days to group by to 7. This will group seven days together on the PivotTable.

section.para: Notice that the resulting table first groups the data into categories for the region and then for dates in each region. This happens because region is listed before date in the row labels section of the PivotTable setup. You can change how data fields are presented in the PivotTable by reordering the fields in the PivotTable setup. The value in cell B6 is $16,054. This represents the sales that occurred between 1/1/20 and 1/7/20 in the East region for the locally owned gas stations in our sample.
You can also perform additional calculations on the data in the PivotTable. The Show Values As options allow us to perform calculations on the values in the PivotTable.

You can also perform additional calculations on the data in the PivotTable. You may have noticed the “Show data as” options in the “PivotTable Field” dialog window when we changed the formatting of our PivotTable to currency. The “Show data as” options allow us to perform calculations on the values in the PivotTable.

section.para: For example, we may wish to perform a calculation to help us determine the percentage of all sales done in the North region. Let’s remove “Date” from the Rows of our PivotTable to simplify things. Now, let’s set the Show Values As option for the PivotTable to % of Column Total. Notice that the values in the PivotTable are changed. Each of the cells is expressed as a percentage of the total at the end of the column for that cell. The value in cell B5 is 25.87% and represents the fact that of the total sales for locally owned gas stations, 25.87% of those sales were done in the East region.

Now let’s set the Show data as option for the PivotTable to % of Column Total. Notice that the values in the PivotTable are changed. Each of the cells is expressed as a percentage of the total at the end of the column for that cell. The value in cell B5 is 25.87% and represents the fact that of the total sales for locally owned gas stations, 25.87% of those sales were done in the east region.

section.para: Focusing on the Grand Totals column of the table, we can determine the percentage of all sales that were realized in the North region. The information is displayed in cell D6. From this analysis, it appears that the four regions are very similar with respect to sales (all regions account for about ¼ of the total sales).

The % of Row Totals option will give us a similar analysis (see ). Notice that “100%” has moved from the end of each column to the end of each row. Each of the cells in the PivotTable will be expressed as a percentage of the row total for that cell. The value in cell B6 is 29.41%. This represents the fact that 29.41% of the total sales in the North region occurred in locally owned gas stations. Overall, 30.47% of total sales occurred in locally owned gas stations (cell B9).

section.title: Additional PivotTable Calculations
section.para: Other calculations compare values in a PivotTable using percentages. One way to do this is to display all values in the table as a percentage of the grand total of all values. For example, we could create a PivotTable to show the total sales by owner and region. Place “Region” as the row labels, “Owner” as the column labels, and the “Sum of Sales” as the data values. We then modify the Show Values As options to display % of Grand Total (see ).

Other calculations compare values in a PivotTable using percentages. One way to do this is to display all values in the table as a percentage of the grand total of all values. For example, we could create a PivotTable to show the total sales by owner and region. Place “Region” as the row labels, “Owner” as the column labels, and the “Sum of Sales” as the data values. We then modify the Show data as options to display % of Grand Total (see ).

The resulting PivotTable displays all the values as the percentage of the total sales in the dataset. The total sales by national owners in the West region is 17.06% of the total. Likewise, locally owned stations account for 30.47% of all sales.

section.para: It is important to take some time to try the different options available for using PivotTables to understand the power and flexibility of the PivotTable features in Excel. We may wish to have a PivotTable compare values in the table. For example, we may wish to calculate the difference in average gas prices between the regions in our gas data. To create this PivotTable, we place “Region” as row labels, “Owner” as column labels, and “Average of Price” as the data values.

It is important to take some time to try the different options available for using PivotTables to understand the power and flexibility of the PivotTable features in Excel. The desktop version of Excel includes other options that can be used to summerize data in PivotTables.

We also need to modify some of the Show Values As settings in the PivotTable. Using the drop-down list, we select Difference From. We are prompted to select a base field and item. Since we want to compare the south region to the other regions in the data, we will use “Region” as the base field and “South” as the base item.

We also need to modify some of the Show data as settings in the PivotTable. Using the drop-down list, we select Difference From. We are prompted to select a base field and item. Since we want to compare the south region to the other regions in the data, we will use “Region” as the base field and “South” as the base item.

After we have configured the PivotTable with these settings, the resulting table will display all of the values in the table as a difference from the south region. For example, the average gas price is $0.04 more expensive for local stations in the east region than for those in the south region. Notice that the values for the south region are all blank. They are blank because these calculated values are all zero.

section.para: The last PivotTable topic we will cover in this lesson is filtering the data displayed in the table. Filtering allows you to include or exclude specific data points from being part of the PivotTable. For example, using the gas station data for this lesson, you could set up a PivotTable to display sales by quarter, month, and region (see ). You could then create a filter to display only those gas stations owned by a national chain. The filter would effectively screen out any of the locally owned gas stations from the PivotTable.

imagedata.alt: Owner displayed as a Filter with the National option selected.
Owner displayed as a Filter with the National option selected.

section.para: There are two ways to filter data in a PivotTable: Filters and Slicers. In the example above, we used the filter option. demonstrates the process for setting up a filter. To set up a filter, you first drag the data field that you wish to use as the filter into the Filters section of the Drag fields between areas section of the PivotTable Field List window. In this case, we wish to use the “Owner” field as the filter. Once “Owner” is dragged into the filter section, a drop-down menu containing each of the unique values for the Owner data field is placed above the PivotTable. Using the drop-down menu, you can select those values for “Owner” that you wish to include in the PivotTable (notice that you can select multiple items at the same time). Once you click OK, the PivotTable will only display the data that you specified in the filter.

There are two ways to filter data in a PivotTable: Filters and Slicers. In the example above, we used the filter option. demonstrates the process for setting up a filter. In this case, we wish to use the “Owner” field as the filter. Once “Owner” is dragged into the filter section, a drop-down menu containing each of the unique values for the “Owner” data field is placed above the PivotTable. Using the drop-down menu, you can select those values for “Owner” that you wish to include in the PivotTable (notice that you can select multiple items at the same time). Once you click Close, the PivotTable will only display the data that you specified in the filter.

section.para: You can also use slicersA visual method for applying a filter to a PivotTable. to apply filters to a PivotTable. A slicer is a visual method for applying a filter to a PivotTable. A slicer can be added to a PivotTable by clicking on Insert Slicer in the Filter group of menu items in the Analyze menu tab of the PivotTable tools menu group (see ). You then select the data field you wish to use for the filter. In this case, we will use “Owner” as the data field for the slicer. When you click OK, a new window will appear in the worksheet that will allow you to choose which values for the “Owner” data field will be displayed in the PivotTable. When you click on “National” in the slicer window, only the gas stations owned by national chains will be displayed. You can clear the filter applied by the slicer by clicking on the Clear Filter icon in the top right corner of the slicer window.

You can also use slicersA visual method for applying a filter to a PivotTable. to apply filters to a PivotTable. A slicer is a visual method for applying a filter to a PivotTable. A slicer can be added to a PivotTable by clicking the down arrow next to the field name in the field list. You then select Add as Slicer.

section.title: The Database Approach

listitem.para: Understand and be confident using basic functions in a Data Management tool.
Understand the basic hierarchy of the Airtable online database, including workspaces, bases, tables, fields, and records.
Know how to build tables, input data, import records, and create forms in Airtable.

section.para: When people hear the word databaseA collection of facts and figures. , they often think of massive government or corporate computer systems with vast stockpiles of information. While big companies and organizations do use databases, individuals and small businesses use them as well. A database is a collection of facts and figures. A database management system A tool that optimizes data storage, preserves data quality, and provides users with efficient access to their data. is a tool that optimizes data storage, preserves data quality, and provides users with efficient access to their data.

Many individuals and small businesses use applications like Excel to store their data. While it is possible to store data in a spreadsheet file, tools like Excel are designed to help a user analyze their data. They have some distinct disadvantages for storing and safeguarding the quality of data. These disadvantages can be overcome by storing data in a database. Although using and maintaining corporate- and government-class databases requires a team of specialists, most people can create and manage a personal database to address their data storage needs.

section.title: Elements of a Database
The Relational Database Model
section.para: There are several approaches to storing data in a database. The most common of these methods is the relational model An approach used to store the data in a database based on the mathematical idea of a relation, which is a two-dimensional table.. The term relational refers to the mathematical idea of a relation, which is a two-dimensional table. Therefore, a relational databaseA database where data are stored in tables. is one in which data are stored in tables.

section.title: Database Tables
section.para: Generally, a database will include a number of tables. Each table is centered around a specific topic. For example, a human resource manager may use a relational database to track information about employees, where they work, and the positions they hold in the company. The database would include a separate table for each of these themes (see ). The employee table would include information about the individual employees, such as first name, last name, and the employee’s salary. The position table would include the data about the various jobs performed in the company and might include the job title and level of education required to fill that position. The location table would include all of the information about the various branches or offices of the company. This table might include the branch name, city, and state of each branch.

imagedata.alt: Three tables. The first is Position. List with Title and Education Required. Second table is Employee. List with First Name, Last Name, Salary, Link to Position, Link to Location. Third Table is Location. List of Branch Name, City, and State.

section.para: Tables are organized in a standard way using columns and rows. Database columns are used to store the attributes of the table. An attribute is a characteristic of the items stored on the table. An example of an attribute of an employee would be their last name. Notice that in , the information about employee last names is stored in one of the columns of the employee table.

imagedata.alt: Three tables. The first is Position. List with Title and Education Required. Second table is Employee. List with First Name, Last Name, Salary, Link to Position, Link to Location. Third Table is Location. List of Branch Name, City, and State.

section.para: Table rows contain all of the information that we know about a specific instance of the items on the table. On the employee table, an individual row would include all of the data that we have about an individual employee of the company. This would include that employee’s first name, last name, and salary. In , we see that everything stored about Kory Atkins is on the same row.

imagedata.alt: Table Rows

section.para: It may seem counterintuitive to organize data into themed tables because the data in the various tables are very likely interrelated. This is easy to see in our example. Employees hold positions and work in specific locations. Segregating information about employees, positions, and locations into three different tables could make it difficult to connect employees with their position and their location. The database approach allows you to preserve the relationships in the data across different tables (see ).

imagedata.alt: Relationships Among the Employee, Location, and Position Tables

section.para: The relationships among the data on different tables are preserved by adding attributes (columns) that link the rows of one table to the rows of other tables with related data. In our example, we would connect employees to their position and location by adding columns that link the individual employees to their position and location (see ). Notice that it is easy to see the position and location that correspond with each employee. Also, notice how easy it is to link an employee to more detailed information about their location or position. From the employee table, we can see Deanne Abbott’s position in the company. We can also use that information to determine the level of schooling she needed to acquire that job. Deanne is a senior developer. The position of senior developer requires a master’s degree. Therefore, Deanne needed a master’s degree to be hired in her position.

figure.title: Linking Employees to Positions and Locations.

imagedata.alt: Linking Employees to Positions and Locations

section.title: Other Database Elements
section.para: In addition to tables, most databases will have other elements that make the process of managing data easier. We will work with two of these as part of the exercises in this class. The first is a form. A database form is a tool that users can use to easily add new rows to database tables. The second is a query. We use queries to retrieve data from a database. We will discuss forms and queries in subsequent sections of the book.

section.title: Designing Database Tables
section.para: A lot of thought goes into organizing the tables and relationships between tables in a database. Well-designed databases are easier to use and less costly to maintain than poorly organized databases. Well-designed databases also help ensure data integrity, which means that our data is accurate and consistent.

The process of organizing tables and relationships between tables in a database is called normalization The process of organizing tables and relationships between tables in a database. . We normalize databases to accomplish two important objectives. First, we ensure that each table contains data about very specific things as we discussed in the last section. Second, we want to limit duplicated or redundant information as much as possible. Duplicating data in a database leads to problems over time, because when data changes it must be updated in multiple places to avoid inconsistencies.

section.title: Principles of Table Design
section.para: There are four principles of good table design that will greatly improve the organization of the data in your table. These principles are: maintain proper table columns, define an appropriate domain for each attribute, make sure that each row contains information about only one instance of the table, and make sure that each instance is recorded entirely on a single row of the table.

section.title: Maintain Proper Columns
section.para: Your table should contain a description of the data stored in each column. This description is called a heading Descriptive text at the top of the data column for each field in a database table. . Placing a heading at the top of a column clearly notes for the user what the values in each column mean. In the employee data example, the values above the first row of the table clearly describe what that data in each column mean.

In addition to using clear column headings that describe the data stored in each column, each column should contain different information. Essentially, this means that you would not place information about a single attribute of the table in more than one column. This makes it easier for a user to find all the information for that element. In this example (), you would not store information about branch location for each employee in more than one column. When column data is duplicated, it is difficult for the user to know precisely where to look to find data. Moreover, if duplicated columns contain inconsistent information, it would be impossible for the user to know which is correct.

imagedata.alt: Do Not Duplicate Columns

section.title: Define an Appropriate Domain for Each Attribute
section.para: The domain Defines which values can be stored in that column. Properly defining the domain for an attribute helps to ensure the quality of the data in the table. for an attribute defines which values can be stored in that column. Properly defining the domain for an attribute helps to ensure the quality of the data in the table. In our example, the first name and last name fields on the employee table contain text. The domain for these fields is any set of alphabetic characters that could form a first or last name. Contrast this with the salary column. The values on this column are all numbers. Additionally, managers may wish to further constrain the numbers that can be stored in the salary field to be within a range (particularly on the top end) so that an employee is not paid too much money. Finally, examine the values for the link to position column. The values for this column are text and they are constrained to the existing positions on the position table.

Database tools will require that you define the data to be stored in each column of each table you build. While the specific data types available may be different across tools, the concept is the same. When designing a database table, carefully determine the types of values to be stored in each column and then use the database tool to enforce the decisions you make.

section.title: Each Row Contains Information about Only One Instance
section.para: The next principle of good table design is that each row of the table should only contain information about one instance of the data on the table. In the example, this means that you would not include information about more than one employee on a single row of the table. Imaging that the row for Charlene Atkins incorrectly contained the salary for Kory Atkins. It would be impossible to know this by just looking at the table. A user would not be able to trust the accuracy of the data on the table, rendering the table useless.

section.title: Each Instance Is Entirely on Only One Row
section.para: The last principle of good table design is related to the previous principle. This last principle states that all information about an instance on a table should be contained on the same row. If information for one instance of a table is split across the table, it may be difficult to analyze data effectively or determine which data elements belong to each instance. For example, if information about the salary for Marquis Aguilar were split across rows, it may become problematic for the user to determine how to interpret and use the data for a few reasons:

listitem.para: Having more than one row for a single employee would make it difficult to know if the duplicate rows are for one employee or more than one employee. In the case of , both of the duplicated rows contain the same values for the first and last name. This would lead the user to conclude that both rows contain information about the same employee. If the first name or last name were missing from one of the rows or was not the same in both rows, it may be difficult for the user to understand whether the rows contain information about one employee or two.

Having more than one row for a single employee may cause information to be duplicated. If the duplicate rows repeat information for the same column in both rows, it would be difficult to know if the data from the duplicated row should be ignored (to avoid the possibility of double counting) or included from both rows (to avoid the possibility of missing information). In this case, it is impossible to determine if Marquis is one employee or two.

Having more than one row for a single employee may lead to missing information. As you can see from , the first of the duplicated rows contains blank cells. If there is not a complete set of data between the two rows, some important data may be missing.

Finally, duplicated rows may contain inconsistent information. For example, if the value for the salary field for the duplicated rows contained different numbers, it would be difficult for the user to know which of the values is correct. In this case it is impossible to know if Marquis' salary is $14,300 or $143,000.

imagedata.alt: Do Not Duplicate Rows

section.title: Using Airtable
section.para: Often individuals and small businesses require a solution that is easy to set up and maintain but takes advantage of all of the benefits of the database approach to storing data. Airtable is an able and intuitive alternative to enterprise database solutions such as Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle, and MySQL, which generally require a team of employees to operate and maintain. Airtable can also be used to support the data needs of fairly large organizations.

section.title: What Is Airtable?
section.para: Airtable is a simple yet powerful online database solution. Because it is web-based, you can use Airtable on virtually any smartphone, tablet, Chromebook, or personal computer, and you can share your data easily with others. Airtable is also easy to use. Much of the hassle associated with installing, configuring, and maintaining a database management system has been eliminated.

To use Airtable, you will need to create an account. In addition to a free account (which is all you need for your class), Airtable offers relatively low-cost options that will meet the data needs of most small organizations. A summary of the pricing options for Airtable can be found at Airtable Pricing.

To create an account, navigate to Airtable Signup Invitation. You will need to provide your name and an email address and password to create your free account. The free account tier allows you to create an unlimited number of projects, called Bases, each with up to 1,200 records and two gigabytes of attached storage.

The free account also provides access to the online help tools (see ). While this lesson will cover the basics of using Airtable so that you can complete the various activities in the class, the online help tools documentation covers all of the features of Airtable. The help documentation is integrated into the various aspects of Airtable and can also be accessed on the main screen after you log in.

imagedata.alt: The Airtable Help Features. Learning and Resources section highlighted.

section.title: Professional Airtable Accounts for Students
section.para: University students qualify for a free upgrade to a professional Airtable account. If you have not already created an Airtable account, you can do so by following this link: Airtable Signup Invitation. After creating your Airtable account, navigate to Airtable for Education Access Form. This website will walk you through the process for obtaining an upgrade to a professional account.

section.title: The Components of an Airtable Project
section.para: The basic hierarchy of Airtable includes workspaces, bases, tables, fields, and records.

listitem.para: WorkspacesThe personal space on Airtable that can be divided into groups. : Your personal space on Airtable is divided into one or more workspaces. You can define your workspaces, which can be configured to organize your projects (i.e., bases), according to your preferences (see ).

Bases Groupings used in Airtable that contains a collection of related tables that store all of the data for a particular project. : Each database project in Airtable is stored as a base. A base is a collection of related tables that store all of the data for a particular project. When you wish to start a new Airtable project, you create a new base. For example, you may wish to create a base to store information about the customers and sales in your small business.

imagedata.alt: Airtable Workspaces and Bases. Workspaces section highlighted with My Bases, Bases Shared With Me, and other options.

listitem.para: Tables A method to organize data into the major data themes of a project; can be organized into two-dimensional tables. : Airtable organizes data into two-dimensional tables according to the major data themes of your project (see ). In your small business base, you would likely organize the data into separate tables. You might have different tables for customer information, sales, and the products you sell.

Fields Elements that contain the attributes of the subject of the table and are arranged as the columns of the table.: Table fields are arranged as the columns of the table. Table columns contain the attributes of the subject of the table. For example, the customer table in our small business base would include all of the relevant attributes of a customer. These might include first and last name, address, phone number, and email address.

Records The basic elements of data in Airtable that comprise the rows of base tables and contain all of the information about an instance of that table.: Records are the basic elements of data in Airtable. They comprise the rows of our base tables and contain all of the information we have about an instance of that table. On our customer table, we would have one record (or row) for each customer that contains everything we know about the attributes of that customer.

imagedata.alt: Base Tables, Fields, and Records. First base layout Product tab highlighted with Tables labeled. Address column labeled Fields. First entry labeled Records.

section.para: The building blocks of an Airtable project are not all that different than the building blocks of a relational database project that might be created using an enterprise database management system. The detailed help topics on the Airtable website fully document all of the aspects of each element. In the next sections of this lesson, we will discuss the basics of each.

section.title: Working with Bases
section.para: As mentioned above, all of the information for a given data project is stored in an Airtable base. You can create a new base by clicking on the Create icon in one of your workspaces. You can create a new base from one of the dozens of preconfigured templates in Airtable by uploading data from a spreadsheet file on your computer or by designing an empty base from scratch (see ).

figure.title: Options for Creating a New Base.

imagedata.alt: Options for Creating a New Base. Start with a template and an icon of a mountain. Import a spreadsheet with an upward-pointing arrow. Start from scratch with an icon of a pencil.

section.para: If you decide to create a new base from scratch, clicking the dropdown next to Untitiled Base will presents you with a window (see ) where you can specify the details of your new base. In this window, you can define a name for the new base, select a color and icon for the base to help you easily distinguish among the bases in your workspace, share your base with others, make a copy of your base, and delete it.

figure.title: Adding a New Base in Airtable.

imagedata.alt: Adding a New Base in Airtable. My First Base setting. Gray square selected, icon with My text, and options to Share, Duplicate base, Move base to another workspace, Slack notifications, and Delete base.

section.para: You can edit an existing base by clicking on the three dots that appears in the base icon when you hover your mouse over that base (see ). The dropdown will provide you with the same configuration options available when you add a new base to a workspace.

imagedata.alt: Editing a Base. My First Base icon with a downward-pointing arrow.

section.para: You can share your new base with others. You can control how others can interact with the base. You can allow others to edit the contents of the base or just view the data included in the base.

To open a base, you click on the icon for that base. This will provide you access to all of the tables you have created for the base. Once a base is open, you can define and manage the tables, fields, and records for the base.

section.title: Working with Tables
section.para: When you open an Airtable base, you can navigate between the base tables using the tabs at the top of the screen. The base in has employee and department tables. You can easily switch between the tables by clicking on the tab for the table you wish to view.

imagedata.alt: Tables in a Base. My First Base menu with a department tab.

section.para: Airtable allows you to view the data in a table in a number of different ways, called views. You can switch among the various views of a table by clicking on the drop-down list in the top left corner of the table (see ).

imagedata.alt: Switching Among Table Views. Department tab with Grid View menu bar highlighted.

section.para: Three common views for reviewing data in an Airtable table are the Grid, Calendar, and Form views. The grid view View that allows you to view the data as a standard two-dimensional table with columns and rows. allows you to view the data as a standard two-dimensional table with columns and rows (see ).

figure.title: Grid View of the Department Table.

imagedata.alt: Grid View of the Department Table. Department tab in Grid View.

section.para: The calendar view View that organizes the data on the table according to dates on a calendar. organizes the data on the table according to dates on a calendar (see ). Of course, you will need date information on the table in order to view the table with the calendar view. You can configure the calendar view to display different time periods (day, multiple days, week, multiple weeks, or month).

figure.title: Calendar View of the Department Table.

imagedata.alt: Calendar View of the Department Table. Calendar View with monthly view of April 2020.

section.para: The form view View used to create a simple interface for adding records to a table. of a table is used to create a simple interface for adding records to a table. When creating a form, you can select a name and description for the form as well as an image or a logo to place on the form. You can also select which of the table fields to include on the form, and the order that each will be displayed in the form. demonstrates the options available when creating a form for the Employee table.

imagedata.alt: Form Design. Form menu with option fields for FirstName and Lastname and Address, etc.

section.para: Once a form is designed, it can be used to enter new data into the table. It can also be shared over the internet with others who may use it to enter new data. The form view does not display any of the existing records of the table. Rather, it provides input boxes for the user to add new data. provides an example of a completed table form that can be used to add a new employee to the table.

imagedata.alt: Completed Employee Form. Employee Form for Add an employee to the employee table with fields for LastName, FirstName, Department, Address, and etc., and a submit button.

section.para: In addition to using the various views of a table to examine the data, Airtable provides additional tools for customizing how you will see the data in the table. While in the Grid view, you can access the Filters, Groups, and Sorting tools (see ).

figure.title: Filter, Group, and Sort Table Tools.

imagedata.alt: Filter, Group, and Sort Table Tools. Menu bar with Employee tab open and sections for Filter, Group, and Sort highlighted.

section.para: Filtering tools Tools that allow you to set rules for viewing records on the table so that only those rows that meet the condition set by the rule will be displayed in the table. allow you to set rules for viewing records on the table. Only those rows that meet the condition set by the rule will be displayed in the table. It is important to note that the rows that are not displayed because they do not meet the filter condition are not removed from the table; they are just hidden from the view of the table. For example, you could set a filter on the table to display only those employees whose last name includes the letter z (see ).

figure.title: Filter to Show Employees with a Last Name with the Letter Z.

imagedata.alt: Filter to Show Employees with a Last Name with the Letter Z. Filter section on the Menu tab and results of search with three records highlighted.

section.para: Group byTools in Airtable that allow you to create categories across the rows of a table according to the values of one of the fields of a table. allows you to create categories across the rows of a table according to the values of one of the fields of a table. For example, we could group the rows of the employee table by the departments each employee belongs to. When the grouping is applied, all of the employees from the same department are displayed together. Summary values can be calculated for each group. For example, displays the employee table grouped by department. Notice that the number of employees for each department is calculated and displayed within the groups.

imagedata.alt: Employee Table Grouped By Department. Menu bar with Grouped by 1 field. Several departments with different records. Thirteen records highlighted in the first section Accounting.

section.para: Sorting A tool in Airtable that allows you to rearrange the rows of a table according to the values in one or more fields of the table. allows you to rearrange the rows of a table according to the values in one or more fields of the table. For example, if you want to view an alphabetized list of employees, you can sort the table by the last name field in ascending order (see ). You can also sort a table by a field in descending order. Finally, you can sort a table by more than one field at the same time.

figure.title: Table Sorted by Last Name in Ascending Order.

imagedata.alt: Table Sorted by Last Name in Ascending Order. Menu bar with Sorted by 1 field. Full column of Last Name highlighted. That column is sorted from A to Z.

section.title: Working with Fields
section.para: Airtable has a number of tools for building and editing table columns, or fields. To add a new column to a table, you click on the plus sign or the Add field button next to the last used column of the table. You can edit or delete a column by clicking on the drop-down arrow on the right of the field name. shows how to add a field.

imagedata.alt: Adding a Table Field. Menu bar with tab for Department and Employee. Department open. Below are options for Filter, Group, and Sort Table. Add field option highlighted.

section.para: There are two important considerations when creating or editing a table field. First, the name of the field must be unique on the table. In other words, no two fields on the same table can have the same name. Second, it is important to carefully consider the data type defined for the field. The name and the data type for the field can be defined using the Customized field type option in the drop-down menu invoked in .

figure.title: Editing and Deleting a Table Field.

imagedata.alt: Editing and Deleting a Table Field. Employee with dropdown arrow options. Customize field type, Rename field, Edit field description, Duplicate field, Insert left, Insert right, Sort A to Z, Sort Z to A, Add filter, Group by this field, Hide field, Delete field.

section.para: depicts the data types A restriction on the type of data that can be added to a field. available for fields in Airtable. Notice that some data types, such as Number, constrain the type of data that can be added to a field. Only a number (not text) can be added to a field designated with a Number data type. Others, such as Single select, further constrain the data to a predefined set of values among which the user must choose.

imagedata.alt: Airtable Data Types. Open field Employee. Link to another record and a long list of other options.

section.para: The Link to another record data type is used to connect a table to data on a related table. For example (see ), the Department field on the Employee table links each employee on the table to their corresponding department on the department table.
