In this regard, the Department field on the Employee table is a foreign key on the Employee table.

figure.title: The Department Field on the Employee Table.

imagedata.alt: The Department Field on the Employee Table. Menu bar with tab for Department and Employee. Employee open. Below are options for Grid View, Hide Fields, Filter, Group, and Sort Table. The last column of the spreadsheet is Department. Department is highlighted.

section.para: You may have noticed that Airtable does not seem to use primary key A field that uniquely identifies a record in the relational database table. fields. This is technically not the case, but the primary key fields are hidden from the user. Instead, the first field of the parent The table that stores the primary key. table is used as the primary key value to link two tables together. and demonstrate how this works. When you add a value for the foreign key The field in a table that is the primary key of another table. (Department) on the Employee table for a specific employee, Airtable presents you with a list of values for the first field on the Department table, and you choose the value for department that you wish to assign to that employee. This serves to simplify the process of connecting tables.

figure.title: The Department Field on the Employee Table.

imagedata.alt: The Department Field on the Employee Table. Line of data on the Department field highlighted.

figure.title: The First Field in the Department Table.

imagedata.alt: The First Field in the Department Table

section.para: The fact that Airtable hides the primary key also obscures the function of the primary key—to differentiate all of the rows of the table. In the absence of a visible primary key, it is a good practice to make the first field of an Airtable table as descriptive as possible to help differentiate all of the rows of the table, particularly if you plan to connect the table with another table.

Notice that the first field (Department) of the Department table in uses a combination of the DepartmentCode and the DepartmentName fields. Airtable can be configured to automatically calculate these values. To do this, you set the data type to Function and define the calculation you wish to use to define the values of the field. In this case, the calculation is DepartmentCode & ' ' & DepartmentName (see ). The ampersand character (&) is used to combine text. This statement tells Airtable to combine the value of the DepartmentCode with a space (within the single quotes) and the value of the DepartmentName for each row of the table.

figure.title: The Calculation for the Department Field of the Department Table.

imagedata.alt: The Calculation for the Department Field of the Department Table. Department field dialog box. Formula tab selected and highlighted.

section.title: Working with Records
section.para: There are a number of ways to add new records (rows) to a table in Airtable. Using the Grid view of a table, you can add new rows by typing them directly in an empty row below the existing data on the table. You can also edit or delete existing data on an Airtable table using the Grid view. Additionally, you also use a form, as we discussed earlier in this lesson, to add new records to the table.

It is also possible to add more than one row at a time to an Airtable table. Airtable has an option to import data into a new table from a spreadsheet file (see ). You can copy and paste values from an application like Excel or Word directly into an existing table.

figure.title: Importing a Spreadsheet into a New Table.

imagedata.alt: Importing a Spreadsheet into a New Table. Menu bar with tab for Department and Employee. Employee open. Add icon next to tab with a dropdown menu with options to Create empty table and Import a spreadsheet.

section.title: Sharing a Base with Others
section.para: Sharing a base with others is an easy process. With the base you wish to share open, click the "Share" button near the top right corner of the Airtable interface. This will invoke the Share dialog window.

imagedata.alt: Sharing an Airtable Base. Click on Share with the base open.

section.para: To share your base, enter the email address of the colleague you wish to share the base with in the "Invite by email..." text box. Click "Invite". You have now shared the base.

imagedata.alt: Share. Options for sharing a base.

section.para: Notice that there are additional options available in the Share dialog window. You can share the base by providing your colleague with a link (instead of using their email address) or make your base available publically. You can also share part of a base (a view) and manage the shares that you have already created to this base.

section.title: Test Your Skills: Designing and Building a Database
Data Independence

listitem.para: Understand and be confident writing and running SQL SELECT clauses.
Know how to use the DISTINCT keyword in SQL queries.
Understand and be confident writing and running SQL with an ORDER BY clause.
Know how to use the LIMIT option in SQL queries.

section.para: As we discussed in , the database approach to storing and managing data has important advantages over storing data in Excel or even data tables. The main advantage we discussed in the last lesson is that the database approach helps us to ensure that our data is of high quality. In this lesson, we will discuss another important advantage: data independence A state in which the data used by a program is maintained separately from the applications or programs that use the data..

Data independence occurs when the data used by a program is maintained separately from the applications or programs that use the data. Generally, the data is stored in a database and managed using specialized database management (DBMS) software Programs and applications that can be hosted by a server (such as an internet site) or installed on a user’s device that are used to manage data in a database. . Airtable can be loosely thought of as DBMS software. Programs and applications can be hosted by a server (such as an internet site) or installed on a user’s device.

Data independence creates two very important outcomes. First, the applications that use the data can be updated without the need to change any of the underlying data used by the application. Second, more than one application can use the same data source at the same time, making it easier to share data across the various programs used by an organization.

section.title: What Is SQL?
section.para: When data and applications are maintained separately, features of the DBMS make it possible for the application to interact with the data stored in the database. Because it is so common to separate data from applications, there is a standard programming language used by applications to interact with the data in a database. This language is called Structured Query Language (SQL)A standard programming language used by applications to interact with the data in a database. . The S in SQL could also easily stand for standard because SQL is used to interact with data in all sorts of databases, including Oracle, SQL Server, and MySQL.

Before you get too nervous about learning a programming language for this class, it is important to recognize that there are only a limited number of things that an application actually needs to do to interact with a database, so the number of things that we actually do with SQL is pretty small. These activities include the following:

listitem.para: Creating database tables
Manipulating (adding, updating, and deleting) data in tables
Retrieving existing data from tables

section.para: In this book, we will cover some of the basics of SQL and focus on SQL features that allow us to retrieve data from an existing table.

section.title: SQL Basics
section.para: When retrieving data from database tables, we use what we call a SELECT query A method used to retrieve data from database tables. . For example, we will discuss how we might use a SELECT query to retrieve data from the employee table depicted in .

imagedata.alt: Spreadsheet table with five columns and 13 lines. Columns are First Name, Last Name, Salary, Link to Position, and Link to Location.

section.para: There are six sections, called clausesSections for a SELECT query. , for a SELECT query. While a single query can use all six of these clauses, most queries—even advanced queries—will only use two or three. The six clauses, in the order we use them when writing a query, are as follows:

listitem.para: SELECT: The SELECT clauseThe clause used first when writing a query; it is used to define which of the columns in the table will be displayed in the query results. is always used first when writing a query. The SELECT clause is used to define which of the columns in the table will be displayed in the query results. If we wanted to write a query to display the first and last names of the employees on the employee table, we would start that query "SELECT FirstName, LastName". The other fields on the table (Salary, Link to Position, and Link to Location) would not appear in the resulting query.

FROM: The FROM clause The clause in a SELECT query that always appears second and lists the tables that will be included in a query result. always appears second in a query. The FROM clause lists the tables that will be included in a query result. In our example, to list the first and last names of the employees on the employee table, our SQL statement would be "SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employee". Because the SELECT and FROM clauses are the only two required clauses in SQL statements, "SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employee" is a valid and complete SQL statement that would return the results shown in .

imagedata.alt: First two columns. Last name entries are alphabetized.

listitem.para: WHERE: The WHERE clause The clause used in a SELECT query to limit the rows that are returned in the query result. is used to limit the rows that are returned in a query result. For example, we may wish to see only those employees whose last name is “Adkins” in our query result. The WHERE clause is optional. When used, it appears directly after FROM. We would add the WHERE clause, "WHERE LastName = 'Adkins'" to our SQL statement to show only those employees with the last name “Adkins” in the query result. The resulting SQL statement would be "SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employee WHERE LastName = 'Adkins'" and the DMBS would return the results in .

figure.title: Query Results with a WHERE Clause.

imagedata.alt: First two columns. Three entries, all with the last name Adkins.

listitem.para: GROUP BY – The GROUP BY clause The clause used in a SELECT query to summarize the data by categories. is used to summarize the data by categories. For example, we may want to know how many employees work in each location. We would use the GROUP BY clause to categorize the query results by location so that we could then calculate the number of employees in each location. The SQL statement required to create this query is beyond the scope of this resource. Because seeing the result of this type of query is helpful in understanding what this type of query does, the results have been included in .

figure.title: Query Result with a GROUP BY Clause.

imagedata.alt: Location and Count columns. Five entries.

listitem.para: HAVING – The HAVING clause The clause used in a SELECT query to restrict the categories in a query result. is also beyond the scope of this class. HAVING is used to restrict the categories in a query result. We might use HAVING to show only those locations where four employees work (see ).

figure.title: Query Result with a HAVING Clause.

imagedata.alt: Location and Count columns. Two entries, both with four count.

listitem.para: ORDER BY – The ORDER BY clause Used in a SELECT query to sort the rows of the query result. is used to sort the rows of the query result. ORDER BY is an optional clause that always appears last in a SQL statement. For example, we may wish to rearrange the three employees returned in the query results in to display the employees in alphabetical order by first name. The ORDER BY clause would be "ORDER BY FirstName". The complete syntax for the SQL statement would be "SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employee WHERE LastName= 'Adkins' ORDER BY FirstName". The query results for this SQL statement would be as displayed in .

figure.title: Query Results with an ORDER BY Clause.

imagedata.alt: First Name and Last Name columns. Three entries, all with Adkins as Last Name.

section.title: webSQL.org
section.para: In order to successfully interact with data in a database, the DBMS must provide a place where the user can write and submit SQL statements and then view or receive the results. When an application submits SQL statements and receives query results, this process is unseen by the user. In this class, however, you need a place to edit SQL statements and see the results displayed. We will use the webSQL.org website for this purpose.

tip.title: What is webSQL.org?
tip.para: The webSQL.org website is a free, browser-based SQL editor built for students learning to write and run SQL statements. There is no account to create and nothing to install: webSQL.org runs a real PostgreSQL database directly in your browser, so you can type a SQL statement, run it, and see the query result immediately on the same page.

section.para: You can start writing and running SQL statements right away at websql.org. Throughout this book, you will also see live, editable query examples embedded directly in the page, like the ones later in this lesson.
In these lessons, we will refer to the Student Organization that we created in the assessment for . A diagram depicting this database can be found in .

imagedata.alt: Lists for Income, Zip code, Member, Major, Attendance, Expense, Event, and Budget. Connected lines from entries in one list to entries in another list.

section.para: In the remainder of this lesson, we will discuss the SELECT and ORDER BY clauses in more detail. We will discuss the WHERE clause in . In , we will cover how to use the FROM clause to include more than one table in a query result.

section.title: The SELECT Clause of the SQL Statement
section.para: SELECT is the first clause in a SQL statement. The SELECT clause is used to specify the columns you wish to see in a query result. Along with the FROM clause, the SELECT clause is required of every SQL statement that we write.

section.title: The Syntax of the SELECT Clause
section.para: The SELECT clause of a SQL statement always begins with the keyword SELECT (note that although the keywords in a SQL statement are not case sensitive, we will capitalize them in this lesson to highlight them). Immediately following the keyword SELECT in the SELECT clause, we list the names of the columns we wish to appear in the query result separated by commas. For example, if we wish to see the last name and email address of each of the members in the student organization data, the SQL statement for that query would be as follows:
SELECT last_name, email FROM Member
The results of this query are displayed in .

You can choose to include or omit any of the columns from the Member table in your result. Additionally, the columns you include can appear in any order you wish. If you wanted the email address to appear before the last name in the result, you would write the query as follows:
SELECT email, first_name FROM Member

There is a shorthand way to include all columns from a table in a query. This is done using the * character instead of the list of column headings in the SELECT clause. This syntax is shown below and will produce the results in . Notice that more columns are displayed in the query result than appear in the screenshot.
The PostgreSQL documentation contains additional instructions and examples for using the SELECT clause of SQL statements. You can access these materials at SELECT.

tip.title: Working with New Data
tip.para: SELECT * from...is a really useful tool to help you get familiar with a new database. Even if you have a good database diagram, it is a great idea to look at the data and make sure it makes sense to you. For example, consider the Position column of the Student table. What does this mean? Is it the rank students hold in their class? Is it a role they play on a sports team? Or is it something else?

Using SELECT * from Student, you can see that the values in the Position column include Member, President, and Vice President. From this, it is clear that Position is the position the students hold in the club. It is usually a good idea to explore all of the tables in an unfamiliar database to make sure you understand the data.

section.title: Removing Duplicate Rows from a Query Result
section.para: You probably recall that every row of a database table is unique. This is not always the case with a query result. Notice that if we were to write a query to show only the position column from the Member table, many of the rows on the query result would contain duplicated information (see ).

The duplicated rows exist because multiple members hold the same position in the club. In this case, many members hold the position of “Member.” There may be times that you will want to see this duplicated information. However, if the original intent of the query was to display a list of the different positions held by members, the duplicated rows could make it difficult to determine the unique positions.

We can rewrite the query to show only unique rows in the query result (see ). To do this, we include the keyword DISTINCT after SELECT in the SELECT clause. The updated syntax is below. Notice that there are only six unique positions held across the 33 members on the table.
SELECT DISTINCT position FROM Member

section.title: The ORDER BY Clause of the SQL Statement
section.para: The ORDER BY clause in a SQL statement is used to sort or rearrange the order in which the rows will appear in a query result. You can sort the rows of a query result in ascending or descending order based on the values of the fields included in the ORDER BY clause. Note that the ORDER BY clause does not remove any rows from the query result—it just rearranges them.

Ascending order implies arranging the rows from smallest to largest for numbers, alphabetically for names or words, or chronologically for dates. When you use an ORDER BY clause in a query, it is assumed that you would like the rows sorted in ascending order. Because of this, you do not need to specify that you wish to arrange the rows in ascending order in the ORDER BY clause. Suppose you want a list of members from the member table arranged alphabetically by last name. The syntax for this query is given below. The results of this query are shown in .
SELECT first_name, last_name FROM Member ORDER BY last_name

figure.title: Query with Results Sorted Alphabetically by Last Name.

section.para: You may also wish to sort the results of a query in descending order. Descending order implies arranging the rows from largest to smallest for numbers, reverse alphabetically for names or words, or reverse chronologically for dates. To specify that you wish the rows of a query result to be sorted in descending order, you add the keyword DESC to the ORDER BY clause. For example, if you wanted the same list of members to be arranged in reverse alphabetical order (Z to A) by last name, you would use the syntax below. The results of this query are in .
SELECT first_name, last_name FROM Member ORDER BY last_name DESC

figure.title: Query with Results Sorted Reverse Alphabetically by Last Name.

section.para: You can also sort a query result by more than one field at the same time. When this is done, the first level of sorting will be done with the first field specified in the ORDER BY clause. The query will then be sorted by each subsequent field in the ORDER BY clause in the order they appear in the clause. The fields are specified using a list of field names separated by commas in the ORDER BY clause.

Suppose we want to see the members of the club arranged by their position first in reverse alphabetical order (descending) and then by their last_name in alphabetical order. The syntax for this SQL statement is below. Notice that the DESC keyword appears next to the position field in the ORDER BY clause because that is the field we wish to have sorted in descending order.
SELECT first_name, last_name, position FROM Member ORDER BY position DESC, last_name

The results of this query appear in . Notice that the rows of the results table are arranged by position in reverse alphabetical order. This is the primary sorting field. When the members’ positions are the same, the members are arranged in alphabetical order by their last name. This is the secondary sorting field. The secondary sorting field will only apply when the values of the primary sorting field repeat.

figure.title: Query Resulting from Sorting on Multiple Fields.

section.para: Additional instructions and examples for the ORDER BY clause can be found in the PostgreSQL documentation at Sorting Rows.

section.title: LIMIT Options
section.para: Often we do not wish to see all of the rows from a table in our query result. One way to reduce the number of rows in a query result is to use the LIMIT clause. Note that LIMIT is not a standard SQL clause and that there are analogous ways to reduce the number of rows in a query result for other DBMS.

The LIMIT clause of a SQL query is included at the end of the SQL statement. The LIMIT clause An additional SQL clause that can be used in a SQL query; it starts with the keyword LIMIT and specifies this number of rows to include in the query result. starts with the keyword LIMIT and specifies this number of rows to include in the query result. For example, if we wish to see only five rows from the member table, we would add a LIMIT clause to the SQL statement, as shown below. The results of this query are found in . Notice that only the first five rows of the query result are displayed.
SELECT first_name, last_name FROM Member LIMIT 5

One important way we can use the LIMIT clause is to see only the top (or bottom) rows of a table according to the values on a specific field. To accomplish this, we will first appropriately sort the query result by the values on that field and then apply the LIMIT clause to only view the required number of rows.

For example, we may wish to see the four biggest expenses incurred by the club. The syntax for this SQL statement is below. Notice that we first sort the rows on the Expense table by cost in descending order. This will arrange the highest cost item first in the query result. Each subsequent row of the query will include an item in which the cost is less than the previous row. Therefore, by limiting the results to four rows, we will only see the four most expensive expense items for the club (see ).
SELECT * FROM Expense ORDER BY cost DESC LIMIT 4

figure.title: Query Result with ORDER BY and LIMIT.

section.para: More information and examples about the LIMIT clause can be found in the PostgreSQL documentation here: LIMIT and OFFSET

section.title: Test Your Skills: Introduction to SQL
What Is the WHERE Clause?
section.para: In section , we discussed the LIMIT clause that reduces the number of rows returned in a query result. When a LIMIT clause is used, the DBMS returns the specified number of rows starting with the first row in a query result. In this lesson, we will discuss a much more powerful and focused way to reduce the number of rows returned in a query result. This approach uses the WHERE clause of the SQL statement.

The WHERE clause in a SQL statement is used to restrict the results of a query to only those rows that meet specified criteria. These criteria are applied to the values of the fields. For example, we may wish to view only those members who have the position of "President". There is only one member who meets this criterion, Sacha Harrison (see ). All other members will be excluded from the query result because they do not meet this criterion.

The WHERE clause appears in the SQL statement after the FROM clause. The general syntax of a WHERE clause is the keyword WHERE followed by the field to which we wish to apply the criterion, a mathematical comparison operator, and the value restriction we wish to add to that field. The simplest form of this syntax is WHERE fieldname = value. The SQL statement used for our example in is below. Notice the syntax of the WHERE clause. Here we are restricting the query results to those members whose position equals "President".
SELECT first_name, last_name, position
FROM member
WHERE position = 'President'

Most WHERE clause conditions will follow the pattern in the example: field, comparison operator, value. There are cases when we need to restrict our query results by more than one criterion. In other cases, we may not know the exact values that we want to use in the restriction.

In this lesson, we will first discuss the comparison operators. We will then cover how to include multiple criteria in our WHERE clauses. Finally, we will outline how to use criterion that incorporate wildcard characters to provide flexibility in the values used to restrict query results.

section.title: Comparison Operators
section.para: The general form of the WHERE clause prescribes a matching of a field, a comparison operator, and a value. You can use any field on the table specified in the FROM clause as part of a WHERE clause whether the field appears in the SELECT clause or not. For example, you could write a query that lists the first name only of all members whose last name is "Smith". The syntax of this query is below. The results are depicted in . Notice that the query successfully returns the only member whose last name is "Smith" and shows only the first name of that member.
SELECT first_name
FROM Member
WHERE last_name = 'Smith'

figure.title: WHERE Clause with Field Not in the SELECT Clause.

section.para: You may also notice something else about the syntax of the WHERE clause in our example. Notice the single quotes (') around the value of 'Smith'. When we use a text field in our criterion, we place single quotes around the value part of the clause.

If the desired value in the comparison is a number, we do not use quotes. We also do not use any formatting of the number—even if the number is generally formatted (such as money with a dollar sign). For example, if we were interested in displaying only those expenses that cost $122.06, our WHERE clause would be: WHERE cost = 122.06 (see ).

With practice, you will remember these rules governing how to use text and numbers as values in a WHERE clause. Also, with practice, you will feel comfortable using the different comparison operators that can be used in a WHERE clause. The comparison operators that can be used in a WHERE clause are shown in .

tr.td: Returns all records larger than the specified value
Returns all records that are at least as large as the specified value (including the value)
Returns all records smaller than the specified value
Returns all records that are at least as small as the specified value (including the value)
Returns all records that are exactly equal to the specified value
Returns all records that are not equal to the specified value

section.para: Up to this point, we have used the "=" operator. This operator is used when we want to ensure that the rows in the query result contain exactly the specified value for the specified field. The syntax "WHERE cost = 122.06" will return only those expenses where the cost is exactly $122.06.

We can also use the inequality operators < or > to create WHERE clause conditions that restrict rows based on ranges of values. For example, we could restrict expenses to those items that cost more than $100. The syntax for this restriction would be: WHERE cost > 100 (see )

figure.title: Criterion with a Greater Than Operator.

section.para: We could also restrict expenses to those that are less than $50. The syntax for this restriction would be: WHERE cost < 50 (see ).

figure.title: Criterion with a Less Than Operator.

section.para: Finally, we can use operators that combine equals (=) with one of the comparison operators (< or >) to create operators that include endpoints. For example, we may want to see all of the line items on the income table that are at least $200. The syntax for the WHERE clause in this example would be WHERE amount >= 200. shows the results of this SQL statement. Notice that the rows included in this query result include those where the amount is greater than $200. It also includes the row where the amount is equal to $200.

figure.title: Query Result with Greater Than or Equal to Operator.

section.title: Working with NULL Values
section.para: From time to time, you will find that a cell on a database table is empty. We call this value a NULL value. A NULL value is different than the value zero or even a blank space. A NULL value is an empty value.

In many cases, it is not desirable for a cell to contain a NULL value. When adding a new member to the student organization database, we generally would want to have a value for each of the fields on that table (name, phone number, email address, etc.). There are times, however, when the information for a particular field is unknown. When this happens, we use a NULL value for the field. For example, Randy Woodard has not yet declared a major. To show this, we use a NULL value in the link_to_major field (see ).

figure.title: Member with a NULL Value for the link_to_major Field.

section.para: It is impossible to use the standard comparison operators (=, <, >, <=, or >=) to detect a NULL value. The syntax 'WHERE link_to_value = NULL' will not work. Instead, we use a different operator that is specific to finding NULL values. The syntax 'IS NULL' is used in the WHERE clause to restrict a query to find NULL values for a field. To return those members who have not yet declared a major (those with a NULL value for the link_to_major field), we would use this syntax in the WHERE clause, 'WHERE link_to_major IS NULL' (see ).

figure.title: Query with the IS NULL Operator.

section.para: A similar SQL statement will return all of the events that contain a NULL value for the notes field. The syntax for the WHERE clause for this query is WHERE notes IS NULL. The results of this query can be seen in .

figure.title: Events with a NULL Value for the Notes Field.

section.para: There are also times when we wish to see all of the rows on a table that do not contain a NULL value for a particular field. The SQL statement used to return all rows without a NULL value for a field is IS NOT NULL. Suppose we wish to see all of the events with a non-NULL value for the notes field. The syntax for the WHERE clause in this example is WHERE notes IS NOT NULL. Notice that in , all of the rows listed in the results for this query contain a value for the notes field.

figure.title: Events with Non-NULL Values for the Notes Field.

section.title: WHERE Clauses with NULL Values Practice
Compound WHERE Clauses
section.para: It is also possible to create a WHERE clause that includes more than one criterion. For example, we may wish to see all income items where the amount is between $100 and $1,000. In this case, we would need two WHERE clause criteria: amount >=100 and amount <=1000 (see ). Notice that only the rows that meet both criteria are included in the query results. We combine criteria in a WHERE clause using the operators AND and OR.

figure.title: Query with a Compound WHERE Clause.

section.para: The AND operator is used to combine criteria when both conditions must be met for a row to be included in the query result. Notice that when you use the AND operator to combine criteria in a WHERE clause, the keyword AND appears between the criteria being combined. Consider another example. Assume we wish to see all items on the expense table that cost more than $20.20 and less than $50.13. The WHERE clause for this query would be WHERE cost >=20.2 AND cost <=50.13 (see ).

figure.title: WHERE Clause with the AND Operator.

section.para: There is a shorthand way to write a query that selects values within a range for the same field. This method involves using the keyword BETWEEN. The conditions "WHERE cost >=20.2 AND cost <=50.13" can be rewritten as "WHERE cost BETWEEN 20.2 AND 50.13" (see ). BETWEEN implies that the endpoints are included in the criteria (>=20.2 and <=50.13). The result will be the same for either approach.

We can also combine criteria that are applied to different fields. For example, we may wish to see all expenses for Pizza (expense_description = 'Pizza') that cost more than $100. The WHERE clause that would produce this result is WHERE cost > 100 AND expense_description = 'Pizza' (see ).

section.title: The OR Operator
section.para: The OR operator is used to combine criteria when we want our results to match any of the criteria. The syntax for using the OR operator is the same as the syntax for using the AND operator. The keyword OR is included between the criteria being combined. If we wish to write a query that displays the names of members with a small or medium shirt size, the syntax of the WHERE clause for this query would be WHERE t_shirt_size = 'Small' OR t_shirt_size = 'Medium' (see ).

figure.title: WHERE Clause with the OR Operator.

section.para: Note that the AND operator would not return the rows that we want for this query. There are no members with both a small and medium shirt size. Instead, we want to return members for which either of these conditions is true. The OR operator accomplishes that.

The query in can be rewritten in a simpler form using the IN operator. The syntax for the IN operator is WHERE fieldname IN (list of values separated by commas). The WHERE clause "WHERE t_shirt_size = 'Small' OR t_shirt_size = 'Medium'" could be rewritten as WHERE t_shirt_size IN ('Small', 'Medium'). displays the results of this query. Notice that the results are the same as in the previous example.

figure.title: WHERE Clause Using the IN Operator.

section.para: We can combine criteria based on different fields using the OR operator. For example, we may wish to see all members with the first name Amy or the last name Sanders. The WHERE clause for this SQL statement would be WHERE first_name = 'Amy' OR last_name = 'Sanders' (see ).
More information and examples about using the AND and OR operators can be found in the PostgreSQL documentation at Logical Operators.

section.title: Combining AND and OR
section.para: A single WHERE clause can contain a combination of AND and OR operators to create a sophisticated set of conditions. There is a standard order in which combined AND and OR operators will be processed, much like the order of operations you learned in elementary school. AND operators will be processed before OR operators. If you would like an OR operator to execute before an AND operator in the same WHERE clause, you can use parentheses around the operators you would like to execute first, just like using parentheses in the order of operations for math. Consider a few examples:

listitem.para:
WHERE color = 'blue' AND size = 'Large' OR size = 'Medium'
This syntax will return those rows that contain large blue items and medium items of any color.

WHERE color = 'blue' AND (size = 'Large' OR size = 'Medium')
This syntax will return those rows that contain large and medium items, all of which will be blue.

section.title: The NOT Operator
section.para: The NOT operator can also be helpful in constructing a WHERE clause. As you recall, we discussed NOT in our conversation about NULL in the previous section. The NOT operator can be used to create a condition that reverses the logic of any condition. The syntax for the NOT operator is NOT(condition we wish to reverse).

Often it is simpler to define a condition based on what it does not contain rather than what it does contain. For example, we may wish to return all of the members with a t_shirt_size other than small. The following WHERE clause would accomplish this objective: WHERE t_shirt_size IN ('Medium', 'Large', 'X-Large'). We can accomplish the same thing using the NOT operator (see ). An equivalent WHERE clause using the NOT operator would be WHERE NOT(t_shirt_size = 'Small').

section.title: Working with Date Fields in a WHERE Clause

videodata.be:name: Working with Dates Fields in a Where Clause.mp4

section.para: We often use date information in WHERE clause restrictions to help us find specific rows in our data. Date information can be a little tricky to work with in a WHERE clause because it is generally stored as both date and time information. Take, for example, the event_date field on the event table in the student organization database (see ).

Notice the date information for the March meeting. It is stored as 2020-03-10T12:00:00. This data is a combination of the day of the March meeting, 2020-03-10 (March 10, 2020), and the time of the meeting, 12:00:00 (noon). The 'T' character in the data delineates the end of the day part of the date and the beginning of the time part of the date.

Even the formatting of the day and time part of the data is somewhat cumbersome. The day part is formatted with a four-digit year, a two-digit month, and a two-digit day, all separated by a '-' character. The time element of the data is formatted with two digits for the hour, two for the minute, and two for the second, all separated with a ':' character. We could even add a decimal after the seconds to precisely store a time that includes fractions of a second.

While this format for date and time is very precise and follows an exact standard, it can be difficult to write WHERE clause restrictions that effectively select rows based on date information. Consider that we may wish to see all events that happened on March 10th of 2020 (see ). The logical WHERE clause SQL statement to retrieve these events would look something like: WHERE event_date = '2020-03-10' (notice that I even formatted 3/10/2020 as it appears in the event_date field on the table).

figure.title: Incorrect Syntax for Date Field in WHERE Clause.

section.para: The reason for this is that in order to return a specific date in a query result, we also need to account for the particular point in time that is associated with the row we wish to see. When we omit the time part of the date from our WHERE clause condition, it is as if we are saying that we are looking for events that occurred at midnight on that date. To see an event that occurred at noon on 3/10/2020, we would need to include the time, 12:00:00, as part of the where condition. The query depicted in demonstrates how this might work.

figure.title: Events that Occurred at Noon on 3/10/2020.

section.para: Of course, this approach would not return all of the events that happened on 3/10/2020. It will only return those events that happened at noon on 3/10/2020. If we wish to see all of the events that happened at any time during the day on 3/10/2020, we would need to use a WHERE clause that searches a range of values. The WHERE clause that would return all of the events that occurred at any time during the day on 3/10/2020 would be WHERE event_date >= '2020-03-10' AND event_date < '2020-03-11' (see ).

figure.title: Events that Occurred at Any Time on 3/10/2020.

section.para: Notice the elements of this WHERE clause. Recall that when the time part of a date is omitted from a WHERE condition, it is as if we are searching for midnight on that day. The first part of the WHERE clause, event_date >= '2020-03-10', means that we are looking for events that happen at midnight or after on 3/10/2020. The last part, event_date < '2020-03-11', means that we are looking for events that happened before midnight on 3/11/2020. Therefore, the WHERE clause literally means that we want to see all events that happened at midnight on 3/10/2020 or after and before midnight on 3/11/2020.

If we wished to return all events that happened in the month of February of 2020, we might use this WHERE clause: WHERE event_date >= '2020-02-01' AND event_date < '2020-03-01' (see ). This statement means that we wish to see all events that happened at midnight or later on 2/1/2020 and before midnight on 3/1/2020.

figure.title: Events that Occurred during February of 2020.

section.title: Using Date Functions to Simplify Working with Dates
section.para: An alternative approach to working with dates involves using predefined functions. Database management systems, including PostgreSQL, have a number of functions that perform calculations in queries. Some functions perform calculations on every row of the query result, and others perform summary calculations across the rows of a query result. While most of these functions are beyond the scope of this learning resource, we will discuss the EXTRACT function, which we can use to pull the year, month, or day out of a date value.

The syntax for using a function as part of a SQL statement is much like the syntax for using a function in Excel. We invoke a function using the name of the function and then list the parameters for the function in parentheses directly following the function name: function_name(function_arguments). The EXTRACT function follows a slightly different pattern: EXTRACT(field FROM source), where field is the part of the date we want (such as YEAR, MONTH, or DAY) and source is the date value. A function can be used in the SELECT clause of a query or in the WHERE clause, though we will focus our instruction on using the EXTRACT function as part of the WHERE clause.

EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ...) is used to pull the 'year' element out of a date field for each row of a query result. The result is a four-digit number that represents the year. For example, we may wish to select all events that occurred during the year 2019 (see ). The syntax for the WHERE clause for this SQL statement would be WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM event_date) = 2019. Notice that the only events returned occurred during 2019.

EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ...) pulls the 'month' part out of a date field for each row of a query result. The result is a number between 1 and 12 that represents the month. For example, we may wish to select all events that occurred during the month of November (see ). The syntax for the WHERE clause for this SQL statement would be: WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM event_date) = 11. In this case, all of the events returned will have occurred in November (month = 11).

The 'day' part of a date is pulled out using EXTRACT(DAY FROM ...). The result is a number between 1 and 31 that represents the day. Consider that we may wish to select all events that occurred on the 12th day of the month (see ). The syntax for the WHERE clause for this SQL statement would be WHERE EXTRACT(DAY FROM event_date) = 12. All of the events returned by this SQL statement occurred on the 12th day of the month. Notice that while each of these events occurred on the 12th of the month, they occurred during different months and even different years.

If we wanted to limit our results to those events that occurred on a specific date, we must use a compound WHERE clause. If we wanted to return those events that happened on March 10, 2020, we would use the following syntax in the SQL statement: WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM event_date) = 3 AND EXTRACT(DAY FROM event_date) = 10 AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM event_date) = 2020 (see ). Notice that this will provide the same result as the following WHERE clause: WHERE event_date >= '2020-03-10' and event_date < '2020-03-11'.

We can use EXTRACT with year, month, and day in any combination to return results that contain any dates we wish. If we wanted to see those events that occurred during the month of February of 2020, the SQL statement would be WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM event_date) = 2 AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM event_date) = 2020 (see ).

figure.title: Events that Occurred during February of 2020.

section.para: Working with dates values in a WHERE clause condition can be a bit tedious. In this section, we described two approaches to creating conditions with dates. As you write WHERE clause conditions that involve date values, you will likely find that one of these approaches is more comfortable than the other. Either approach should prove to be equally effective.

section.title: Wildcards
section.para: You may sometimes need to write a SQL statement to restrict rows, but you may not know which values to return. Notice the unique values for expense_description returned from the query depicted in . Suppose we want to write a query to produce the expenses that include water.

figure.title: Unique Values for the expense_description Field.

section.para: Unfortunately, expenses for water appear in the same row as other items, such as cookies or supplies. The WHERE clause "WHERE expense_description = 'Water'" will not produce any results because none of the rows of the table have the exact value 'Water' (see ).

figure.title: No Records Contain an expense_description of Exactly 'Water'.

section.para: The good news is that we can write a query that will return any rows of the table where the expense_description contains the word 'Water', even if there are other letters or words in the same value. To do this, we use a WHERE clause with wildcards.

A WHERE clause with a wildcard criterion uses the keyword LIKE and a combination of normal and wildcard characters. There are two types of wildcard characters. The first uses the '%' (percent) character, which represents any combination of characters. The second is the '_' (underscore) character, which represents a single character. The syntax for a WHERE clause with a wildcard value is WHERE fieldname LIKE 'set of normal and wildcard characters'.

We use a WHERE clause with a wildcard to write a SQL statement that will return the rows with an expense_description that contains the word 'Water'. The syntax for the WHERE clause of this query would be WHERE expense_description LIKE '%Water%' (see ). Notice that all of the rows in the results contain the word 'Water' somewhere in the expense_description. All other rows are excluded from the query results.

figure.title: WHERE Clause with a Wildcard Criteria.

section.para: The positioning of the '%' characters is significant. Placing the '%' characters both before and after the word 'Water' means that we are looking for the characters 'Water' (in that order) anywhere in the expense_description for the rows that are included in the query result. If we were to only put the '%' before 'Water', we would be looking for the word 'Water' at the end of the expense_description (any set of characters before 'Water' and nothing after). If we were to put the '%' after 'Water', we would be looking for the word 'Water' as the first word of the expense_description (nothing before 'Water' and any set of characters after).
Consider some additional examples for using the '%' wildcard.

listitem.para: '%we' would return 'awe', but not 'awesome' or 'welcome'
'we%' would return 'welcome', but not 'awe' or 'awesome'
'%we%' would return 'awe', 'welcome', or 'awesome'

section.para: The underscore '_' works in a similar way. The '_' character will substitute for any single character. For example, we might know that there are two members in our student organization database whose first names start with the letters 'Adel' but whose names end with different characters. We may want to construct a query to display both members. The syntax for the WHERE clause of this query would be: WHERE first_name LIKE 'Adel_' (see ). Notice that only those members whose first name starts with the letters 'Adel' and contains exactly one additional character are returned. If there were a member with the first name 'Adella', that person would not be in the query results because the '_' character can only substitute for a single character.

figure.title: WHERE Clause with the Underscore Wildcard Character.

section.para: Examine a few more examples of using the '_' wildcard character:

listitem.para: 't_p' would return 'tip', 'tap', or 'top; but not 'stop'
'p_st' would return 'past', but not 'paste'
'h_ _s_n' would return 'hanson', 'hensen', or 'hansen'; but not 'harrison' (note that 'h%s_n' would return 'harrison')

section.para: Additional information and examples about using wildcard characters in the WHERE clause can be found in the PostgreSQL documentation at Pattern Matching: LIKE.

section.title: Wildcards in the WHERE Clause Practice
Test Your Skills: Queries with Restrictions

chapter.title: Joining Multiple Tables in the Same Query

section.title: What Is a JOIN?

listitem.para: Understand the difference between primary and foreign keys.
Understand and be confident writing and running SQL with a JOIN clause.
Know how to join three or more tables using JOIN clauses.
Know how to use fields from different joined tables in the same WHERE clause.

section.para: You will recall that the FROM clause of a SQL statement is used to define which table will be used as the source of the fields in the query result. It is often the case that you will need fields from more than one table to be included together in the same query result. When we include fields from more than one table in the same query result, we call that a JOIN query Queries that include fields from more than one table in the same query result..

section.title: How Does a JOIN work?
section.para: In order to understand how a JOIN query works, we first need to understand what primary and foreign keys are. A primary key is a field on a database table that uniquely identifies each row of the table. You are probably familiar with the concept of a student ID at your university. Your student ID is a form of primary key because it uniquely identifies you as a student to your university. No other student at the university has your student ID, and you only have one student ID.

In much the same way, the Major table in the student organization database has a primary key. The primary key field is the major_id. Note that in , each of the different majors has a unique value for the major_id. No two majors have the same major_id, and no major has more than one major_id. Because of this, we can use the major_id to uniquely identify each of the majors on the major table.

A foreign key is another special field on a database table. A foreign key is used to combine the rows from one table with the rows of another table. A foreign key is generally a primary key from another related table. For example, there is a foreign key field on the member table of the student organization database that links the rows from the member table with corresponding rows from the major table (see ).

The link_to_major field on the member table contains the values for the major_id from the major table that map each member to their individual major. Because of this, we can map each member with their major. Angela Sanders is a Business major, Grant Gilmore is an Electrical Engineering major, and Luisa Guidi is a Civil Engineering major. We know this because the link_to_major and major_id fields pair members with majors. Note the link_to_major value for Angela Sanders. It corresponds with the major_id for the Business major on the major table.

We join tables together using these primary and foreign key pairings. We can only directly join tables that have primary and foreign key pairs. If we need fields from two tables that are not directly related through primary and foreign key pairs, we may need to involve additional tables until a chain of related tables can be completed. The diagram in shows such a situation. We cannot directly link members to the events that they have attended because this is not a field on the member table that links directly to the events table, and there is not a field on the events table that links directly to the member table. In order to link members to events, we need to involve the attendance table.

figure.title: Linking Tables through Primary and Foreign Keys.

imagedata.alt: Lists for Income, Zip code, Member, Major, Attendance, Expense, Event, and Budget. Connected lines from entries in one list to entries in another list.

section.para: Using primary and foreign keys, we can generally combine any table in a database with another table if we involve enough intermediate tables.

section.title: Types of Joins
section.para: Now that we understand how primary and foreign keys work together to link rows from one table to rows of a related table, we can now discuss how those linkages allow us to combine records in a joined query. There are essentially three types of joins that we use for this purpose. Each join uses primary and foreign key pairings in a slightly different way to join tables together.

The first type of join is called an inner join A join that ensures that all of the rows from both tables included in the query result match up. . An inner join ensures that all of the rows from both tables included in the query result match up. If there is a row on one table that does not match with a row on the related table, it will be excluded from the query result when an inner join is used. For example, Randy Woodward does not have a value for the link_to_major field. This is likely because Randy has not yet declared a major. When we use an inner join to combine the member and major tables together, Randy would not appear in the query results because Randy does not match with a major (see ).

iframe.alt: Three columns and a single entry.

section.para: Inner joins are the most common type of join. Because of this, we will not discuss the other joins in much detail. The other types of joins are partial outer joins, called left and right joins, and full outer joins. All of the outer join types allow us to include rows from a database table that do not match with rows from the related table in a query result. While these types of joins can be interesting, they are beyond the scope of this discussion.

section.title: How to Create a Join Query
section.para: We join tables together in the FROM clause. We use the keyword JOIN to define which tables will be combined in the query result and the keyword ON to specify the primary and foreign key pairs that will be used to match the rows of the tables together. The general syntax for a join is FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.keyfield = table2.keyfield. Consider the FROM clause of a SQL statement that combines the major and member tables together: FROM major JOIN member ON major.major_id = member.link_to_major. contains the results of a query that joins these tables together.

figure.title: The Major and Member Tables Joined in the Same Query.

section.para: Once tables are successfully joined together in the FROM clause, we can use any of the fields from the combined tables in the other clauses of our SQL statements. For example, we can display the last_name field from the member table with the college field from the major table to show which members have majors in each college. The syntax for this query is below:
SELECT last_name, college
FROM major JOIN member ON major.major_id = member.link_to_major
The results of this query are shown in .

figure.title: Members with the College for Their Majors.

section.title: Joining Three or More Tables
section.para: We have already discussed that it may be necessary to join more than two tables together in order to include all of the fields we need in a query result. While the syntax needed to join more than two tables together may seem complicated, it is really not much more difficult than joining two tables together.

The general syntax for joining three tables together is FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.keyfield = table2.keyfield JOIN table3 ON table2.keyfield = table3.keyfield. Notice that table1 and table2 are joined just as they were when we joined two tables together. Once table1 and table2 are joined together, they are immediately joined with table3 based on primary and foreign key relationships that exist between table2 and table3. For example, if we wish to see the first name, state, and major for each member in our database, the syntax would be as follows:
SELECT first_name, major_name, state
FROM major JOIN member on major.major_id = member.link_to_member
JOIN zip_code on member.zip = zip_code.zip_code

The query results for this SQL statement are in . Notice that the first_name field is on the member table, the major_name is on the major table, and the state field is on the zip_code table. We can seamlessly use all three of these fields in the same query once the three tables are joined together.

figure.title: Joining the Major, Member, and Zip_code Tables.

section.para: We can join any number of tables together in the same query. The syntax for joining four tables together is below:
FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.keyfield = table2.keyfield JOIN table3 ON table2.keyfield = table3.keyfield JOIN table4 ON table3.keyfield = table4.keyfield.
The syntax for joining five tables together is below:

FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.keyfield = table2.keyfield JOIN table3 ON table2.keyfield = table3.keyfield JOIN table4 ON table3.keyfield = table4.keyfield JOIN table5 ON table4.keyfield = table5.keyfield.
Notice that joining any number of tables together in a query result involves joining the next table with the combined result of the tables that have already been joined.

section.title: When a Field Name Is in More than One Joined Table
section.para: When joining tables together, you will occasionally find that you join two tables that have fields with the same name. For example, the income and event tables in the student club database both have a field named "notes" (see ). This presents a problem if we wish to display either of the notes fields in a query result where the income and event tables are joined together. This is a problem because the query processing engine does not know whether to display the values for the notes field from the income table or the event table.

To fix this problem, we must fully specify in the SELECT clause which table to source the notes field from. To fully specify the table associated with a field in the SELECT clause, we use the following syntax:

SELECT tablename.fieldname FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.keyfield=table2.keyfield.
Note that tablename refers to either table1 or table2 depending on which table has the field that we wish to display in the query result.
If we want to display the notes field from the event table, we use the following syntax:

SELECT event.notes FROM income JOIN member ON income.link_to_member = member.member_id JOIN attendance ON member.member_id=attendance.link_to_member JOIN event ON event.event_id = attendance.link_to_event
Notice that we fully specify that the notes field comes from the event table.

You may fully specify the table source for any field you wish to include in the SELECT clause of a query. However, if you wish to include a field that has the same name as a field in another table that was joined in the FROM clause of your query, you must fully specify the table source in a query.
For example:
The syntax "SELECT notes FROM income" is valid. There is no need to specify the source for the notes field because only the income table is in the query.
The syntax "SELECT income.notes FROM income" is valid. Though it is not necessary to specify the source of the notes field, it is ok to do so.

The syntax "SELECT event.notes FROM income ...JOIN event ON event.event_id = attendance.link_to_event" is valid (assuming the ... includes the syntax for eventually joining the event and income tables together). We must specify the source of the notes field in this case because the income and event tables are joined together, and both include a field named notes.

The syntax "SELECT notes FROM income ... JOIN event ON event.event_id = attendance.link_to_event" is not valid because both the income and event tables, which are joined in the query, have notes fields.

section.title: Joining Three or More Tables Practice
Using Fields from Different Joined Tables in the Same WHERE Clause
section.para: You have no doubt noticed that using fields from different joined tables in the SELECT clause does not require any special syntax. The only exception to this would occur if two different fields from different tables that are joined together have exactly the same name (this is a fairly common practice, so it bears noting in this discussion). When this occurs, we must specify which table we wish the field to come from in the SELECT clause. For example, if the link_to_major field on the member table were actually named major_id on both the major and the member table, we would need to specify which table we want the field to come from if we use major_id in the SELECT clause. The syntax for this would be as follows:
SELECT first_name, major.major_id
FROM major JOIN member ON major.major_id = member.major_id

Note that to specify which table we wish the major_id to come from in the SELECT clause, we just need to add the tablename with a period before the major_id. While we could technically specify the table needed for any field in the SELECT clause the same way (for example, member.first_name is valid syntax), we only need to do this if a fieldname is the same on more than one table joined in the query.

Once a field is available in the query (because the table that contains the field is in the FROM clause), it can be used in any of the other clauses of the SQL statement. Suppose we want to see all of the members from New York (short_state = 'NY') who are engineering majors (college = 'College of Engineering'). The syntax for this query would be as follows:
SELECT first_name, last_name, college, state
FROM major JOIN member ON major.major_id = member.link_to_major
JOIN zip_code ON member.zip = zip_code.zip_code
WHERE college = 'College of Engineering' and short_state = 'NY'
The results of the query are found in .

figure.title: Query with Fields from Two Tables in the WHERE Clause.

section.para: More information and examples about using joins are available in the PostgreSQL documentation: Joined Tables.

section.title: Queries with JOIN and WHERE Practice

section.label: A.1
section.title: Printing in Excel

note.title: Unavailable Options
note.para: The Online version of Excel has limited options for printing worksheet data. To learn how to change other layout options, switch your textbook to display either the Windows or Mac version of Excel and practice using a desktop version of Excel.

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Advanced Options link displayed on right side of MyEducator window.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

imagedata.alt: Radio buttons next to three options. Top option: Excel for Windows. Next option: Excel for Macintosh. Third option: Excel Online.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

section.para: On occasion, you may find it necessary to print a report from a worksheet. You can format worksheets to make them more printer-friendly by managing the Page Layout menu tab options of the worksheet. demonstrates the process for setting the margins and page orientation for a worksheet. Margins refer to the amount of blank space that appears around the outside of a printed document. You define the margins for a worksheet using the Margins menu list in the Page Setup group of the Page Layout menu tab. Notice that you have a number of predefined options for page margins but that you can also customize the page margins by clicking on Custom Margins… on the Margins menu list.
On occasion, you may find it necessary to print a report from a worksheet. You can format worksheets to make them more printer-friendly by managing the Page Layout menu tab options of the worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Page margin options available on dropdown menu.
Page margin options available on dropdown menu.
Page orientation options for portrait and landscape displayed on dropdown.
Page orientation options for portrait and landscape displayed on dropdown.
Page orientation options for portrait and landscape displayed on dropdown.
Page Layout menu options displayed in ribbon.

section.para: Page orientation The layout of the paper; either portrait, with the widest part of the page running from the top to the bottom, or landscape, with the widest part of the page running from side to side. refers to the layout of the paper when the worksheet is printed. PortraitA page orientation with the widest part running from the top to the bottom. orientation means that the worksheet will be printed on the page with the widest part of the page running from the top to the bottom. LandscapeA page orientation with the widest part of the page running from side to side. orientation means that the worksheet will be printed with the widest part of the page running from side to side.
You can change the page orientation of a worksheet by clicking on the Orientation menu list in the Page Setup group of the Page Layout menu tab.

You can change the page orientation of a worksheet by clicking on the Page Setup option in the Page Setup group of the Page Layout menu tab. A new window will be displayed on the right side of the screen with options to change the orientation.

imagedata.alt: Page orientation options for portrait and landscape displayed on dropdown.

section.para: The reports you generate using spreadsheets can often span multiple pages. You may wish to repeat some elements of the report, like a title or page numbers, across all pages. This is done by defining page headers and footers (). To define report headers and footers, click on Print Titles in the Page Setup group of the Page Layout menu tab. This will open the Sheet tab of the Page Setup dialog window. Switch to the Header/Footer tab. You can use the drop-down menus below Header: and Footer: to add commonly used elements (such as page numbers) to your reports. Notice that you can also define custom headers and footers.

figure.label: Figure A.2
figure.title: Repeating Report Elements across Multiple Printed Pages.

imagedata.alt: Print title options available on the Page Setup dialog box.
Print title options available on the Page Setup dialog box.

informalfigure.title: Sheet tab on the Page Setup window.

imagedata.alt: Sheet tab of the Page Setup dialog box.
Sheet tab of the Page Setup dialog box.

informalfigure.title: Header/Footer tab on the Page Setup window.

imagedata.alt: Header and Footer tab of the Page Setup dialog box.
Header and Footer tab of the Page Setup dialog box.

section.para: You may also find that you need to print a large data table that will span multiple pages. When this is the case, it is helpful for the reader if you repeat the column or row labels on each page of the report. You can define rows or columns to repeat on a printout in the Page Setup dialog window (see ).

You may also find that there are parts of your worksheets that you do not wish to include in a report printout. You can define the parts of the worksheet that you want to print by setting the Print Area of the worksheet. As the name implies, the print area is the set of consecutive cells in a worksheet that will be printed. Anything outside of the print area will not be sent to the printer when the print command is given. To define the print area of a worksheet, select the range of cells you would like to include in the print area and click Set Print Area, which is found after clicking the Print Area button in the Page Setup group of the Page Layout menu tab (). A worksheet can only have one print area defined at a time. If you need to print multiple areas in the same worksheet, you will need to add those areas to the print area using the Add to Print Area option. Notice that the Add to Print Area option is only available if a print area is already defined.

figure.label: Figure A.3
figure.title: Defining the Print Area of a worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Print Area dropdown options.
Print Area dropdown options.
Print Area dropdown options.

section.para: Printing can also be done by selecting the File menu option and choosing Print and then selecting Print again in the new pane. The Print Settings dialog box displays the options to set how much of the workbook you want to print. You can print the Active Sheet, the Entire Workbook, or just the Current Selection.

imagedata.alt: Print settings dialog box.

section.title: Using Formula Auditing Features

note.title: Unavailable Options
note.para: The formula auditing features Trace Precedents and Trace Dependents are currently not available in the online version of Excel. To learn about these features, switch your textbook to display either the Windows or Mac version of Excel and practice using a desktop version of Excel.
To switch to a desktop version:

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Advanced options displayed on the right pane of the window.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

imagedata.alt: Versions of book available.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

note.title: Resource File
note.para: Download the file from the link below to follow along with the text example or video and to practice on your own.

section.para: Despite our best intentions, we will all make errors in our spreadsheet formulas and functions. Unfortunately, the errors we make are not always evident or easy to track down. Often the best way to discover the source of an error is to visually inspect the formulas and functions we have created. This is usually done by clicking on individual cells and examining one formula at a time.

Excel has some good troubleshooting tools to aid in the process of identifying and correcting errors. Specifically, the Trace Precedents feature allows you to see which cells are referenced in a particular formula or function. The Trace Dependents feature allows you to see which other cells reference a particular cell. Finally, the Show Formulas feature displays the functions and formulas used in each cell of a worksheet instead of the formula or function results. The figures below outline how to access each feature and demonstrate how each works.
The troubleshooting features of Excel are available in the Formula Auditing section of the Formulas tab.

figure.label: Figure B.1
figure.title: Trace Precedents, Trace Dependents, and Show Formulas.

imagedata.alt: Formula menu with Formula Auditing section identified by a red box.
Formula menu with Formula Auditing section identified by a red box.

section.para: To see which cells are referenced by a formula or function, click on the cell where the calculation is stored. Once that cell is selected, click the Trace Precedents button. Excel will insert arrows pointing from the cells that are referenced by a formula or function to the cell that stores the formula/function (). This feature allows you to see if the formulas/functions you use reference the correct cells. This is particularly useful if you have a function or formula that is not behaving as you expect because you can work backward to find the source of your error.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet with trace precedents turned on for cell C7. Blue arrows point to cell C7 from cells B1 and B7 as the cells used in C7.

section.para: You can trace dependents in a similar fashion. To find which formulas or functions reference a particular cell, select the cell and click Trace Dependents. As demonstrates, Excel inserts arrows that point from the selected cell to the functions and formulas that reference that cell. This feature is particularly useful if you wish to determine which cells depend upon a particular value. In the case of , we can use the Trace Dependents feature to easily track which cells reference the sales tax rate.

imagedata.alt: Blue arrows point form cell B1 to cell B4 through B11 because there is a reference to that cell in all the other cells.

section.para: Both the Trace Precedents and Trace Dependents features insert arrows into the spreadsheet. To remove the arrows, click the Remove Arrows button in the Formula Auditing section of the Formulas tab.

At times it is helpful to review the syntax of more than one function or formula at the same time. Excel allows you to display the values that result from the formulas and functions in a worksheet–this is the default option for Excel. By way of contrast, you can display the syntax for all of the functions and formulas used in a worksheet. This is done by clicking on the Show Formulas button, which is found under the Formula tab in the Formula Auditing group. As demonstrates, Excel automatically shows the complete formulas and functions used in the spreadsheet.

imagedata.alt: Worksheet displays the formulas in cells C4 through C11 and cells D4 through D11. Cell D12 displays the Sum function used to total the values in column D.

note.title: Practice
note.para: Use the Windows or Mac desktop version of Excel to complete this practice assignment.

section.label: C.1
section.title: Manually Adding and Editing Chart Data Series
section.para: In , we discussed how to use Excel to create charts to visually depict data. The easiest way to create a chart is to first select the data you wish to add to the chart and then use the charting menu items to insert the chart. In most cases, this approach will create the correct chart. There are times when Excel does not automatically create the right chart or when it is difficult to select all of the values needed before inserting the chart. In these situations, it is possible to add a data series once a chart is created.

Data series can be edited and added manually only in the desktop versions of Excel. While this feature will likely be added to Excel online in the future, tools for working with chart data series are very limited. In this appendix, we will demonstrate how some of the charts we created in can also be created by manually manipulating the chart data series.

section.label: C.2
section.title: Building a Column Chart
section.para: contains an example of a column chart that displays the percent of each education level attained for adults 25 years old or older in the United States.

figure.label: Figure C.1
figure.title: Education Attained in the United States.

imagedata.alt: Chart displaying the education attainment in the United States. Seven columns representing the percent of people that attained each level of education from less then 9th grade to graduate or professional degrees.

section.para: To create this chart, you first need to have the data typed in a worksheet (see ).

figure.label: Figure C.2
figure.title: Education Attainment Data in a Worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Data for the education attainment in the United States. Columns for male, female and the entire population of the dataset. Row data representing the percent of people that attained each level of education from less then 9th grade to graduate or professional degrees.

section.para: You then insert the chart into the worksheet by selecting a two-dimensional column chart from the Column menu item in the Charts group of the Insert menu tab (). While the chart used in this lesson is a two-dimensional column chart, you could choose any of the other column chart options and achieve a similar result. Notice that you can select from two-dimensional and three-dimensional options as well as 2-D and 3-D Bar charts.

imagedata.alt: Drop down of the column chart type displaying the types of charts available.
Drop down of the column chart type displaying the types of charts available.

section.para: Once we have selected the type of chart we wish to build, it is time to select the data for the chart. In most cases, Excel will try to automatically populate your chart when it is inserted into the worksheet. Excel populates a chart with any data that you might select before you insert the chart. While this lesson demonstrates the processes involved with specifying each element of your chart, you will likely find that if you highlight the right set of data in your worksheet before inserting your chart, Excel will automatically create a chart that is very close to your desired result.

In this case, we must specify the data to be used in the chart. First, you click on Select Data in the Data group of the Design tab of the Chart Tools menu options. The Select Data Source dialog window will be displayed.

In this case, we must specify the data to be used in the chart. First, you click on Select Data in the Data group of the Chart Design tab of the Chart menu options. The Select Data Source dialog window will be displayed.

imagedata.alt: Empty select data source dialog box.
Empty select data source dialog box.

section.para: The Select Data Source dialog window is divided into two areas where the Legend Entries (Series) and the Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels are defined. A data series A set of data that you want to include on your chart. is a set of data that you want to include on your chart.

To create the education chart above, we need to add a data series that includes the number of adults who attained each level of education. We click Add in the Legend Entries (Series) area to bring up the Edit Series dialog window. We can use the dialog window to add the education data. The name of this series, "Population", can be referenced in cell E5. The values for this series are contained in the range E6:E12. You will click OK to exit the Edit Series window and return to the Select Data Source window.

To create the education chart above, we need to add a data series that includes the number of adults who attained each level of education. Select Chart data range at the top of the window. Select the data values in range E6:E12 to add the education data. Notice that Excel also put this range in the "Y values" and added "Series1" to the "Legend entries (Series)". Next to Name this series, "Population", select Name and reference cell E5.

imagedata.alt: Edit Series dialog box with E5 as the series name and E6:E12 as the series values.
Legend Entries (series) for population
Legend Entries (series) for population

section.para: Next, we will define the horizontal axis labels to include each of the education levels stored in the range B6:B12. This is done by clicking Edit in the Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels area and entering the appropriate range in the Axis Labels dialog window. Again, you will click OK to return to the Select Data Source dialog window.

Next, we will define the horizontal axis labels to include each of the education levels stored in the range B6:B12. This is done by using the "Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels" box and selecting the range B6:B12.

imagedata.alt: Axis labels dialog box with B6:B12 entered.
Select Data Source dialog box with Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels defined.
Select Data Source dialog box with Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels defined.

section.para: After reviewing the data we have selected for the chart, we click OK to exit the Select Data Source dialog window. At this point, the appropriate data has been selected for the chart, and it is time to modify the title and data labels for the chart.
