imagedata.alt: Worksheet with data used in chart highlighted and column chart of the data.
Worksheet with data used in chart highlighted and column chart of the data.

section.para: The title of the chart should read “Education Attainment in the United States” (). To change the title of the chart, click on the title to select it. After the title is selected, click again on the title to edit the text. It is worth noting that you can edit the text that is displayed as the title and modify the size and other font properties using the menu items on the Home tab menu in Excel.

imagedata.alt: Column chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.

section.para: At this point, most of the editing you will need to do can be accomplished by selecting the chart you wish to change and then clicking on the large plus icon outside the top right corner of the chart. This icon will display the same menu items as the Add Chart Element menu item in the Design tab.

imagedata.alt: Plus icon shortcut for Add Chart Elements.

section.para: Finally, we will add data labels to the chart that display the percent of people who attained each education level near the top of each of the columns in the chart (). To do this, we click on the plus icon and select the menu triangle to the right of Data Labels. To place the labels near the top of each column, select the Outside End option.

Finally, we will add data labels to the chart that display the percent of people who attained each education level near the top of each of the columns in the chart (). To do this, we click on the Add Chart Element icon and select the menu triangle to the right of Data Labels. To place the labels above each column, select the Outside End option.

figure.label: Figure C.10
figure.title: Add Data Labels to the Columns.

imagedata.alt: Data Label options available for selected chart.
Data Label options available for selected chart.

Completed column chart that displays an approprate title, columns that represent the percent of the population that attains each level of education. Data labels are displayed on the outside end of the columns with the percent of each column.

section.label: C.3
section.title: Building a Stacked Column Chart
section.para: Stacked Column Charts A column chart that breaks the column bars into segments that represent subcategories that are consistent across the columns. are used to compare categories of items or the same category over time. However, a stacked column chart breaks the column bars into segments that represent subcategories that are consistent across the columns. For example, we could construct a stacked column chart to compare education attainment levels for different age groups. We could break each of the bars into segments that represent the percent of people who attained each education level in each age group. demonstrates what this chart might look like.

figure.label: Figure C.11
figure.title: Stacked Column Chart Comparing Education Attainment.

imagedata.alt: Stacked column chart of the level of education by age range from 18 to over 64.

section.para: demonstrates the first step in creating the stacked column chart above. The first step is to insert a stacked column chart into the worksheet.

figure.label: Figure C.12
figure.title: Insert a Stacked Column Chart into the Worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Dropdown displaying the column chart options with the staked column chart highlighted.
Dropdown displaying the column chart options with the staked column chart highlighted.

section.para: The next step in creating the stacked column chart is to add the data series to the chart. A stacked column chart requires a data series for each segment of the chart columns. In this case, there are three segments for each of the columns: less than high school, high school graduates, and bachelor’s or above. outlines the series names and values for each data series.
To add the second and third data series, click the plus icon below the Legend Entries (Series) box to insert another data series.

figure.title: Data Series Information for the Stacked Column Chart.

Select Data Series dialog box with High School Graduate values displayed.

imagedata.alt: Select Data Series dialog box with bachelors or above values displayed.
Select Data Series dialog box with bachelors or above values displayed.

section.para: The next step in the process involves setting the values for the horizontal axis labels (). The horizontal axis labels are referenced in range C3:G3.

imagedata.alt: Select Data Source dialog box with the horizontal axis lables values added.
Select Data Source dialog box with the horizontal axis lables values added.

section.para: The data source settings for the chart should appear as in . Notice that all three data series (one for each segment of the stacked columns) in the Legend Entries (Series) section of the dialog window.

imagedata.alt: Select Data Series dialog box with all the values entered for the stacked column chart.
Select Data Series dialog box with all the values entered for the stacked column chart.

section.para: Next, we want to edit the title of the chart (see ). The title of the chart should be “Level of Education by Age Range.”

A chart legend is used to help the user differentiate between the various data series depicted in a chart. There are three data series in the education chart, but it is difficult to know what each color represents without referring back to the original data. Adding a legend to clearly label what each color represents makes it much easier to interpret the chart. To add the legend, click the plus icon, choose the arrow head after Legend, and select bottom from the Legend menu.

A chart legend is used to help the user differentiate between the various data series depicted in a chart. There are three data series in the education chart, but it is difficult to know what each color represents without referring back to the original data. Adding a legend to clearly label what each color represents makes it much easier to interpret the chart. To add the legend, click the Add Chart Element icon on the Chart Design menu, choose Legend, and then select bottom from the Legend menu.

imagedata.alt: Chart legend added below the chart data.
Chart legend added below the chart data.

section.para: The final step is to add axis titles to the chart. Axis titles will further clarify the chart for the user by labeling what each axis of the chart represents. Select the Axis Titles option from the plus icon and then rename the x-axis "Age Range" and the y-axis "Percentage".

The final step is to add axis titles to the chart. Axis titles will further clarify the chart for the user by labeling what each axis of the chart represents. Select the Axis Titles option from the Add Chart Elements icon, select Primary Horizontal, and rename the x-axis title "Age Range". Next, choose Axis Titles and Primary Vertical from the Add Chart Elements menu and rename the y-axis title "Percentage".

imagedata.alt: Drop down menu for the Axis titles.
Drop down menu for the Axis titles.
Completed Stacked Column chart with a title, legend and axis titles.

section.label: C.4
section.title: Building a Pie Chart
section.para: The next type of chart we will cover in this lesson is a pie chart. Pie chartsA chart used to compare the parts of a category (expressed as percentages) to the whole. are used to compare the parts of a category to the whole. Typically, each of the parts is expressed as a percentage of the whole. For example, we could use a pie chart to display the percent of people in each level of education attainment. We created a chart of this data in the column chart lesson, but a pie chart of the data might be more meaningful.

imagedata.alt: Column chart of Education attainment in the United States for people 25 years and over.
Pie chart of Education attainment in the United States for people 25 years and over.

section.para: The first step in creating the pie chart is to insert a new pie chart into your worksheet. depicts this activity.

figure.label: Figure C.19
figure.title: Insert a Pie Chart into the Worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Dropdown menu for the pie chart option.
Dropdown menu for the pie chart option.

section.para: Since a pie chart compares the parts of a single category to the total for that category, there is typically only one data series for a pie chart. In this case, the data series name, "Population", is referenced in cell E5 and the values are the individual percent of people in each level of education attainments in range E6:E12 ().

figure.label: Figure C.20
figure.title: The Data Series for the Pie Chart.

imagedata.alt: Edit Data range entered for the pie chart.
Edit Data range entered for the pie chart.

section.para: Pie charts are different from the other charts we have discussed in this lesson because they don’t have a horizontal axis. Instead, the segments, or wedges, of the pie chart are treated as the horizontal axis categories. Therefore, the axis labels are the names of the wedges of the chart. In this case, the wedges are the percent of people in each level of education attainment. The names of the wedges are referenced in range B6:B12 ().

figure.label: Figure C.21
figure.title: Define the Names of the Pie Chart Wedges.

imagedata.alt: Select Data for the names of the pie chart wedges for the pie chart.
Select Data for the names of the pie chart wedges for the pie chart.

section.para: The data sources for the pie chart should look like .

figure.label: Figure C.22
figure.title: Data Sources for the Pie Chart.

imagedata.alt: Dialog box with the values and labels for the pie chart.
Dialog box with the values and labels for the pie chart.
Pie chart with one 100% pie wedge and less than 9th grade as the title.

section.para: Next, we will edit the title of the chart (). The title of the chart should be “Education Attainment in the United States.”

figure.label: Figure C.23
figure.title: Edit the Title for the Pie Chart.

imagedata.alt: Chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.
Chart with title Education Attainment in the United States.

section.para: The next step in creating this chart is to add data labels to the chart. This can be done using the Data Labels menu item after clicking the plus icon next to the chart. It can also be done by selecting an appropriate chart layout in the Design tab. You may need to scroll through the chart layout options to find the appropriate template. In this case, we are interested in a title centered at the top of the chart, a legend to the right of the chart, and data labels expressed as percentages centered in each wedge of the chart. This layout isn’t always displayed but can be found under the Quick Layout option. Click on the appropriate layout option to update the chart.

The next step in creating this chart is to add data labels to the chart. This can be done using the Data Labels menu item after clicking Add Chart Element. It can also be done by selecting an appropriate chart layout in the Chart Design tab. You may need to scroll through the chart layout options to find the appropriate template. In this case, we are interested in a title centered at the top of the chart, a legend to the right of the chart, and data labels expressed as percentages centered in each wedge of the chart. This layout isn’t always displayed but can be found under the Quick Layout option. Click on the appropriate layout option to update the chart.

imagedata.alt: Drop down menu of quick layout options available for the pie chart
Drop down menu of quick layout options available for the pie chart

section.para: The chart now shows the Data Labels on the slices and the Legend on the right side of the chart. Moving the legend to the right allowed the pie to be displayed in a larger size.

imagedata.alt: Pie chart with data labels and legend display on the right.
Pie chart with data labels and legend display on the right.

section.para: The last step in creating this chart is to fix the color on the Graduate or professional degree slice. The black text on the dark blue makes it difficult to read. To fix the color, select the slice by clicking on it twice. The sizing handles will be displayed on only that slice. Choose Fill color on the Home menu or Shape Fill on the Format menu. Select the standard color Yellow as the new fill color.

imagedata.alt: Completed pie chart with graduate and professional degree slice displayed in a light yellow so that the data label is readable.

section.para: At this point, the chart is complete.

section.title: Displaying Data Labels as Percentages
section.para: The default option for data labels is to show the raw values for each of the different categories. Because a pie chart represents the data categories as the percentage of the whole, it generally makes more sense to show the data labels as a percent of the total values rather than as the raw values. The raw data for the “Education Attainment in the United States” chart in this example are already expressed as a percentage, so the values for the data labels do not need to be changed. When you have raw numbers as the source data for your pie chart, you will need to modify the data labels options to display data labels as a percentage.

This is done by double-clicking on one of the data labels on the chart. This will open the Format Data Labels window. Click on the Label Options tab in the window. Uncheck the box next to Value and check the box next to Percentage. This will display the data labels as a percentage.

figure.label: Figure C.27
figure.title: Displaying Data Labels as a Percentage.

imagedata.alt: Configure data labels to be displayed as a percentage.
Configure data labels to be displayed as a percentage.

section.label: D.1
section.title: Analyzing Spreadsheet Models Using What-If Analyses

note.title: Unavailable Options
note.para: The What-If tools, Scenarios, Goal Seek, and Data Tables are currently not available in the online version of Excel. Switch your book to either the Windows or Mac version of to learn about these tools.
To switch to a desktop version:

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Advance options menu displayed on the right side of the window.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

imagedata.alt: Versions available in book.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

section.para: Building a spreadsheet model of real-life process can provide a powerful way to improve your decision-making about that process. For example, if you build a spreadsheet model to help you plan your finances, you can use that tool to figure out how best to manage your money so that you have enough to pay your bills and perhaps have some left in your checking account. You will be able to see where your major expenses will come from and plan your finances to meet those obligations.

If your model is flexible enough, you could even use it to see how hypothetical changes in your life (such as buying a new car or getting a new job) may impact your financial situation. When you use an existing model to examine the impact of hypothetical changes in the model inputs, you are conducting a what-if analysis.

section.title: Conducting What-If Analysis in Excel
section.para: Excel can be used to conduct some very sophisticated analyses on your spreadsheet models. Among these is the capability to perform various types of what-if analyses. Excel has three specific tools for conducting a what-if analysis on your spreadsheet model: scenario manager An analysis that examines the impact of several inputs to a spreadsheet model changing at the same time and is sophisticated enough to allow you to create a number of different scenarios based on different states of the same set of inputs. , goal seek An analysis tool that allows users to work backward, specifying the desired value for an outcome cell and selecting an input cell that Excel should modify to achieve the desired outcome. , and data tables An analysis tool that allows users to specify a number of different possible states for one or two input variables and construct a table in the worksheet that calculates an outcome for the model based on each state of the input variables. . In this lesson, we will review how each of these analyses work in the context of a modified version of the loan application spreadsheet model (see ).

figure.label: Figure D.1
figure.title: Spreadsheet Model of Mortgage Approval Process.

imagedata.alt: Table of Mortgage Approval. First potion is Customer Information. Annual Income $62,000; Cash Available for Down Payment $10,000; % of Cash to Pay Debt 100%; House Price $225,000; Interest Rate 5.25%; Term (Years) is 30; Monthly Debts (If Not Retired) $1,000; Acceptable % of Income 40%; Second portion is Income Calculation; Monthly Income $5,167; Acceptable Income Servicing Debt $2,067; Monthly Debts (After Debt Retired) $500; Max Payment $1,567. Monthly Payment Calculation. Loan Amount $225,000; Interest Rate 5.25%; Term (Years) is 30. Monthly Payment $1,242. Income Surplus and $324. Approval is Yes.

Table of Mortgage Approval. First potion is Customer Information. Annual Income $62,000; Cash Available for Down Payment $10,000; % of Cash to Pay Debt 100%; House Price $225,000; Interest Rate 5.25%; Term (Years) is 30; Monthly Debts (If Not Retired) $1,000; Acceptable % of Income 40%; Second portion is Income Calculation; Monthly Income =C5/12; Acceptable Income Servicing Debt =C15*C12; Monthly Debts (After Debt Retired) =C11-C6*C7*0.05; Max Payment =C16-C17. Monthly Payment Calculation. Loan Amount =C8-C6*(1-C7); Interest Rate =C9; Term (Years) =C10. Monthly Payment =ABS(PMT(C22/12,C23*12,C21). Income Surplus =C18-C24. Approval =IF(C24 less than =C18,’Yes’,’No’).

section.para: In the modified model, the user will input the income and loan information about the applicant, and then the model will complete a series of calculations to derive a suggestion regarding whether the applicant should be approved. In this model, the user will also input the amount of cash available from the applicant to use as a down payment or to pay off debt. Money put toward a down payment will reduce the loan amount and, therefore. the monthly payment that the applicant will pay on the loan. Alternatively, money put toward retiring debt will reduce the monthly debts owed by the applicant allowing the applicant to afford a larger loan amount every month.

In the next sections, we will highlight how to use scenario manager, goal seek, and data tables. We will also use the modified mortgage approval model to analyze hypothetical scenarios for an applicant to demonstrate the usefulness of these approaches.

section.title: Scenario Manager
section.para: The scenario manager is useful for examining the impact of several inputs to a spreadsheet model changing at the same time. Scenario manager is sophisticated enough to allow you to create a number of different scenarios based on different states of the same set of inputs. In this way, you can compare a number of different hypothetical situations at the same time.

The first step to using scenario manager is determining which of the input variables in your model you want to change for each scenario examined. You then determine the values that you would like to examine for each variable. The next step involves defining each scenario in Excel by selecting the inputs used in that scenario and entering the values for each input. Finally, you can create a summary worksheet that compares each scenario and how each influences a specified model outcome.

For example, we can use scenario manager to examine how the amount of cash available for a down payment, the percentage of that cash that is used to retire debt, the house price, and the interest rate for a given applicant will impact whether they will be approved for their proposed loan. highlights the first two steps in this process by outlining the input variables and the values for the variable under each scenario. In each scenario, the applicant has $10,000 available for a down payment. The percentage of money going toward debt reduction varies under each scenario. Scenarios 1–4 assume the house price is $225,000. Because applicants will pay a higher interest rate as they reduce their down payment, each scenario highlights larger interest rates as the percentage of the cash going toward paying off debt increases. Finally, Scenario 5 includes the effect of the applicant purchasing a more expensive home if they use all of their available cash to retire debt.

figure.label: Figure D.2
figure.title: Hypothetical Scenarios for a Loan Applicant.

imagedata.alt: Scenarios table. Model Input, Scenario 1, Scenario 2, Scenario 3, Scenario 4, Scenario 5.

section.para: The next step involves defining each scenario in the scenario manager dialog window (see ). The Scenario Manager dialog window is found in the Data tab of the Excel menu system, under the What-If Analysis icon in the Data Tools group of menu items. To define a scenario, we open the Scenario Manager dialog window and click on Add to add a new scenario. We then enter the name and changing cells for scenario 1 in the Edit Scenario dialog window. Notice that the range where the user enters values for each input defines the changing cells for that scenario (e.g., Cash Available for Down Payment is entered in cell C6). Finally, we enter the values for each input variable in the Scenario Values dialog window. This process is repeated until all five scenarios have been defined.

imagedata.alt: Excel open to File tab and in the Data Tools section, the What-If Analysis button.
Excel open to File tab and in the Data Tools section, the What-If Analysis button.

informalfigure.title: Click Add to add a new scenario.

imagedata.alt: Scenario Manager in dialog box. Scenarios section, add, merge, delete, edit buttons. Changing cells field. Comment field. Buttons for Show and Close.
Scenario Manager in dialog box. Scenarios section, add, merge, delete, edit buttons. Changing cells field. Comment field. Buttons for Show and Close.

informalfigure.title: Enter the Scenario name and Changing cells.

imagedata.alt: Edit Scenario dialog box. Scenario name with field for Scenario 1. Changing cells with field containing $C$6:$C$9. Comment with field filled by Created by Windows User 6/24/2013. Section for Protection and checkboxes for Prevent changes and Hide. Buttons for OK and Cancel.

Edit Scenario dialog box. Scenario name with field for Scenario 1. Changing cells with field containing $C$6:$C$9. Comment with field filled by Created by Windows User 6/24/2013. Section for Protection and checkboxes for Prevent changes and Hide. Buttons for OK and Cancel.

Dialog box for Scenario Values. Enter values for each of the changing cells. Numbered fields. 1 is $C$6 and 10000, 2 is $C$7 and .25, 3 is $C$8 and 225000, 4 is $C$9 and 0.0525. Buttons for Add, OK, and Cancel.

Dialog box for Scenario Values. Enter values for each of the changing cells. Numbered fields. 1 is $C$6 and 10000, 2 is $C$7 and .25, 3 is $C$8 and 225000, 4 is $C$9 and 0.0525. Buttons for Add, OK, and Cancel.

section.para: Once all five scenarios are created, we are ready to create a summary worksheet that compares the outcomes of each scenario (see ). We first click on the Summary… button in the Scenario Manager dialog window. Note that the Show button does not create the summary worksheet. Rather, it updates the original model with the values outlined in the scenario selected. Next, we define the outcome cells we want to include in our summary report. In this case, we would like to see both (to select nonconsecutive cells, hold down the control button when selecting the cells) the approval recommendation (cell C28) and the “Income Surplus” calculation (cell C26) that calculates the difference between the applicant’s maximum payment allowed (given their income and debt load) and the projected monthly payment based on the proposed loan. This will better demonstrate how the different inputs for each scenario influence the outcome calculations. Finally, we click OK to create the summary worksheet.

imagedata.alt: Scenario Manager in dialog box. Scenarios section with text for Scenario 1 through 5. Add, merge, delete, edit buttons. Changing cells field contains $C$6:C$9. Comment field contains Created by Windows User 6/24/2013. Buttons for Show and Close.

Scenario Manager in dialog box. Scenarios section with text for Scenario 1 through 5. Add, merge, delete, edit buttons. Changing cells field contains $C$6:C$9. Comment field contains Created by Windows User 6/24/2013. Buttons for Show and Close.
Scenario Summary dialog box. Report type section with radio button for Scenario summary and Scenario PivotTable report. Result cells section with field for C26,C28 and buttons for OK and Cancel.
Scenario Summary dialog box. Report type section with radio button for Scenario summary and Scenario PivotTable report. Result cells section with field for C26,C28 and buttons for OK and Cancel.

Scenario Summary. Column B contains Changing Cells and Result Cells with absolute references. D contains Current Values, E contains Scenario 1, F contains Scenario 2, G contains Scenario 3, H contains Scenario 4, I contains Scenario 5.

Scenario Summary. Column B contains Changing Cells and Result Cells with title such as Down payment, debt, Price, rate, and surplus. D contains Current Values, E contains Scenario 1, F contains Scenario 2, G contains Scenario 3, H contains Scenario 4, I contains Scenario 5.

section.para: The summary worksheet presents an overview of the current state of the spreadsheet model (Current Values) and each of the scenarios created in scenario manager for that model. This summary sheet can be edited to include appropriate row headings to make interpreting the model easier. In this case, we see that allocating too much cash to a down payment would be a mistake for this applicant. Rather, available cash should be used to retire other debts. Notice that if all of the available cash were diverted to paying off other debts, the applicant could even afford a more expensive house.

section.title: Goal Seek
section.para: Scenario manager is a powerful way to modify several inputs to a spreadsheet model at the same time to analyze how these changes impact model outcomes. Goal Seek, on the other hand, is used to work backward from an outcome calculation to determine an input condition necessary to arrive at the desired outcome. Goal seek allows the user to specify the desired value for an outcome cell and select an input cell that Excel should modify to achieve the desired outcome.

We could use goal seek in our mortgage example to determine the percentage of the cash available for the down payment that should be used to retire debt in order to qualify for a home priced at $260,000 (see ). Goal seek is also found under the What-If Analysis icon in the Data Tools section of the Data menu items. To complete the analysis, we need to specify the location of the “Income Surplus” calculation (cell C26) as our “Set cell”. We want the value of this calculation to be zero. When surplus is greater or equal to zero, the applicant will be approved for the loan. Since increasing the percentage of the cash used to retire debt increases the income surplus, selecting zero as the desired outcome will allow Excel to calculate the smallest percentage of the cash that can be used to retire debt and still approve the applicant’s loan. The “By changing cell” for this analysis is the percentage of cash used to retire debt (cell C7).

imagedata.alt: Goal Seek dialog window. Set cell field contains $C$26, To value contains 0, By changing cell $C$7. Buttons for OK and Cancel.
Goal Seek dialog window. Set cell field contains $C$26, To value contains 0, By changing cell $C$7. Buttons for OK and Cancel.
Goal Seek Status dialog box. Goal Seeking with Cell C26 found a solution. Target value: 0 and Current value: $0. Buttons for Step, Pause, OK, and Cancel.
Goal Seek Status dialog box. Goal Seeking with Cell C26 found a solution. Target value: 0 and Current value: $0. Buttons for Step, Pause, OK, and Cancel.

section.para: Once these values are entered into the Goal Seek dialog window, we can click OK to complete the calculation. Because it is possible that Goal Seek will not find an appropriate value for the selected “By changing cell” input that will lead to the desired value for the “Set cell”, Excel displays the Goal Seek Status window after completing the analysis. This informs the user if a feasible solution was found. In this particular case, the applicant will need to devote 98% of his available cash to pay off debt if the house price is $260,000 (given a 30-year term, 6% interest, and $10,000 cash available).

Goal Seek has some important limitations. First, you can only specify one input and one outcome variable to change as part of the analysis. Also, you must specify an exact amount for the desired outcome. Finally, your set cell must contain a formula. Within the context of these limitations, Goal Seek is a good tool for analyzing spreadsheet models.

section.title: Data Tables
section.para: The final method for conducting a what-if analysis presented in this lesson is data tables. Using data tables, we can specify several different possible states for one or two input variables and construct a table in our worksheet that calculates an outcome for our model based on each state of the input variables. When one input variable is used, the analysis is called a one-way data table. Examining the effect of two variables at once requires a two-way data table.

section.title: One-Way Data Tables
section.para: Constructing data tables requires some setup in our worksheets. After the model is created, we need to list the desired states for our single input in either a single row or column with the cells directly to the right of the column (or under the row) left blank (see ). The remainder of this description will assume that values for the input variable are listed in a column, though the process is similar when the values are placed on a row. We want to use different values for the interest rate as the inputs to our data table, so we list those in the first column of the data table. We can label the cell directly over our different levels for interest rate to describe the analysis, but the cell directly over the blank cells to the right of the input values must also be left blank (see ).

imagedata.alt: Column E for Interest Rate. Rows with percentages ranging from 4 to 6.50%.

Table of Mortgage Approval. First potion is Customer Information. Annual Income $62,000; Cash Available for Down Payment $10,000; % of Cash to Pay Debt 25%; House Price $260,000; Interest Rate 6.00%; Term (Years) is 30; Monthly Debts (If Not Retired) $1,000; Acceptable % of Income 40%; Second portion is Income Calculation; Monthly Income $5,167; Acceptable Income Servicing Debt $2,067; Monthly Debts (After Debt Retired) $875; Max Payment $1,192. Monthly Payment Calculation portion. Loan Amount $252,500; Interest Rate 6.00%; Term (Years) is 30. Monthly Payment $1,514. Income Surplus and -$322. Approval is No. Interest Rate=C26. Rows with percentages ranging from 4 to 6.50%.
Same spreadsheet as above as Mortgage Approval. Rows 5 through 16 are highlighted. Interest Rate is -$332. Data Table Row input cell field is blank. Column input cell field contains $C$9.
Same spreadsheet as above as Mortgage Approval. Rows 5 through 16 are highlighted. Interest Rate is -$332. Data Table Row input cell field is blank. Column input cell field contains $C$9.
Column E is Interest rate and contains percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%. Column F contains negative numbers ranging from $13 to $405.

section.para: Once the data table is set up, we can begin the analysis. We place the calculation that we want to be completed for each input value on the data table in the blank cell at the top of the row to the right of the input values (cell F5 in this case). You can just reference the cell that contains the calculation you want to be completed from our spreadsheet model if you don’t want to reconstruct the calculation. In this case, we want to complete the data table with the income surplus calculation in cell C26.

We then select the input cell that we want to correspond with the values in the first column of our data table. This is done by highlighting all of the cells in the data table (E5:F16) and selecting Data Table from the What-If Analysis icon in the Data Tools group of items in the Data menu tab. Because we have our changing values entered in a column, we reference cell C9 (the location of the interest rate input) as our Column input cell. Once we click OK, Excel will automatically complete the data table with the income surplus calculation in column F for each value of the interest rate in column E. We can see how varying levels of interest rate affect the income surplus calculation in our model.

section.title: Two-Way Data Tables
section.para: Constructing a two-way data table is similar to constructing a one-way data table. The setup for a two-way data table requires that we create a list of values for the two input variables that we want to vary in our analysis. We position input in the first column of our data table and the other in the first row. demonstrates how to set up a two-way data table. Notice that the cell directly above and to the left of the input values is left blank. Any labels used to describe the inputs are placed over and to the left of the data table.

Next, we construct the calculation we want to be performed in the data table in the blank cell directly to above and to the left of our input values (cell I5 in our example). As before, we can either build the expression we wish to use in the data table calculations or we can reference a cell from our model that has the calculation we want. In this case, we will reference cell C28, which contains the approval recommendation for the applicant. Our data table will display the combinations of down payment totals (the row input for our analysis) and interest rates (the column inputs for our analysis) that will lead to an applicant being approved (notice that all other inputs are held constant in this analysis).

imagedata.alt: Down Payment columns J through N. Interest Rate is rows 6 through 16 and percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%.

informalfigure.title: Complete the calculation used in the Data Table.

imagedata.alt: Down Payment columns J through N. Interest Rate is rows 6 through 16 and percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%. I5 contains =C28.

informalfigure.title: Select Column and Row Input cells.

imagedata.alt: Down Payment columns J through N. Interest Rate is rows 6 through 16 and percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%. I5 contains =C28.
Down Payment columns J through N. Interest Rate is rows 6 through 16 and percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%. I5 contains =C28.

Down Payment columns J through N rows $5,000, $10,000, $15,000, $20,000, $25,000. Interest Rate is rows 6 through 16 and percentages ranging from 4.00% to 6.50%. I5 contains No. Column J contains cells with No. Column K contains cells with No. L6 and L7 contain Yes. The rest of the column contains No. M6 through M10 contain Yes. M11 through M16 contain No. N6 through N12 contain Yes. N13 through N16 contain No.

section.para: We next select the column and row input cells for the table. To do this, we highlight the entire table area (I5:N16) and select Data Table from the menu options. The different levels of cash available for down payment are in the first row of our data table, so we reference the location for the cash available for down payment cell of our model (cell C6) as the row input cell in our data table. Likewise, we reference the location of the interest rate input as the column input cell for our analysis because the different interest rate values are located in the first column of our table.

When we click OK, the approval calculation will be completed in each cell of our data table for each combination of cash available for down payment and interest rate defined on the table (e.g., cell K7 would correspond to the $10,000 cash and 4.25% interest rate). From this table, we can easily see the down payment amounts and interest rates necessary for this loan to be approved.

note.title: Practice
note.para: Use the Windows or Mac desktop version of Excel to complete this practice assignment.

note.title: Assessment
note.para: Use the Windows or Mac desktop version of Excel to complete this assessment.

section.label: E.1
section.title: Working with Named Ranges

note.title: Unavailable Options
note.para: Named ranges are not currently available in the online version of Excel. To learn about this feature, switch the book to a desktop version.
To switch to a desktop version:

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Advanced option displayed on the right side of the window.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

imagedata.alt: Versions available for book.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

section.para: One method for dealing with the complexity of cell references when working with large spreadsheet models is using named ranges. As the name implies, Excel allows you to define a cell or set of cells and refer to the set by a name that you provide. This helps to simplify the process of working with cell references because once you define a name for a range of cells, you no longer need to use the location of the cells to reference the cells.

Let’s look at an example. Suppose you have created a simple model to help you think through your budget for tuition, books, and other expenses at the beginning of the semester. You have saved $5,000 to pay for the upcoming semester, and you want to make sure that this will be enough money. You have listed four major expenses that you will incur at the beginning of the semester: tuition, books, supplies, and other miscellaneous expenses.

To simplify the process of completing the budget, we will create a few named ranges. You will name cell C4 “Savings”. Cells C7:C10 will be named “Expenses”. Cell C12 will be named, “TotalExpenses” (notice that we don’t use spaces in named ranges). Finally, cell C14 will be named “Balance”.

imagedata.alt: Named ranges. Money Saved for School $5,000.00. Expenses table and Tuition $2,000; Books $1,000; Supplies $500; Misc. $50. Total Expenses $3,550. Ending Savings Balance $1,450.

section.para: To define a name to associate with a range of cells in a worksheet, you will use the Defined Names section of menu items in the Formulas tab (See ). To associate the name “Savings” with cell C4, select C4 and click on the Define Name menu item. The New Name dialog window appears. Notice that because I have selected cell C4, the Refers to: element of the window is prepopulated with a reference to cell C4. It is important to note that the cell reference for this named range is an absolute reference. Anytime you copy and paste a formula that uses a reference to a named range, the reference will always behave as an absolute reference (the column and row references will stay fixed on the cells defined in the named range).

Also, notice that Excel has made a guess about the name you wish to associate with this range in the Name: element of the window. This guess comes from the text in cell B4. While this is a pretty good guess, we will need to change the name to “Savings”. Once we click OK, cell C4 will always be associated with the name “Savings”. We can use the word “Savings” to reference cell C4 in formulas and calculations.

imagedata.alt: Excel Menu bar with File Tab selected. Group for Defined Names selected.
Excel Menu bar with File Tab selected. Group for Defined Names selected.

Notice how the references to named ranges replace any specific references to cells in our model. This includes the reference to the set of expenses (cells C7 to C10) used to calculate the total expenses.

section.label: F.1
section.title: Working with Circular References in Excel

note.title: Unavailable Option
note.para: Circular References are currently not correctable with iterative calculations in the online version of Excel. To learn about this feature, switch the book to a desktop version.
To switch to a desktop version:

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.

imagedata.alt: Advanced options displayed on the right side of the window.

listitem.para: Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

imagedata.alt: Versions available in book.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

section.para: At times you will find that the process you are modeling requires completing two interdependent calculations, which rely on the results from one calculation as needed inputs for the other calculation. While such circular logic can be confusing, it is necessary for some types of spreadsheet problems. This lesson will expose you to the issue of circular references in Excel and describe how to use them in your spreadsheet models.

section.title: What Is a Circular Reference?
section.para: A circular reference occurs when one cell either directly or indirectly references itself in completing a calculation. demonstrates both types of circular references. Notice that with the direct circular reference A cell that contains a reference to itself. , the argument for the SUM function includes a reference to cell C7. When the cell contains a reference to itself, it is a direct circular reference. In the indirect reference example, there are three items that all reference a different item. Item A references Item C, Item B references Item A, and Item C references Item B. While none of the cells contain a direct circular reference, the cell references another cell that eventually references back to itself, creating an indirect circular reference A cell references another cell that eventually references back to itself. .

imagedata.alt: Spreadsheet. Direct Circular reference. Item 1 Cost is $10. Item 2 Cost is $20. Total Cost =SUM(C4:C7).
Indirect Circular reference. Item A (Half of Item C) is =0.5*C6. Item B (Three Times Item A) is =3*C4. Item C (One-and-a-half Times Item B) is =1.5*C5.

Microsoft Excel Warning Box. The text is as follows. Careful, we found one or more circular reference in your workbook that might cause your formulas to calculate incorrectly. FYI: A circular reference can be a formula that refers to its own cell value, or refers to a cell dependent on its own cell value. Then buttons for OK and Help.

Circular reference warning: There are one or more circular references where a formula refers to its own cell either directly or indirectly. This might cause them to calculate incorrectly. Try removing or changing these references, or moving the formulas to different cells. OK button.

section.para: To make matters more complicated, while both of these examples illustrate the concept of a circular reference, neither presents a valid use of circular references. In the direct circular reference example, adding cell C7 to the sum of C4 and C5 will always increase the value of cell C7, so the sum will always be increasing. If Excel were to allow this formula to recalculate over and over again, it would never arrive at a final answer because the final answer would be infinity. With the indirect circular reference example, there is no starting point for any of the values. While the references might create a mathematically correct answer for all values if a valid starting point were given, Excel will not arrive at a valid answer to the set of calculations if not given a place to start.

Perhaps this is why using circular references is not for the faint of heart. It is also why Excel will always display the circular reference warning in whenever any circular references are created in a model. In most cases, circular references are not necessary and should be avoided. However, there are some cases in which circular references are necessary. These situations tend to come up frequently in the finance and engineering fields. The hallmark of a circular reference problem is a model that has two calculations that must be completed simultaneously but that depend on the solution from the other calculation as an input.

Excel is able to solve this type of problem by allowing the workbook to continually recalculate the formulas within the spreadsheet model and slightly adjust the values for each circular reference until Excel finds an equilibrium solution that satisfies both calculations.

This recalculation option must be selected by changing the default options in Excel. This is done by selecting Options from the File menu tab. Next, click on Formulas and check the box to Enable iterative calculation. This will instruct Excel to recalculate each formula on the worksheet until the number of Maximum Iterations (set under the“Enable iterative calculation option) has been reached.

This recalculation option must be selected by changing the default options in Excel. This is done by selecting Preferences from the File menu tab. Next, click on Calculation in the Formulas and Lists group and check the box to Use iterative calculation. This will instruct Excel to recalculate each formula on the worksheet until the number of Maximum Iterations (set under the Use iterative calculation option) has been reached.

figure.label: Figure F.2
figure.title: Setting Excel to Recalculate Formulas in a Spreadsheet Model.

Calculation Dialog box. Sections for Calculation Options. Automatic selected. Checkbox for Enable multi-threaded calculation. Checkbox for Use iterative calculation selected. Maximum iterations set to 100. Maximum change set to .001. Section for When Calculating Workbooks.

section.para: Let’s look at a concrete example. In , we present a spreadsheet model of a simple budget of student expenses at the beginning of a semester. The student has saved $2,500 for the semester. Unfortunately, his expenses total $3,550. He will need to take out a loan for the difference between the $2,500 he has saved and the $3,550 he must pay at the beginning of the semester. The student wants to keep his loan debt to a minimum, but he must incur enough debt to cover his total expenses (including the 1.5% loan fee that is charged by the lender when he takes delivery of the loan).

The loan must cover the expenses plus the loan fee. If the student takes out a loan for $1,050 to cover the cost of the expenses, he will be short by the amount of the loan fee—$15.75. Of course, if the loan increased to cover the cost of the loan fee, then the fee will also go up (since it is a function of the loan amount). This is a classic circular reference problem. The loan fee calculation needs the loan amount in order to correctly determine the amount of the loan fee. At the same time, the loan amount calculation requires the loan fee amount in order to correctly determine the amount of the loan needed to cover all of the expenses for the semester.

figure.label: Figure F.3
figure.title: Simple Beginning of a Semester Budget.

imagedata.alt: Circular Logic. Money Saved for School $2,500.00. Expenses table and Tuition $2,000; Books $1,000; Supplies $500; Misc. $50. Total Expenses $3,550. Shortfall ($1,050.00). Loan.

section.para: To complete this model, we’ll add the formulas for the loan amount and loan fee in cells C17 and C11, respectively (see ). Notice that the loan amount references cell C15 (Shortfall). The loan fee references the loan amount. This creates an indirect circular reference because the shortfall calculation depends on the loan fee, and the loan amount depends on the shortfall amount. If you haven’t changed the options to enable iterative calculations, you will receive the circular reference warning message. Once iterative calculations are enabled, Excel will arrive at an appropriate solution to the model.

informalfigure.title: Simple budget with loan and loan fee calculations completed.

imagedata.alt: Circular Logic. Money Saved for School $2,500.00. Expenses table and Tuition $2,000; Books $1,000; Supplies $500; Misc. $50. Loan Fee (1.5% Loan Amount) is $15.99. Total Expenses $3,565.99. Shortfall ($1,065.99). Loan $1,065.99.

informalfigure.title: Formulas used to complete the model.

imagedata.alt: Circular Logic. Money Saved for School $2,500.00. Expenses table and Tuition $2,000; Books $1,000; Supplies $500; Misc. $50. Loan Fee (1.5% Loan Amount) contains the function =C17*.015. Total Expenses =SUM(C7:C11). Shortfall =C4-C13. Loan =-C15.

section.para: Excel is able to arrive at an appropriate solution to the problem because of the iterative calculation option. outlines an example of how an iterative calculation might work. The first pass of calculations for the loan and loan fee would be $1,050 and $15.75, respectively. At this pass, the loan fee is correct given the loan amount, but the loan amount would not be sufficient to cover the expenses plus the loan fee. In the second pass, the loan amount is adjusted up to $1,065.75 to cover the loan fee from pass 1, and the loan fee is $15.99—which is correct given the new loan amount. In pass 2, the loan is not sufficient to cover the additional $.24 in loan fees incurred from the increased loan amount. In pass 3, the loan amount is adjusted to $1,065.99 to cover the additional loan fees incurred in pass 2. The loan fee amount in pass 3 is stable from pass 2 to pass 3 because the increase in loan amount is not significant enough to lead to an increase in loan fees. At this point, equilibrium is reached because the loan amount does not need to increase to cover additional loan fees. Both the loan amount and loan fees are at an appropriate level.

imagedata.alt: Table with Pass, Loan Amount, and Loan Fee. Pass 1, loan amount $1,050.00 and Loan Fee $15.75. Pass 2, loan amount $1,065.75 and Loan Fee $15.99. Pass 3, loan amount $1,065.99 and Loan Fee $15.99.

section.label: G.1
section.title: Building Optimization Models

listitem.para: Recognize and know when to use an optimization model.
Effectively use constraints to find an optimal solution.

section.para: Excel has several features that allow users to conduct powerful data analyses. Among these is the capability to perform optimization analyses, which are used to find an optimal or best possible solution to a problem. Business problems that might be solved with optimization include the following:

listitem.para: Determining the mix of stocks to include in an investment portfolio
Deciding the production mix for a set of products that share a manufacturing facility
Determining the location of a warehouse to minimize shipping costs to branch locations
Scheduling employees to minimize payroll costs while ensuring appropriate coverage

section.para: Optimization techniques are particularly valuable when the problem you are solving has one or more of the following characteristics:

listitem.para: There is a single, best answer to the problem. You are not looking for a satisfactory solution but want to find an optimal solution.
There are multiple decisions that must be made to arrive at the optimal solution.
There are multiple constraints on the decisions that can be made.

section.para: Excel has an add-in that you can use to solve optimization problems. This tool is called Solver An Excel add-in that you can use to solve optimization problems. Before you can effectively use Solver, you must understand how to build an optimization spreadsheet model. In this lesson, we first discuss how to build optimization models in Excel. We will then discuss how to use Solver to arrive at an optimal solution for the model.

section.title: Building Blocks of Optimization Models
section.para: There are three major elements of an optimization modelA model used to find the optimal solution for a problem. The three major elements are (1) the objective, which represents the best possible outcome for the model or optimal solution; (2) decisions, which are the elements of the model that can change to arrive at the optimal solution; and (3) one or more constraints, which must be satisfied by the optimal solution.: (1) the objective, which represents the best possible outcome for the model or optimal solution; (2) the decisions, which are the elements of the model that can change to arrive at the optimal solution; and (3) one or more constraints, which must be satisfied by the optimal solution. The rest of this section will describe each of these elements and the role they play in an optimization model.

To facilitate our discussion, we’ll develop an optimization model for Benito’s Burrito Bar, a traveling lunch truck owned by Benito Chavez. Benito makes four types of burritos and drives to various businesses to sell them to the employees of these businesses for lunch. The four types of burritos are steak, chicken, veggie, and mega (a steak and chicken combination burrito). Benito must decide how many of each type of burrito to make every day. He would like to make as much profit as he can.

section.title: Where to Start?
section.para: The starting point for an optimization model is to capture the basic logic of the process you are modeling in a simple spreadsheet. The simple model should include both the elements of the decision that can change in order to arrive at the optimal solution as well as a way to model the objective.

For example, highlights a basic spreadsheet model that Benito might use to determine how much money he will earn by making various combinations of burritos to sell. Notice that the model has a place for him to change the number of each type of burrito he will make and a way to calculate total profit.

imagedata.alt: Benito’s Burrito Bar table in a spreadsheet. Column B contains Steak, C contains Chicken, D contains Veggie, E is Mega. A contains questions. How many should I make? Steak is 50, Chicken is 50, Veggie is 50, Mega is 50. Profitability portion. Price/Burrito for Steak is $7.95, for Chicken is $7.95, for Veggie is $6.95, for Mega is $8.95. Cost/Burrito for Steak is $3.15, for Chicken is $3.05, for Veggie is $2.25, for Mega is $3.10. Profit/Burrito for Steak is $4.80, for Chicken is $4.90, for Veggie is $6.95, for Mega is $5.85. Total Profit is $1,012.50.

section.para: A reasonable objective for this situation is to maximize total profit. We are calculating total profit in cell B12. Cells B5:E5 represent the parts of the decision that can be changed in order to arrive at the optimal solution. Benito must decide how many of each type of burrito to make. The final piece of the spreadsheet model involves determining and modeling the constraints.

section.title: Adding Constraints to Your Model

videodata.be:name: Mac - Adding Constraints To Your Model

section.para: Adding constraints to a model does not need to be difficult. Most model constraints are intuitive as you think through the issues that need to be addressed in order to arrive at an optimal solution. Modeling constraints involves the two-step process of identifying the constraints and modifying the spreadsheet model to account for those constraints.

In our example, Benito must decide how many of each type of burrito to make in order to maximize profits. The first step in modeling the constraints for this situation involves determining what those constraints are likely to be. In this case, Benito will likely need to account for at least two things: (1) the demand for each type of burrito and (2) the availability of the ingredients for each type of burrito. highlights the optimization model that accounts for both these constraints.

imagedata.alt: Benito’s Burrito Bar spreadsheet. Column B contains Steak, C contains Chicken, D contains Veggie, E is Mega. A contains questions. How many should I make? Steak is 50, Chicken is 50, Veggie is 50, Mega is 50. Demand: Steak 100, Chicken 100, Vegie 100, Mega 100. Ingredients portion. Beans for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Total Profit is $1,012.50

Steak is 50, Chicken is 50, Veggie is 50, Mega is 50. Demand: Steak 100, Chicken 100, Vegie 100, Mega 100. Ingredients portion. Beans for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Chicken for Steak is 0, for Chicken is 2, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Steak for Steak is 2, for Chicken is 0, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Veggies for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 2, for Mega is 1. Materials section. Beans for cost is $.50, for used is the function =SUMPRODUCT($B$5:$E$5,B10:E10), for Available is 300. Rice for cost is $.35, for used is =SUMPRODUCT($B$5:$E$5,B11:E11), for available is 400. Chicken for cost is $.75, used is =SUMPRODUCT($B$5:$E$5,B12:E12), available is 300. Cost/Burrito for Steak is =SUMPRODUCT(B10:B14,$B$18:$B$22), for Chicken is =SUMPRODUCT(C10:C14,$B$18:$B$22), for Veggie is =SUMPRODUCT(D10:D14,$B$18:$B$22), for Mega is =SUMPRODUCT(E10:E14,$B$18:$B$22). Profit/Burrito for Steak is =B25-B26, for Chicken is =C25-C26, for Veggie is =D25-D26, for Mega is =E25-E26. Total Profit is =SUMPRODUCT(B$5:$E$5,B27:#27).

section.para: Notice that cells B6:E6 are now used to store the demand for each type of burrito. These numbers do not factor into the calculations for total profit but will be used when we perform the Solver analysis. The demand numbers would come from Benito’s experience or from market research.

Cells A8:E14 are used to detail the amount of each type of ingredient that is used when making each type of burrito. This information would come from Benito’s experience with making burritos. It is used to determine how much of each ingredient will be consumed when Benito has made all of the burritos. These calculations are made in cells C17:C22.

The total number of ingredients consumed is a function of the number of each type of burrito made and the amount of each type of ingredient that is used in each type of burrito. (For example, we are making 200 total burritos—50 of each type. Since each type of burrito requires one helping of beans, we expect to use 200 total helpings of beans in making all of the burritos.) In this model, functions are used to calculate the amount of each type of ingredient consumed so that when the number of each type of burrito changes, the model automatically updates the total ingredients used.

We can now compare the ingredients consumed to the amount of each ingredient that is available at the time the burritos are made (cells D17:D22). This comparison will be helpful in completing the optimization analysis.

Because we have modeled the number of ingredients that are consumed in making each type of burrito, we can calculate the cost for each type of burrito (it will be the sum of the costs of all of the ingredients used in that type). By listing the costs for each type of burrito in cells B17:B22, we can easily update the costs of the ingredients when they change. The costs of each type of burrito will change according to the changes in the costs of the ingredients.

section.title: Finding an Optimal Solution
section.para: This burrito example is fairly straightforward. In fact, you’d likely be able to find the optimal solution without using the Solver application. Let’s assume that Benito does not want to make more burritos than he can sell (therefore, he won’t exceed demand) and that he can’t consume more ingredients than are available to him. Notice that we have just set the conditions of the constraints.

The rational way to address this problem without Solver would be to make as many of the burrito with the highest profit (in this case, the mega burrito) as possible without exceeding demand for that type of burrito or the ingredients needed to produce that burrito. Assuming there are still ingredients available after making enough of the mega burrito to meet demand, Benito should then make as many of the next-highest-profit type of burrito (the chicken burrito) as possible. This process would continue until either Benito has made as many burritos as he can sell or he has used up the supply of one of his ingredients. demonstrates the optimal solution for this example.

imagedata.alt: Benito’s Burrito Bar spreadsheet. Column B contains Steak, C contains Chicken, D contains Veggie, E is Mega. A contains questions. Row 5 selected, How many should I make? Steak is 50, Chicken is 50, Veggie is 50, Mega is 100. Demand: Steak 100, Chicken 100, Vegie 100, Mega 100. Ingredients portion. Beans for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Chicken for Steak is 0, for Chicken is 2, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Steak for Steak is 2, for Chicken is 0, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Veggies for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 2, for Mega is 1. Materials section. Row 18 highlighted Beans for cost is $.50, for used is 300, for Available is 300. Rice for cost is $.35, for used is 300, for available is 400. Row 20 is selected Chicken for cost is $.75, used is 300, available is 300. Row 21 selected Steak for cost is $.80, for used is 200, for available is 200. Veggies for cost is $.70, for used is 350, for available is 500. Profitability section. Price/Burrito for Steak is $7.95, for Chicken is $7.95, for Veggie is $6.95, for Mega is $8.95. Cost/Burrito for Steak is $3.15, for Chicken is $3.05, for Veggie is $2.25, for Mega is $3.10. Profit/Burrito for Steak is $4.80, for Chicken is $4.90, for Veggie is $4.70, for Mega is $5.85. Total Profit is $1,550.00

section.para: The optimal solution represents the mix of burritos that Benito should make subject to the demand and raw material constraints that he faces. There is not another mix of burritos that he could make that would produce more profit than this solution. As illustrated in , Benito should make 100 each of the mega and chicken burritos and 50 each of the steak and veggie burritos. At that point, he will have exhausted all of the steak, chicken, and beans he has available, so he will be unable to make any more burritos despite the fact that there would still be demand for more steak and veggie burritos.

This is a simple scenario; however, it would not be difficult to conceive of a more complex set of constraints. For example, Benito may have to worry about the vegetable ingredients spoiling. In this case, the optimal solution would have to include the constraint that all vegetables be consumed. This minor change in constraints makes the problem much more difficult to solve. The more difficult the problem becomes, the more likely we’ll need Solver to find an optimal solution.

In summary, optimization spreadsheet models include three common elements: (1) an objective that is used to determine when an optimal solution is reached, (2) a set of decision elements that can be changed to arrive at the optimal solution, and (3) a set of decision constraints that will influence the optimal solution. Developing optimization models is a necessary first step to using the Solver element of Excel.

section.label: G.2
section.title: Using Solver for Optimization

note.title: Unavailable Options
note.para: Solver is not currently available in the online version of Excel. To learn about this feature, switch the book to a desktop version.
To switch to a desktop version:

listitem.para: Click Advanced Options in the menu on the right-hand side of the screen.
Select the version of Excel that corresponds with the version you want to use and click Save.

note.para: You will now see instructions based on your selected version.

videodata.be:name: 2019 - Using the Solver Add-in for Excel
Mac - Using the Solver Add-in for Excel

section.para: At this point, we are ready to use Solver to complete an optimization analysis. We will use the example of Benito’s Burrito Bar from the previous section to demonstrate how an optimization analysis works.

In the previous section of this lesson, we discussed what an optimization model is and how to construct optimization models in Excel. We also walked through the process of performing a simple optimization analysis manually. In this section, we will discuss how to use the Solver feature in Excel to complete an optimization analysis.

section.title: Installing the Solver Add-In
section.para: Solver is available as an add-in for Excel 2016. This means that Solver comes with Excel but is not typically available until you install it. To install the Solver Add-in, click on the File menu tab and select Options. In the Options dialog window select Add-ins from the left column of the window and click on Go. Check the box next to Solver Add-in and click OK. The Solver Add-in should now be accessible in the Analysis group of menu items in the Data menu tab.

Solver is available as an add-in for Excel. This means that Solver comes with Excel but is not typically available until you install it. To install the Solver Add-in, click on the Tools in the Mac Menu Bar at the top of the window. Then select Excel Add-ins.... In the Add-Ins dialog window, place a checkmark in front of Solver Add-in and click OK. The Solver Add-in should now be accessible in the Analysis group of menu items in the Data menu tab.
