Excel menu bar. Data tab. Analysis section. Solver icon with a question mark and an arrow.

section.title: Performing Optimization Analysis with Solver
section.para: In the previous section, we constructed a spreadsheet model to find the optimal number of each type of burrito that Benito should make in order to maximize the profitability of his business. Remember that an optimization spreadsheet model contains three parts: (1) the objective, (2) decisions, and (3) constraints. presents the optimization model for Benito’s business.

imagedata.alt: Benito’s Burrito Bar spreadsheet. Column B contains Steak, C contains Chicken, D contains Veggie, E is Mega. A contains questions. How many should I make? Steak is 0, Chicken is 100, Veggie is 0, Mega is 100. Demand: Steak 100, Chicken 100, Vegie 100, Mega 100. Ingredients portion. Beans for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Rice for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 1, for Mega is 1. Chicken for Steak is 0, for Chicken is 2, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Steak for Steak is 2, for Chicken is 0, for Veggie is 0, for Mega is 1. Veggies for Steak is 1, for Chicken is 1, for Veggie is 2, for Mega is 1. Materials section. Beans for cost is $.50, for used is 200, for Available is 300. Rice for cost is $.35, for used is 200, for available is 400. Chicken for cost is $.75, used is 300, available is 300. Steak for cost is $.80, for used is 100, for available is 200. Veggies for cost is $.70, for used is 200, for available is 500. Profitability section. Price/Burrito for Steak is $7.95, for Chicken is $7.95, for Veggie is $6.95, for Mega is $8.95. Cost/Burrito for Steak is $3.15, for Chicken is $3.05, for Veggie is $2.25, for Mega is $3.10. Profit/Burrito for Steak is $4.80, for Chicken is $4.90, for Veggie is $4.70, for Mega is $5.85. Total Profit is $1,075.00.

section.para: The objective of this model is to maximize profit. Profit is calculated in cell B29 and is a function of the number of each type of burrito made and the amount of profit made for each type of burrito. The profit from each type of burrito is the price per burrito minus the cost per burrito. The cost per burrito is calculated by multiplying the cost of the ingredients (cells B18:B22) by the amount of each ingredient used in making each burrito (cells B10:E14). The decisions for this model are entered in cells B5:E5. Benito must decide how many of each type of burrito to make. The constraints for the model include the following:

listitem.para: Benito will not make more of any type of burrito than he thinks he can sell (notice that demand is modeled in cells B6:E6).

Benito cannot consume more materials in making the burritos than he has available to him (notice how the materials used are calculated by multiplying the number of each type of burrito made in cells B5:E5 by the amount of each type of ingredient used in making the burritos in cells B10:E14).
Benito wishes to make at least 50 of each type of burrito.
Benito cannot sell a partial burrito (he must make whole-number amounts of each type of burrito).

section.para: Notice how the spreadsheet model is designed to account for the objective, decisions, and constraints of the problem. Solver can be used to determine how many of each type of burrito to make in order to meet the objective (maximize profit) while satisfying all the model constraints.

To use Solver, click on the Solver icon in the Analysis group of menu items in the Data menu tab. The Solver Parameters dialog window will appear. The three elements of optimization models (objective, decisions, and constraints) are represented in the Solver Parameters dialog window. Note that the decisions for the model are represented in the By Changing Variable Cells section of the window.

imagedata.alt: Solver dialog window. Set Objective section set to $A$1. By Changing Variable Cells: section. Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

Solver dialog window. Set Objective section set to $A$1. By Changing Variable Cells: section. Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

section.para: First, we define the Set Objective (the cell address where the objective is defined in the spreadsheet model) and the To setting (information about how that cell should be treated by Solver). The Max option will tell Solver to find the largest value possible for the objective cell given the constraints of the model. The Min option will alert Solver to find the smallest value for the objective cell given the model constraints. Finally, the Value Of option will force Solver to find a solution in which the objective cell is a specific value (defined in the box to the right of the Value Of option). In the case of our analysis, Benito’s profit is calculated in cell B29 of the spreadsheet model and should be set to Max.

figure.label: Figure G.4
figure.title: Solver Parameters Dialog Window: Defining the Objective.

imagedata.alt: Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5 (this area highlighted). Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5 (this area highlighted). Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

section.para: The decisions that must be made to achieve the objective are placed in the By Changing Variable Cells section of the Solver Parameters dialog window. In Benito’s case, he must decide how many of each type of burrito to make. Therefore, the By Changing Variable Cells range will be cells B5:E5 (the location in the spreadsheet model where we input the number of each type of burrito to make).

figure.label: Figure G.5
figure.title: Solver Parameters Dialog Window: Defining the Decision.

imagedata.alt: Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5 (this area highlighted). Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5 (this area highlighted). Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

section.para: It’s now time to model the constraints. The first constraint is that Benito does not want to make more of any type of Burrito than he can sell. This means that the values in cells B5:E5 of the spreadsheet model must be less than or equal to the corresponding value in cells B6:E6 of the spreadsheet.

To create this constraint in Solver, we click on Add in the Solver Parameters dialog window. This invokes the Add Constraint dialog window. There are two parts to the Add Constraint window. The first part, Cell Reference, refers to the location of the cells in the spreadsheet model that must be constrained by Solver. The second part, the Constraint section, is used to define how the cell reference should be constrained. Notice that you can select an operator (such as less than or equal to) when you create a constraint. The value in the Constraint section can be a specific value (such as a number) or a reference to another set of cells in the spreadsheet that contain the values for the constraint.

informalfigure.title: Click Add to add a constraint.

imagedata.alt: Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5. Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Add button suggested. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5. Subject to the Constraints: and blank field. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Add button suggested. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.
Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and a less than and equal to sign. Constraint field contains $B$6:$E$6. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.
Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and a less than and equal to sign. Constraint field contains $B$6:$E$6. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: In this example, we want the values in cells B5:E5 of the model to be constrained to less than or equal to the values in cells B6:E6 of the model. When constraints are added as ranges, the cells in the ranges are paired together to apply the constraint. So when we add the constraint that B5:E5 must be less than or equal to B6:E6, Solver interprets this constraint as B5 must be less than or equal to B6, C5 must be less than or equal to C6, D5 must be less than or equal to D6, and E5 must be less than or equal to E6.

The second constraint for this model is that Benito cannot use more ingredients to make his burritos than are available to him. This means that the ingredients consumed in making all burritos (cells B18:C22) must be less than or equal to the ingredients available (cells D18:D22).

imagedata.alt: Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains C$18:$C$22 and a less than sign and equal to sign. Constraint field contains $D$18:$D$22. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.
Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains C$18:$C$22 and a less than sign and equal to sign. Constraint field contains $D$18:$D$22. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: The third constraint is that Benito wishes to make at least 50 of each type of burrito. This constraint is created by setting cells B5:E5 so that they are greater than or equal to 50.

imagedata.alt: Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and a greater than sign and an equal sign. Constraint field contains 50. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.
Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and a greater than sign and an equal sign. Constraint field contains 50. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: The final constraint is that Benito must make whole-number amounts of each type of burrito. We call this an integer constraint. To create an integer constraint, you enter the cell reference for the cells that are to be constrained as integers and choose “int” from the drop-down list. Notice that once “int” is selected, the word “integer” appears in the constraint box. In this example, cells B5:E5 are to be set as integers.

imagedata.alt: Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and an int in the middle field. Constraint field contains the word integer. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.
Add Constraint dialog window. Cell Reference contains $B$5:$E$5 and an int in the middle field. Constraint field contains the word integer. Buttons for Add, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: Once the objective, decisions, and constraints are added to the Solver Parameters dialog window (see ), we need to tell Solver which method to use to complete the analysis. Although there are three methods to choose from, most simple Solver analyses can be completed using the Simplex LP method.

Finally, we need to tell Solver if it is allowable for “unconstrained variables” to be negative. Essentially, we are deciding if the By Changing Variable Cells defined in the Solver Parameters can be less than zero. In this case, it doesn’t make sense for Benito to make a negative number of burritos, so we will leave the checkmark in the box next to Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative.

figure.label: Figure G.10
figure.title: Solver Parameters Dialog Window with All Parameters Set.

imagedata.alt: Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5. Subject to the Constraints: and several constraints. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Add button suggested. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

Solver Parameters window. Set Objective section set to $B$29. Options for To with Max, Min, and Value Of. Max selected. By Changing Variable Cells: field with $B$5:$E$5. Subject to the Constraints: and several constraints. Buttons for Add, Change, Delete, Reset All, Load/Save. Add button suggested. Checkbox for Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative. Buttons for Close and Solve.

section.para: For those who need to conduct more complicated analyses or who need more control over the Solver algorithm, there are a number of settings available. To access these settings, click the Options button in the Solver Parameters dialog window. Once you are happy with the settings for the model, you can perform the analysis by clicking the Solve button. After the analysis is complete, the Solver Results dialog window is displayed.

imagedata.alt: Solver Results dialog box. Solver found a solution. All constraints and optimality conditions are satisfied. Section and options for Keep Solver Solution (selected) and Restore Original Values. Section Reports and Answer. Buttons for Save Scenario, Cancel, and OK.

Solver Results dialog box. Solver found a solution. All constraints and optimality conditions are satisfied. Section and options for Keep Solver Solution (selected) and Restore Original Values. Section Reports and Answer. Buttons for Save Scenario, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: The Solver Results dialog window reports whether Solver found a solution. displays the results from running Benito’s model. As you can see, an optimal solution was found.

There are some situations in which Solver is unable to find an optimal solution because all the problem constraints cannot be satisfied at the same time. For example, if Benito had to make 1,000 steak burritos and only had 100 servings of steak, it would be impossible for Benito to satisfy both constraints at the same time. When Solver is unable to find an optimal solution, the Solver Results dialog window displays the message shown in .

figure.label: Figure G.12
figure.title: Solver Results Dialog Window for No Feasible Solution.

imagedata.alt: Solver Results dialog box. Solver could not find a feasible solution. Section and options for Keep Solver Solution (selected), Restore Original Values, and Solve Without Integer Constraints. Section Reports. Buttons for Save Scenario, Cancel, and OK.

Solver Results dialog box. Solver could not find a feasible solution. Section and options for Keep Solver Solution (selected), Restore Original Values, and Solve Without Integer Constraints. Section Reports. Buttons for Save Scenario, Cancel, and OK.

section.para: The Solver Results window provides several options. You can decide to update the values in the spreadsheet to those calculated by Solver or have these values revert back as they were before Solver was used. Additionally, you can save the values calculated by Solver as a scenario (see Appendix for information on scenario manager). You can return to the Solver Parameters dialog window to modify your analysis. Finally, you can click OK to return to view the spreadsheet model.

note.para: Use the Windows or Mac desktop version of Excel to complete this practice assignment.

section.title: Introduction
section.para: This appendix provides several shortcuts for menu commands and mouse commands. It also outlines quick ways to move around the worksheet or workbook and gives tips for completing some time-consuming tasks quickly.
Shortcut keys that must be pressed together will be shown as follows:
Ctrl+Q
command ⌘+Q
Holding down the Ctrl key while pressing the Q key on the keyboard will execute the command associated with this shortcut.
Holding down the command ⌘ key while pressing the Q key on the keyboard will execute the command associated with this shortcut.

section.label: Undo and Redo
section.para: The commands in this table will allow you to quickly undo or redo a previous entry.

section.label: Find and Replace
section.para: The commands in this table will allow you to quickly find and replace data in a workbook.

tr.td: Display the find and replace dialog box.
Display the find and replace dialog box with the replace option selected.

section.label: Absolute Cell References
section.para: The F4 function key can be used to cycle through absolute references. The table below demonstrates pressing F4 on a reference to cell A1:

tr.td: Both the column and row references are absolute.
The column reference is relative and the row reference is absolute.
The column reference is absolute and the row reference is relative.

section.label: Entering Data in Functions
section.para: Functions like SUM, PMT, and AVERAGEIF, for example, allow for a varying number of argument inputs.
Hold down Ctrl while selecting the arguments with your mouse. This will add commas between each argument for you.
Hold down command ⌘ while selecting the arguments with your mouse. This will add commas between each argument for you.

section.label: Fill Series
section.para: The mouse shortcuts in this section can be used to quickly fill a range with a series of data.

section.title: Drag and Drop
section.para: To fill a series of data with a pattern:

listitem.para: Highlight the cells with the pattern.
Place the mouse pointer on the bottom right corner of the selection.
Hold down the left mouse button.
Drag down or right to fill the series.

imagedata.alt: Cells A1 and A2 selected, and lower right corner with a plus icon.

section.para: The pattern will fill in all the cells when you release the mouse button.

section.title: Column Fill
section.para: To fill a series of data with data in a column directly left or right of the pattern:

listitem.para: Highlight the cells with the pattern.
Double-click the bottom right corner of the selection.

imagedata.alt: A1:A5 contain numbers 1 through 5 and Column B B1:B5 contains numbers 10 through 50.

section.para: The pattern will fill in all the cells below that have data to the left or right.
To fill a series using the Auto Fill Option:

listitem.para: Enter a starting value.
Drag bottom right corner of the cell down or right to fill cells with starting value.
Click the Auto Fill Options icon that is displayed to the right of the last cell.
Select Fill Series.

imagedata.alt: A1:A5 contain the number 1. Cells selected, dropdown menu open, Fill Series selected.
A1:A5 contain the number 1. Cells selected, dropdown menu open, Fill Series selected.

section.para: Cells will fill with a series that increments one value at a time.

section.title: Repeat Unique Pattern
section.para: To fill a series with a unique pattern:

listitem.para: Enter the pattern.
Highlight the cells with the pattern.
Hold down the Ctrl key.
Hold down the option ⌥ key.
Then drag down or to the right.

imagedata.alt: A1:A2 contain numbers 1 and 2. A2:A6 selected. Next image has A1:A6 and contains alternating numbers 1 and 2.

section.label: Proofing Text
section.para: Text is important to the reader of a worksheet. The commands in this table will allow you to quickly open the Spelling and Thesaurus dialog boxes.

section.label: Sum Columns
section.para: To quickly Sum a column of data:

listitem.para: Select the empty cell below a column or to the right of a row of numbers.
Press the keysAlt+=.
Press Enter to accept the function.
Select the data including blank cells at the bottom and to the right before pressing Alt+=.

imagedata.alt: Columns A through D selected and through row 5. Row 5 is blank and column D is blank. Another spreadsheet with blank row and column filled.

section.para: This feature also works for multiple columns and rows.

section.para: The commands in this table will allow you to quickly format selected data.

note.title: Mac Version
note.para: The Mac version of Excel does not currently have the keyboard menu shortcuts available. These shortcuts are available only on the Windows and online versions of Excel.

section.label: Excel Menu
section.para: All the menu commands have an Alt + letter or number combination that will allow you to exclusively use the keyboard to select all the menu options. Pressing Alt will display the letters for each of the menu tabs. Press the letter that corresponds to the menu tab that you want and the letters will change to the letters or numbers of all the commands in that menu.
For example, to bold the selected cells, press Alt. The shortcut menu options will appear.

imagedata.alt: Excel Menu Home tab. Numbers and key squares near each tab.
Excel Menu Home tab. Numbers and key squares near each tab.

section.para: Press the H key. The Home ribbon menu shortcuts will appear.

imagedata.alt: Excel Menu Home tab. Numbers and key squares near each option.
Excel Menu Home tab. Numbers and key squares near each option.

section.para: Press 1. The current selection will be bolded and the Alt menu options will be removed from the display.

Menu items that display a drop-down arrow will highlight the displayed option and allow the user to press the Up or Down arrow keys to move to other options on the drop-down menu. Press Enter to accept the selected item on the drop-down menu.
